name: Dunmore East Castle
description: A striking Martello-style fortification perched above the fishing village of Dunmore East, watching over Waterford Harbour with salt-sprayed battlements and sweeping Atlantic views.
history: Built in the early 19th century as part of coastal defences during the Napoleonic Wars, Dunmore East Castle (also called Duncannon Tower) guards the entrance to Waterford Harbour. The structure served as a signal station and defensive post, later falling into disuse as military threats receded.

name: Askeaton Castle
description: Dramatic limestone ruins of a Desmond stronghold rising above the River Deel, its Great Hall one of the finest medieval banqueting chambers in Ireland.
history: Built by the Earls of Desmond in the 13th century, Askeaton became the chief seat of the Fitzgerald dynasty. The exquisite Banqueting Hall dates to the 15th century, featuring intricate carved windows. The castle was besieged multiple times during the Desmond Rebellions and finally fell to Cromwellian forces in 1652. The Franciscan friary adjacent was founded in 1389.

name: Dunloe Castle
description: Ancient Anglo-Norman fortress guarding the fabled Gap of Dunloe, its weathered towers commanding views across Kerry's most dramatic mountain pass.
history: First recorded in 1215 when rebuilt by the son of Thomas Fitzgerald, this Anglo-Norman castle controlled the strategic river crossing to the Gap of Dunloe. It changed hands repeatedly between Gaelic and Norman lords, was partly demolished during the Cromwellian campaigns, and fell into dereliction. Rescued from collapse in 2013, it now stands within the grounds of the Dunloe Hotel.

name: Ferns Castle
description: A brooding half-ruined Norman fortress in the quiet Wexford countryside, its surviving circular tower still imposing after eight centuries of storms and sieges.
history: Built around 1220 by William Earl Marshal on the site of an earlier Gaelic royal seat, Ferns Castle features a fine circular keep and rectangular towers. Ferns was once the capital of the Kingdom of Leinster under Diarmait Mac Murchada, whose invitation to the Normans changed Irish history forever. The castle was damaged in the 1641 Rebellion and abandoned by the 17th century.

name: Granny Castle
description: A haunting Butler stronghold overlooking the River Suir, its ivy-clad walls whispering tales of medieval power struggles in the golden vale of Kilkenny.
history: Built in the 14th century by the Butler family, Granny Castle (Grannagh) stands on a rocky outcrop above the River Suir near Waterford. The castle features a large rectangular tower house within a bawn wall. It served as a key defensive position controlling river traffic and was occupied until the 17th century Cromwellian period.

name: Clara Castle
description: One of Ireland's best-preserved tower houses, this imposing 15th-century fortress stands proud in the Kilkenny countryside with its original roof and battlements remarkably intact.
history: Built around 1450 by the Shortall family, Clara Castle is a fine example of a fortified tower house with its five storeys, murder holes, and original vaulted ceilings still intact. It passed through several families over the centuries. Recently restored, it offers a rare glimpse into authentic medieval Irish domestic life with original features preserved.

name: Cavan Castle
description: Scant but evocative remains of the O'Reilly clan's seat of power, a once-mighty fortress that anchored Gaelic authority in the drumlin heartlands of Ulster.
history: Originally built by the O'Reilly clan in the medieval period, Cavan Castle served as the chief stronghold of the Lords of East Bréifne. The castle was rebuilt and fortified multiple times, playing a role in the Nine Years' War. It was largely destroyed in the Cromwellian conquest and little remains above ground today, the site now absorbed into the town of Cavan.

name: Roche Castle
description: A spectacular Anglo-Norman fortress clinging to a volcanic crag in County Louth, its dramatic silhouette one of the most photogenic castle ruins in Ireland.
history: Built around 1236 by Lady Rohesia de Verdun, Roche Castle is a superb example of an Anglo-Norman fortress. Legend holds that Rohesia invited the architect to dinner after completion—then had him thrown from the window so he could never build its equal. The castle controlled a strategic position on the Louth-Armagh border and was abandoned by the 17th century.

name: Dunmore Castle
description: Crumbling remains of a Butler fortress in the rolling Kilkenny countryside, where medieval walls emerge from tangled woodland like a forgotten dream.
history: Dunmore Castle in County Kilkenny was built by the Butler family in the medieval period. The town of Dunmore received its charter in the 14th century, and the castle served as a local stronghold. Though much reduced, the remaining walls and earthworks hint at its former importance as a centre of Butler power in the region.

name: Enniscorthy Castle
description: A magnificently restored Norman castle dominating the town of Enniscorthy, now housing a superb museum chronicling the turbulent history of County Wexford.
history: Originally built by Raymond le Gros in the 1190s, Enniscorthy Castle was granted to the Prendergast family before passing to the MacMurrough Kavanaghs. Rebuilt in the 16th century by Sir Henry Wallop, it played roles in the 1641 and 1798 rebellions. Extensively restored in 2011, it now houses the Enniscorthy Castle Museum exploring 1798, the Great Famine, and Easter 1916.

name: Ballyhack Castle
description: A powerful Knights Hospitaller tower house commanding the Waterford Estuary, its brooding bulk the origin of the Irish curse 'Go to Ballyhack.'
history: Built c.1450 by the Knights Hospitallers of St John, Ballyhack Castle overlooks the confluence of the rivers Barrow, Nore and Suir. The massive tower house was occupied by Cromwellian forces during the 1641 Rebellion and used as a deportation point under the 1652 Act of Settlement. Restored and now open to visitors with displays on the Crusades and Normans.

name: Rathmacknee Castle
description: A remarkably intact 15th-century tower house standing sentinel over the flat Wexford farmland, its five storeys and bawn wall beautifully preserved.
history: Built around 1450 by the Rossiter family, Rathmacknee is one of the best-preserved tower houses in County Wexford. The five-storey tower retains its battlements and sits within a large bawn with corner towers. It served as a key Rossiter stronghold until the Cromwellian period.

name: Burnchurch Castle
description: A tall, gaunt tower house rising from the Kilkenny pastures like a stone finger, its unusual height and intact battlements making it a striking medieval survivor.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Fitzgerald family, Burnchurch Castle is an impressively tall tower house standing six storeys high. The castle features fine battlements, a vaulted ground floor, and murder holes. It remained in the Fitzgerald family until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Castledermot Round Tower
description: An ancient monastic round tower and exquisite high crosses standing in the quiet Kildare countryside, a Celtic treasure trove of early Christian art.
history: Founded as a monastery by St Diarmuid in the 9th century, Castledermot preserves a fine round tower, two remarkable Romanesque high crosses, and a Hogback tomb. The high crosses display scenes from the Bible including Daniel in the Lions' Den and the Crucifixion. The site was an important ecclesiastical centre and survived Viking raids.

name: Dunsink Castle
description: A modest medieval tower house in north Dublin, quietly decaying amid suburban encroachment, a forgotten relic of the Pale's defensive network.
history: Dunsink Castle is a small medieval tower house dating to the 15th century, located near the famous Dunsink Observatory in north Dublin. It formed part of the network of tower houses that protected the English Pale. Now in a ruinous state, it stands as a reminder of Dublin's medieval hinterland.

name: Howth Castle
description: An ancient seat of the St Lawrence family perched on the Howth peninsula, where 800 years of continuous habitation have created a fascinating architectural palimpsest.
history: Home to the St Lawrence family since 1177, Howth Castle is one of the oldest continuously inhabited residences in Ireland. The original Norman fortress was remodelled over centuries, with notable work by Sir Edwin Lutyens in the 1910s. Legend says Grace O'Malley, finding the gates locked at dinner, kidnapped the heir until the family promised never to refuse hospitality.

name: Bullock Castle
description: A squat Norman fortress overlooking the harbour at Dalkey, its weathered walls recalling centuries of monastic rule and fishing village life on Dublin Bay.
history: Built in the 12th century by the Cistercian monks of St Mary's Abbey, Dublin, Bullock Castle controlled the small harbour and fisheries. The castle served as a customs post and defensive position. It passed through various hands after the dissolution of the monasteries and stands today as a compact ruin in the seaside suburb of Dalkey.

name: Athcarne Castle
description: A crumbling Elizabethan fortified house in the Meath countryside, its ornate carved doorway hinting at the ambitions of a Pale gentry family.
history: Built around 1590 by the Dowdall family, Athcarne Castle is a fortified house reflecting the transitional period between medieval defence and Renaissance comfort. The building features fine stone carvings and mullioned windows. It was damaged in the Confederate Wars and abandoned by the 18th century.

name: Ardee Castle
description: The largest fortified medieval tower house in Ireland, this imposing Louth fortress now serves as a courthouse—justice dispensed from Norman walls.
history: Built in the 13th century and substantially enlarged in the 15th century, Ardee Castle is reputedly the largest fortified tower house in Ireland. The castle anchored the defence of the Pale's northern frontier. It was granted to the Pipard family and later passed to the Plunkett and Brabazon families. It has served as Ardee's courthouse since the 18th century.

name: King John's Castle (Carlingford)
description: A dramatic D-shaped Norman fortress perched on a rocky outcrop above Carlingford Lough, its massive walls framing sublime views of the Mourne Mountains.
history: Built around 1190 by Hugh de Lacy, King John's Castle at Carlingford is one of the earliest Norman fortresses in Ireland. King John himself visited in 1210 during his campaign to assert authority over the Irish lords. The castle features an unusual D-shaped plan and massive curtain walls, guarding the entrance to one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval villages.

name: Taafe's Castle
description: A striking fortified tower house in the heart of medieval Carlingford, its imposing bulk a testament to the wealth of the old merchant families.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Taafe family, this fortified merchant's house stands in the centre of Carlingford village. The Taafes were a prominent Anglo-Norman family who dominated local commerce. The castle features a fine vaulted ground floor and is one of several medieval buildings that make Carlingford a uniquely atmospheric heritage town.

name: Termonfeckin Castle
description: A compact 15th-century tower house standing guard over the quiet Louth village where early Irish Christianity once flowered.
history: Built in the 15th century, Termonfeckin Castle is a well-preserved three-storey tower house near the coast of County Louth. The village name derives from the Irish for 'sanctuary of St Feichin,' referencing the 7th-century monastery. The castle served as the residence of the Primate of Armagh.

name: Ballymoon Castle
description: A vast, roofless Norman enclosure castle in the Carlow countryside—its enormous curtain walls enclosing nothing but sky, wildflowers, and eight centuries of silence.
history: Built around 1290, possibly by the Bigod or Carew families, Ballymoon Castle is one of the largest and most enigmatic Norman castles in Ireland. The massive rectangular enclosure with projecting towers has no keep—an unusual plan suggesting it was never completed. It was briefly held by the Kavanaghs and ruinous by the 16th century.

name: Easkey Castle
description: Windswept ruins of an O'Dowd stronghold on the wild Sligo coast, where Atlantic gales have sculpted both stone and legend for centuries.
history: Originally an O'Dowd castle dating from the 15th century, Easkey Castle stands near the famous surfing beach of Easkey in County Sligo. The O'Dowds were chiefs of Tireragh. The castle was a centre of Gaelic power before falling to English control. Today the atmospheric ruin overlooks some of Ireland's best surf breaks.

name: Burt Castle
description: A striking O'Doherty fortress commanding views over Lough Swilly and the Inishowen Peninsula from its dramatic Donegal ridge.
history: Built in the early 16th century by the O'Doherty clan, Burt Castle guards a strategic position overlooking Lough Swilly. It served as the chief O'Doherty residence before being supplanted by nearby Grianan of Aileach. Burned during the O'Doherty rebellion of 1608 and subsequently fell into ruin.

name: Raphoe Castle
description: The fortified palace of the Bishops of Raphoe—a massive, moody ruin in the Donegal midlands where ecclesiastical ambition met military necessity.
history: Built in 1636 by Bishop John Leslie, Raphoe Castle served as the fortified palace of the Church of Ireland bishops of Raphoe. The impressive structure features corner towers and substantial walls. It was damaged during the 1641 Rebellion and Williamite Wars, and abandoned as a residence in the late 18th century.

name: Clogh Oughter Castle
description: A hauntingly beautiful circular tower rising from a tiny island in Lough Oughter, its reflection shimmering in the still waters of Cavan's lake country.
history: Built in the 13th century by the Anglo-Normans on a crannog in Lough Oughter, this unusual circular castle was soon seized by the O'Reilly clan. It held Bishop Bedell during the 1641 Rebellion. The last castle in Ireland to fall to Cromwell's forces in 1653, it was deliberately destroyed by explosion but its walls still stand in the lake.

name: Ross Errilly Friary
description: The largest and best-preserved medieval Franciscan friary in Ireland, a sprawling monastic complex where cloisters, kitchens, and fishpond survive in extraordinary detail.
history: Founded around 1351 and substantially rebuilt in the 15th century, Ross Errilly near Headford is the largest Franciscan friary in Ireland. Its remarkably complete remains include a double nave church, a beautiful cloister, a kitchen with fish tank, bakery, and dormitories. The friars were repeatedly expelled but always returned; the last friar died here in 1753.

name: Ballinasloe Castle
description: Faded remnants of a medieval stronghold in the great fair town of Ballinasloe, where the River Suck once carried commerce and conflict in equal measure.
history: Ballinasloe Castle dates to the Norman period and was held by various families including the O'Kellys. The town became famous for its great October horse fair, one of the oldest and largest in Europe. Little remains of the castle today, but the strategic crossing of the River Suck ensured its military significance throughout the medieval period.

name: Tyrone House
description: A magnificent Georgian ruin overlooking Galway Bay—its roofless grandeur a haunting monument to the rise and catastrophic fall of the St George family.
history: Built in 1779 by Christopher St George, Tyrone House was a grand Georgian mansion overlooking Galway Bay. The family fell into debt and the house was used as a workhouse during the Great Famine. Burned by anti-Treaty forces in 1920, its spectacular shell remains one of Ireland's most dramatically ruined country houses.

name: Killahara Castle
description: A formidable Butler tower house standing proudly in the Tipperary farmland, one of the finest examples of a medieval Irish fortified residence.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Butler family, Killahara Castle is a well-preserved tower house near Dundrum in County Tipperary. The castle features four storeys with vaulted ceilings and a fine bawn wall. It has been carefully restored by its current owners and offers a remarkable glimpse into medieval Irish castle life.

name: Loughmoe Castle
description: An extraordinary hybrid of medieval tower and Elizabethan mansion in the Tipperary countryside, where two ages of architecture collide in one dramatic ruin.
history: Loughmoe Castle combines a 13th-century tower house with a grand 17th-century fortified house added by the Purcell family. The Purcells, Barons of Loughmoe, were one of Tipperary's most powerful Old English families. The building fell into disuse after the Cromwellian confiscations and now stands as a striking ruin showing the evolution of Irish castle architecture.

name: Glenstal Castle
description: A romantic Norman Revival castle set in ancient woodland, now home to a Benedictine monastery whose monks chant amid Victorian fantasy architecture.
history: Built between 1837 and 1839 by the Barrington family in an extravagant Norman Revival style, Glenstal Castle was designed by William Bardwell. The estate was purchased by the Benedictine order in 1927 and now houses Glenstal Abbey, a monastery and prestigious boys' school. The castle features fine medieval-inspired stonework and sits in 500 acres of parkland.

name: Kilcoe Castle
description: A dramatically restored tower house on a tidal island in Roaringwater Bay, famously owned and brought back to life by actor Jeremy Irons.
history: Built in the 15th century by the McCarthy clan, Kilcoe Castle sits on a rocky tidal island in Roaringwater Bay, West Cork. It passed through several families before falling into ruin. In 1998, actor Jeremy Irons purchased and meticulously restored the castle, controversially painting it white initially before the traditional grey stone was restored. The castle is not open to the public.

name: Dunboy Castle
description: Tragic ruins of the O'Sullivan Bere stronghold on the Beara Peninsula, site of one of the last stands against Elizabethan conquest.
history: Dunboy Castle near Castletownbere was the seat of the O'Sullivan Bere clan. In 1602, during the Nine Years' War, it was besieged and destroyed by English forces under Sir George Carew after a ferocious defence. Donal Cam O'Sullivan Bere then led his legendary march to Leitrim. A later Puxley mansion was built nearby and later destroyed during the War of Independence.

name: Mallow Castle
description: Ivy-draped ruins of a grand Elizabethan fortified house in the heart of Mallow, surrounded by a deer park where herds still graze beneath ancient oaks.
history: Built around 1585 by Sir Thomas Norreys, Lord President of Munster, Mallow Castle was one of the finest Elizabethan fortified houses in Ireland. The three-storey mansion replaced an earlier Desmond stronghold. It was badly damaged during the Cromwellian wars. A Georgian house was built alongside in the 18th century, and the grounds remain a public park with a resident deer herd.

name: Macroom Castle
description: Gateway to West Cork's wild interior, this medieval castle's surviving archway frames the bustling market town that grew in its protective shadow.
history: Originally built by the McCarthy clan in the medieval period, Macroom Castle was later held by the Muskerry branch of the McCarthys. It was granted to Admiral Sir William Penn (father of William Penn of Pennsylvania fame) during the Cromwellian settlement. The castle was largely destroyed by fire in 1922 during the Civil War, but the impressive gateway and some walls remain.

name: Minard Castle
description: A spectacularly ruined fortress on a wild Dingle Peninsula beach, its shattered walls testimony to Cromwell's devastating bombardment.
history: Built by the Knight of Kerry (FitzGerald family) in the 16th century, Minard Castle stands on a storm beach on the Dingle Peninsula. During the Cromwellian wars in 1650, the castle was besieged and its garrison massacred. The castle was deliberately demolished afterward and now stands as a dramatic ruin surrounded by massive storm-tossed boulders on the Kerry coast.

name: Listowel Castle
description: A proud Norman fortress standing watch over the literary capital of Kerry, its twin towers a gateway to centuries of Munster history and rebellion.
history: Built by the Anglo-Norman Fitzmaurice family (Lords of Kerry) in the 15th century, Listowel Castle was the last castle in Munster to hold out against the Elizabethan conquest, finally surrendering in 1600 after a lengthy siege. The garrison was executed. The impressive twin towers and connecting wall have been restored and the castle is now open to the public.

name: Carrigafoyle Castle
description: A towering fortress on the Shannon Estuary, its five-storey tower offering staggering views and a visceral sense of the power struggles that ravaged Munster.
history: Built in the late 15th century by the O'Connor Kerry clan, Carrigafoyle Castle was one of the principal fortresses of the Shannon Estuary. In 1580 it was besieged by Sir William Pelham's Elizabethan forces with cannon fire—the first recorded use of artillery against an Irish castle. The garrison of 50 was massacred. The castle's impressive height and strategic position remain striking.

name: Dromore Castle (Limerick)
description: A flamboyant Gothic Revival fantasy rising from the Limerick countryside, its turrets and towers designed by the great Edward William Godwin.
history: Built between 1867 and 1873 for the 3rd Earl of Limerick by architect E.W. Godwin (famous for his Aesthetic Movement designs), Dromore Castle is a spectacular Gothic Revival confection. The castle was abandoned in the 1950s when the family could no longer afford its upkeep. It stands as one of Ireland's most dramatic Victorian castle ruins.

name: Kilbolane Castle
description: A massive medieval motte-and-castle ruin crowning a hill in north Cork, its sprawling earthworks testimony to centuries of frontier warfare.
history: Kilbolane Castle in County Cork dates from the 13th century and was built by the de Barry family. The large motte and ruined tower house occupy a commanding hilltop position. It changed hands between Norman and Gaelic lords numerous times and was involved in the Desmond Rebellions. The earthworks and tower remains offer wide views across the Blackwater valley.

name: Lough Key Castle
description: The romantic ruin of a MacDermot stronghold on an island in Lough Key, visible from one of Ireland's most beautiful forest parks.
history: Castle Island in Lough Key was the ancient seat of the MacDermot clan, Kings of Moylurg in County Roscommon. The island fortress, known as The Rock, was their stronghold for centuries. The castle and island are now part of Lough Key Forest Park, one of Ireland's premier recreational areas, and can be viewed from the park's treetop walkway.

name: Rindoon Castle
description: A vast, forgotten Anglo-Norman walled town on the shores of Lough Ree, its extensive ruins hidden on a remote peninsula accessible only on foot.
history: Founded in the 1220s during the Anglo-Norman push into Connacht, Rindoon was a fortified town with castle, church, hospital, windmill, and town walls on a peninsula in Lough Ree. It was repeatedly attacked by the Gaelic Irish and abandoned by the 14th century. The extensive ruins spread across the peninsula make it one of Ireland's most atmospheric and least-visited medieval sites.

name: Ballyportry Castle
description: A beautifully restored tower house in the shadow of the Burren, offering luxury accommodation within authentic 15th-century walls.
history: Built in the 15th century, Ballyportry Castle is a fine tower house in the Burren region of County Clare. It has been carefully restored and operates as exclusive holiday accommodation, allowing guests to experience medieval castle life amid the extraordinary limestone landscape of the Burren. The castle retains many original features including spiral staircases and vaulted ceilings.

name: Newtown Castle
description: A uniquely designed round tower house perched on a square base in the Burren, now a beautifully restored cultural centre and gallery.
history: Built in the 16th century, Newtown Castle near Ballyvaughan is architecturally unique in Ireland—a round tower house on a square pyramidal base. Restored in the 1990s by the Burren College of Art, it now serves as a gallery and cultural space. The unusual design reflects both defensive needs and the status of its O'Brien builders.

name: Doonagore Castle
description: Ireland's most photographed tower house, its perfect silhouette crowning the cliffs above Doolin with the Aran Islands shimmering on the Atlantic horizon.
history: Built in the 16th century, Doonagore Castle is a round tower house perched dramatically on the cliffs above Doolin in County Clare. In 1588, survivors from the Spanish Armada shipwrecks were held here before being executed. Privately restored in the 20th century, the castle is not open to visitors but its iconic profile against the Atlantic sky makes it one of Ireland's most photographed buildings.

name: Ballinalacken Castle
description: A crumbling O'Brien tower house perched on a Burren hilltop, with sweeping views from the Cliffs of Moher to the Aran Islands and Galway Bay.
history: Built by the O'Brien clan in the 15th century, Ballinalacken Castle commands one of the finest panoramic positions in County Clare. The four-storey tower house stands on the edge of the Burren's limestone karst landscape. A Victorian country house was built beside it and now operates as a hotel. The castle ruins are freely accessible in the hotel grounds.

name: Danganbrack Castle
description: A lonely tower house ruin in the Clare countryside, its crumbling walls slowly returning to the limestone landscape from which they were quarried.
history: Danganbrack Castle is a 15th-century tower house in the Burren region of County Clare. Built by a local clan, possibly the O'Briens or McNamaras, the modest tower house is now in a ruinous state but representative of the hundreds of such structures that once dotted the Irish landscape. It stands in farmland with limited access.

name: Gleninagh Castle
description: A striking O'Loughlin tower house clinging to the wild Burren coast, its stark walls rising above Black Head where limestone meets the Atlantic.
history: Built in the early 16th century by the O'Loughlin clan, Gleninagh Castle occupies a dramatic position on the coast road beneath Black Head in the Burren, County Clare. The four-storey tower house controlled the coastal route and trade. It was forfeited during the Cromwellian period. The castle's dramatic setting, with the grey Burren hills behind and Galway Bay before it, makes it one of Clare's most evocative ruins.

name: Mooghaun Fort
description: One of the largest prehistoric hillforts in Europe, its massive stone ramparts enclosing a vast area atop a hill overlooking the Shannon Estuary.
history: Dating to the Late Bronze Age (around 900 BC), Mooghaun Fort near Newmarket-on-Fergus is one of the largest hillforts in Europe, with three concentric stone walls enclosing over 12 hectares. In 1854, the Great Clare Gold Hoard—the largest collection of Bronze Age gold ever found in Western Europe—was discovered nearby. The fort was likely a major ceremonial and defensive centre.

name: Clifden Castle
description: Gothic ruins of John D'Arcy's ambitious mansion overlooking Clifden Bay, a haunting monument to the man who founded Connemara's capital and died in its Famine.
history: Built around 1818 by John D'Arcy, founder of Clifden town, this Gothic Revival manor house commanded spectacular views over Clifden Bay and the Twelve Bens mountains. D'Arcy poured his fortune into developing Clifden but died bankrupt. The castle was sold and eventually abandoned, its ruins now freely accessible via a scenic coastal walk from Clifden town.

name: Fiddaun Castle
description: One of Ireland's largest and best-preserved tower houses, this massive O'Shaughnessy fortress dominates the south Galway landscape from behind its double bawn walls.
history: Built in the late 15th or early 16th century by the O'Shaughnessy clan, Fiddaun Castle is among the largest tower houses in Ireland. The castle features an impressive double bawn wall—an inner and outer enclosure—and a tall tower with fine architectural details. The O'Shaughnessys held the castle until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Annaghdown Castle
description: Lakeside ruins of a medieval ecclesiastical stronghold on the shores of Lough Corrib, where bishops and chieftains once vied for spiritual and temporal power.
history: Annaghdown on the shore of Lough Corrib was an important ecclesiastical site from the 6th century, associated with St Brendan the Navigator. The castle dates to the medieval period and was linked to the diocese of Annaghdown. Nearby are the ruins of a cathedral, priory, and churches spanning several centuries of Christian worship.

name: Menlo Castle
description: A hauntingly beautiful ivy-cloaked ruin on the banks of the River Corrib, destroyed by a devastating fire that claimed the lady of the house in 1910.
history: Home to the Blake family for over 400 years, Menlo Castle was a 16th-century tower house expanded into a substantial mansion. In 1910, a catastrophic fire gutted the building, killing the elderly Eleanor Blake. The ruins, smothered in ivy beside the River Corrib just outside Galway city, are among the most atmospheric and photographed castle ruins in the west of Ireland.

name: Clonbur Castle
description: Quiet ruins of a medieval tower in the scenic lakeland between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask, where Connemara's wildness meets the fertile Joyce Country.
history: Clonbur Castle is a medieval tower house in the village of Clonbur, County Galway, situated in the scenic corridor between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask. The area was historically part of the Joyce Country, named after the Welsh-Norman Joyce family who settled here in the 13th century. The castle remains stand in the village as a reminder of medieval settlement in this lakeland district.

name: Moyode Castle
description: A magnificent ruin of a Persse family mansion in east Galway, its castellated Victorian towers and sweeping demesne a ghost of Ascendancy grandeur.
history: Moyode Castle near Craughwell was the seat of the Persse family, prominent Galway landlords. The original tower house was incorporated into a grand Victorian castellated mansion. The family was noted for their fox hunting and Lady Augusta Gregory (nee Persse) of nearby Coole Park was related. The house was burned during the Civil War in 1922 and stands as an impressive ruin.

name: Killarone Castle
description: A modest tower house ruin in the Galway countryside, one of countless small medieval fortifications that once studded the landscape of Connacht.
history: Killarone Castle is a small medieval tower house in County Galway, typical of the hundreds built by minor chieftains and landowners across Connacht between the 14th and 16th centuries. These compact defensive residences reflected the turbulent conditions of medieval Ireland where every family of means required a fortified home.

name: Lydacan Castle
description: A hidden medieval tower house in the Galway countryside, its quiet decay among the fields a testament to the countless small strongholds that once networked Irish power.
history: Lydacan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Like many similar structures in the west of Ireland, it served as a fortified residence for a local family during the turbulent 15th and 16th centuries. The tower retains some structural integrity though largely ruinous, standing in agricultural land.

name: Merlin Park Castle
description: A striking ruined mansion on the eastern fringe of Galway city, its Gothic towers rising from a public woodland park popular with walkers and runners.
history: Merlin Park was the seat of the Blake family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The castle evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial mansion, which was damaged by fire. The grounds were acquired by the state and now form a public park adjoining University Hospital Galway, with woodland walks and the castle ruins as a centrepiece.

name: Gallarus Castle
description: A fine 15th-century tower house standing near the famous Gallarus Oratory on the Dingle Peninsula, bridging a thousand years of Kerry heritage.
history: Built by the Knight of Kerry (FitzGerald family) in the 15th century, Gallarus Castle is a well-preserved tower house near the renowned Gallarus Oratory, an early Christian dry-stone church dating to perhaps the 8th century. The castle served as a FitzGerald stronghold on the Dingle Peninsula and retains its four storeys and much of its original fabric.

name: Rahinnane Castle
description: A ruined FitzGerald tower house on the Dingle Peninsula, perched on an ancient ringfort with spectacular views across Ventry Harbour to the Atlantic.
history: Built on the site of a much older ringfort, Rahinnane Castle was a FitzGerald (Knight of Kerry) stronghold on the Dingle Peninsula. The tower house dates to the 15th century and commands views over Ventry Harbour. The underlying ringfort suggests the site's strategic importance stretching back into pre-Norman times.

name: Killaha Castle
description: A small FitzGerald tower house in the Kerry countryside near Kenmare, its modest proportions belying its significance in the intricate web of Desmond power.
history: Killaha Castle is a tower house associated with the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond, located near Kenmare in County Kerry. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the Desmond lordship that controlled much of Munster. After the Desmond Rebellions and the subsequent confiscation of lands, the castle fell into decay.

name: Dunkerron Castle
description: Atmospheric ruins of an O'Sullivan Mor stronghold overlooking the Kenmare estuary, where chieftains once commanded the wild reaches of south Kerry.
history: Dunkerron Castle near Kenmare was the principal stronghold of the O'Sullivan Mor clan, chiefs of the Beara and Bantry region. The medieval castle controlled access to the Kenmare River estuary. The O'Sullivans were major Gaelic lords who resisted English expansion until the 17th century. The castle ruins stand in a picturesque setting overlooking Kenmare Bay.

name: Lixnaw Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of the Fitzmaurice Lords of Kerry's ancestral stronghold, now absorbed into the quiet north Kerry village that was once a seat of power.
history: Lixnaw was the ancestral seat of the Fitzmaurice family, Lords of Kerry, from the 13th century. The castle served as their chief residence and the village developed around it. The Fitzmaurices played significant roles in the Desmond Rebellions. Little of the original castle remains today, with fragments incorporated into later buildings in the village.

name: Belvelly Castle
description: A fine medieval tower house guarding the narrow strait between Great Island and the mainland, the gateway to Cork's historic harbour.
history: Belvelly Castle dates to the 15th century and guards the narrow channel between Cobh's Great Island and the mainland in Cork Harbour. It was held by the Hodnett family and later the Barrymore Barrys. The four-storey tower house has been restored and is one of the few tower houses in the Cork harbour area to survive substantially intact.

name: Ballinacarriga Castle
description: A beautifully situated tower house rising above a lake in West Cork, its rare sheela-na-gig carving adding a touch of medieval mystery.
history: Built around 1585 by Randal Hurley (O'Hurley), Ballinacarriga Castle is a well-preserved tower house in West Cork. The castle features a notable sheela-na-gig carving over its entrance, one of the best-preserved examples in Ireland. The five-storey tower overlooks a small lake and retains fine architectural details including carved window surrounds.

name: Castletownshend Castle
description: The historic Townshend family seat overlooking the picturesque harbour village of Castletownshend, one of West Cork's most charming settlements.
history: Castletownshend has been the seat of the Townshend family since the 17th century plantation era. The village, perched on a steep hill above the harbour, was home to authors Edith Somerville and Martin Ross, famous for their 'Irish R.M.' stories. The castle and associated buildings reflect the Ascendancy world of West Cork's Anglo-Irish families.

name: Carriganass Castle
description: A dramatic O'Sullivan Bere tower house at the head of Bantry Bay, steeped in legends of the Elizabethan wars and the flight of the Gaelic lords.
history: Built by the O'Sullivan Bere clan in the 16th century, Carriganass Castle near Kealkill in West Cork was a key stronghold during the Elizabethan conquest of Munster. It was at Carriganass that Donal Cam O'Sullivan Bere planned his resistance against the English. The castle was captured and partially destroyed during the Nine Years' War. Restored in recent years, it stands as a memorial to Gaelic resistance.

name: Lohort Castle
description: A magnificent 15th-century tower house in the Blackwater Valley, dramatically enlarged in the 19th century into an imposing castellated mansion.
history: Originally built by the Roche family in the 15th century, Lohort Castle in north Cork was extensively remodelled and enlarged in the 19th century. The castle stands in a fine estate along the Blackwater Valley. It was home to several prominent families including the Percevals. The combination of medieval tower and Victorian additions creates an impressive silhouette in the lush Blackwater landscape.

name: Monkstown Castle
description: A striking Elizabethan fortified house overlooking Cork Harbour, its distinctive profile visible from the city's harbour ferries and waterfront walks.
history: Built around 1636 by the Archdeacon family, Monkstown Castle is a well-preserved fortified house overlooking Cork Harbour. The substantial building features corbelled turrets, mullioned windows, and an impressive entrance. Its prominent position above the harbour made it a landmark for centuries of shipping entering Cork. The castle has undergone various restorations.

name: Carrigaphooca Castle
description: The 'Rock of the Fairy' — a dramatic McCarthy tower house perched on a massive glacial boulder beside the road to Macroom, steeped in folklore and shadow.
history: Built by the McCarthy clan in the 15th century, Carrigaphooca (Rock of the Fairy) Castle sits atop a huge glacial erratic boulder along the road between Cork and Macroom. Legend says the rock was placed by a pooka (fairy spirit). The tower house commanded the route into West Cork and was a McCarthy stronghold until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Ballincollig Castle
description: Substantial medieval remains in the heart of Cork's largest suburb, a surprising survivor of Norman power amid modern housing estates and shopping centres.
history: Built by the de Cogan family in the 13th century and later held by the Barretts and the Earls of Desmond, Ballincollig Castle is a significant Norman ruin now surrounded by Cork's suburban sprawl. The castle features a large rectangular tower and curtain wall. It played a role in the Desmond Rebellions before passing to English settlers. Now maintained as a local heritage site.

name: Dundanion Castle
description: A small but atmospheric medieval tower in the Cork suburb of Blackrock, its ancient stones now surrounded by parkland and sea views.
history: Dundanion Castle in Blackrock, Cork, is a medieval tower house that once overlooked the southern channel of Cork Harbour. The compact structure dates to the medieval period and was associated with various local families. Now preserved in a public park setting, it provides an unexpected medieval encounter in suburban Cork.

name: Ballindoney Castle
description: A secluded tower house ruin in the rolling Cork countryside, another link in the chain of medieval fortifications that once controlled the Munster landscape.
history: Ballindoney Castle is a medieval tower house in County Cork, one of many such structures built by local landholding families during the turbulent 15th and 16th centuries. The tower stands in rural farmland and, while ruinous, retains enough structure to convey the scale of medieval fortified domestic architecture in Munster.

name: Ardfinnan Castle
description: A commanding Norman castle on a high rock above the River Suir, guarding a strategic crossing point in the golden vale of Tipperary since the 13th century.
history: Built by King John around 1185 on a dramatic rock above the River Suir, Ardfinnan Castle was one of the earliest Norman fortifications in Tipperary. It controlled a vital river crossing on the route between Waterford and Cashel. The castle passed through various families including the Butlers and was rebuilt in the 19th century. It remains in private hands on its spectacular riverside perch.

name: Ballynahow Round Castle
description: A rare and remarkable circular castle in Tipperary — one of only a handful of round tower houses in Ireland, its unusual form both beautiful and mysterious.
history: Ballynahow Castle near Thurles is one of Ireland's rare circular castle towers, dating to the 16th century. The round design is exceptionally unusual for an Irish tower house. The castle features a spiral staircase within its walls and retains much of its original height. Its unique form has made it a subject of considerable archaeological interest.

name: Ormond Castle
description: Ireland's finest Elizabethan manor house, this remarkable Carrick-on-Suir treasure was built to welcome a queen who never came.
history: Built in the 1560s by 'Black Tom' Butler, 10th Earl of Ormond, Ormond Castle in Carrick-on-Suir is the finest surviving Elizabethan manor house in Ireland. Tom Butler, a cousin and favourite of Elizabeth I, built the elegant unfortified manor to welcome the queen on a planned visit that never materialised. The interior features remarkable decorative plasterwork with portraits of the queen. The manor adjoins two earlier 15th-century tower houses.

name: Knockelly Castle
description: A well-preserved Butler tower house in the Tipperary countryside, its intact roof and battlements a rare survival among Ireland's countless medieval towers.
history: Knockelly Castle near Fethard in County Tipperary is a well-preserved 15th-century tower house associated with the Butler family. The castle retains its roof and many original features, making it an unusually complete example of its type. It stands in farmland in the rich agricultural heartland of south Tipperary.

name: Lackeen Castle
description: A tall, imposing O'Kennedy tower house in north Tipperary, its six storeys of dark stone exuding medieval menace across the boggy lowlands.
history: Built by the O'Kennedy clan in the 15th century, Lackeen Castle is a substantial six-storey tower house near Lorrha in north Tipperary. The O'Kennedys were one of the chief Dalcassian families of north Munster. The castle features a fine bawn wall and retains much of its height and original fabric, including murder holes and a vaulted ground floor.

name: Redwood Castle
description: A beautifully restored Norman tower house in the Tipperary boglands, brought back to life as a centre for clan heritage and medieval culture.
history: Built by the Norman de Marisco family in the 12th century and later held by the MacEgan clan (hereditary Brehon lawyers), Redwood Castle near Lorrha has been carefully restored. The MacEgans ran a famous law school here. The castle now serves as a heritage centre celebrating Gaelic culture and Brehon law, offering medieval banquets and cultural events.

name: Grannagh Castle
description: A substantial Butler fortress on the banks of the River Suir, its impressive tower and curtain walls commanding the vital waterway between Kilkenny and Waterford.
history: Built by the Butler family in the 14th century, Grannagh Castle stands on the River Suir between Kilkenny and Waterford. The castle features a large tower house within a curtain wall and controlled river traffic on this important waterway. It served the Butlers as a strategic link in their chain of fortifications across the southeast.

name: Clomantagh Castle
description: A tall, slender tower house in the Kilkenny farmland, its dramatic profile visible for miles across the fertile Nore valley.
history: Clomantagh Castle is a 15th-century tower house in County Kilkenny, associated with the Shortall family. The tall, narrow tower retains much of its original height and features. It stands in rich agricultural land in the Nore valley and is characteristic of the many tower houses that dotted medieval Kilkenny.

name: Ballyragget Castle
description: A commanding tower house in the north Kilkenny market town, once a key outpost of Butler power on the frontier with Gaelic Laois.
history: Ballyragget Castle was built by the Butler family in the medieval period and served as an important stronghold on the northern frontier of their territory. The castle controlled the route from Kilkenny into the Gaelic territories of Laois and Offaly. The tower house retains significant structural elements and stands in the centre of the town.

name: Foulksrath Castle
description: A superbly restored Norman tower house near Kilkenny, now serving as a youth hostel where travellers sleep within 14th-century walls.
history: Built by the Purcell family in the 14th century, Foulksrath Castle near Jenkinstown was held by various Norman families. The well-preserved tower house features fine medieval details and has been restored and operated as An Oige (Irish Youth Hostel Association) accommodation, offering visitors the unique experience of staying in an authentic medieval castle.

name: Shankill Castle
description: An enchanting estate near Kilkenny where a medieval tower meets Georgian elegance, its walled gardens among the finest in the southeast.
history: Shankill Castle near Paulstown combines a 16th-century tower house with later Georgian additions, creating a harmonious blend of architectural periods. The estate has been home to the Cope family for over 300 years. The renowned walled gardens have been restored and opened to visitors, featuring formal plantings, a Victorian glasshouse, and woodland walks.

name: Bargy Castle
description: A fine fortified tower house in the Wexford Baronies, where the last speakers of the ancient Yola dialect once passed through its medieval doorway.
history: Bargy Castle is a medieval tower house in the Barony of Bargy, south Wexford. The Barony was notable for preserving the Yola language, an ancient form of Norman French-English, into the 19th century. The castle was held by various Anglo-Norman families and reflects the unique cultural heritage of this corner of Wexford that retained its Norman identity for centuries.

name: Clonmines
description: A ghostly deserted medieval town on the Wexford mudflats, its cluster of tower houses, churches, and monastery forming one of Ireland's most atmospheric lost settlements.
history: Clonmines was a prosperous Norman town in south Wexford that thrived in the medieval period as a port on the Bannow estuary. As the harbour silted up, the town was gradually abandoned. What remains is extraordinary: tower houses, two churches, an Augustinian priory, and town walls, all clustered together in an eerie landscape of mudflats and marsh.

name: Stradbally Castle
description: A medieval tower in the heart of Stradbally, Laois, known today more for the Electric Picnic festival that transforms its adjoining estate each September.
history: Stradbally Castle in County Laois was built by the O'More clan in the medieval period. The area later became part of the Cosby estate, whose grounds now host the Electric Picnic, one of Ireland's largest music festivals. The castle remains stand as a reminder of the area's turbulent medieval history when the O'Mores fiercely resisted English plantation.

name: Molana Abbey
description: A secluded island monastery on the Blackwater River, where ancient walls and a remarkable collection of medieval tombs lie hidden among the trees.
history: Founded in the 6th century on an island in the River Blackwater near Youghal, Molana Abbey became an Augustinian priory in the 12th century. The extensive ruins include a church, cloister, and fine collection of medieval tomb effigies. Raymond le Gros, the Norman knight who led the invasion of Ireland, is traditionally said to be buried here. The island setting adds to the atmosphere of seclusion.

name: Donore Castle
description: A compact medieval tower house in the Meath heartland near the Hill of Tara, standing watch over the storied landscape of ancient Irish kingship.
history: Donore Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, located in the historically rich landscape near the Hill of Tara and the Boyne Valley. The tower dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences that characterised the English Pale's borderlands. The area's deep historical significance stretches back millennia.

name: Rathmiles Castle
description: A quiet tower house ruin in the Meath countryside, one of countless small medieval fortifications that once punctuated the rolling fields of the Pale.
history: Rathmiles Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, dating to the 15th century. Like many similar structures in the Pale, it served as a fortified residence for a local landholding family. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land, a quiet reminder of the medieval landscape.

name: Castlepollard Castle
description: A quiet market town's medieval tower, now absorbed into the streetscape of this pretty Westmeath village near the shores of Lough Derravaragh.
history: Castlepollard in County Westmeath takes its name from its medieval castle, associated with the Pollard and later Pakenham families. The town sits near the legendary Lough Derravaragh, associated with the tale of the Children of Lir. The Pakenham family (Earls of Longford) built nearby Tullynally Castle, one of Ireland's largest castellated houses.

name: Fore Castle
description: A Norman motte and fortification in the extraordinary monastic village of Fore, home to the famous 'Seven Wonders' of Irish folklore.
history: Fore in County Westmeath is one of Ireland's most remarkable heritage sites, combining a Norman motte castle with an extensive Benedictine priory founded in the 7th century. The 'Seven Wonders of Fore' include a monastery built on a bog, water that flows uphill, and a stone raised by prayer. The castle motte dates to the Norman period and overlooks the entire valley settlement.

name: Rathconnell Tower
description: A modest medieval tower in the Westmeath midlands, a quiet survivor from the days when the Anglo-Norman frontier pushed west into Gaelic Ireland.
history: Rathconnell Tower is a medieval fortification in County Westmeath, dating to the period of Anglo-Norman expansion into the Irish midlands. The tower served as a local stronghold and reflects the pattern of Norman settlement in the region. The surrounding area contains numerous other medieval and pre-Norman archaeological sites.

name: Morett Castle
description: A ruined tower house in the Laois flatlands, a remnant of the O'More clan's fierce resistance against Tudor plantation.
history: Morett Castle in County Laois dates to the medieval period and was associated with the O'More (O'Moore) clan, who vigorously resisted the Tudor plantation of Laois (then called Queen's County). The area was the scene of some of the fiercest resistance to English colonisation in the 16th century. The castle ruins reflect this turbulent frontier history.

name: Mongavlin Castle
description: Scant remains of a great O'Donnell stronghold in the Finn Valley, once a centre of Tyrconnell power before the Flight of the Earls changed Ireland forever.
history: Mongavlin Castle near Lifford in County Donegal was a major stronghold of the O'Donnell clan, Lords of Tyrconnell. The castle played a significant role in the Nine Years' War and the events leading to the Flight of the Earls in 1607. Little remains above ground, but the site's historical significance in the story of Gaelic Ireland's final stand is immense.

name: Kilbarron Castle
description: A dramatic clifftop ruin overlooking Donegal Bay, associated with the great Franciscan historian Brother Michael O'Clery and the Annals of the Four Masters.
history: Kilbarron Castle near Ballyshannon was the ancestral home of the O'Clery family, hereditary historians to the O'Donnells. Brother Michael O'Clery, chief compiler of the Annals of the Four Masters (one of Ireland's greatest historical manuscripts), was born here around 1590. The castle ruins perch dramatically on cliffs above Donegal Bay.

name: Castle Murray
description: A ruined tower on the dramatic Donegal coast near Dunkineely, overlooking the wild Atlantic waters of McSwyne's Bay.
history: Castle Murray, also known as McSwyne's Castle, stands on the coast near Dunkineely in County Donegal. It was associated with the MacSweeney clan, gallowglass warriors who served the O'Donnells. The castle commands views over McSwyne's Bay and the Atlantic. A well-known restaurant and hotel nearby take the castle's name.

name: Malin Castle
description: Ruins of a 17th-century plantation house near Ireland's most northerly point, where the wild Inishowen Peninsula meets the Atlantic.
history: Malin Castle, near the village of Malin in Inishowen, County Donegal, dates to the early 17th century plantation period. The ruins stand near Malin Head, Ireland's most northerly point. The building served as a fortified plantation house during the Ulster Plantation. Malin Head was later used as a weather reporting station and wartime lookout.

name: Carrickabraghy Castle
description: A remote and dramatic tower house on the Isle of Doagh, Inishowen, battered by Atlantic storms at the very edge of the inhabited world.
history: Carrickabraghy Castle on the Isle of Doagh, Inishowen Peninsula, was built in the 16th century, possibly by the O'Doherty clan. The castle stands on a rocky promontory exposed to the full force of the Atlantic. Its remote and dramatic setting at the northernmost tip of Inishowen makes it one of Ireland's most wildly situated castles.

name: Rathmullan Priory
description: Evocative ruins of a Carmelite friary on the shores of Lough Swilly, where the Flight of the Earls departed Ireland forever in 1607.
history: Rathmullan Priory was founded by the Carmelite order in the 15th century on the western shore of Lough Swilly, County Donegal. The site gained its greatest historical significance on 14 September 1607, when the Earls of Tyrone and Tyrconnell sailed from Rathmullan in the Flight of the Earls, ending the old Gaelic order in Ireland. The friary ruins are well-preserved with fine carved details.

name: Castleroche
description: A spectacular Anglo-Norman fortress crowning a volcanic crag in County Louth, with legends of its murderous lady builder and views stretching to the Mourne Mountains.
history: Built around 1236 by Lady Rohesia de Verdun on a dramatic rocky outcrop, Castleroche (also known as Roche Castle) is one of Ireland's most impressively sited Norman castles. Legend says Rohesia promised to marry whoever designed the castle, then threw the architect from the window. The castle controlled the Louth-Armagh borderlands and features massive curtain walls and a fine gatehouse.

name: Dunmahon Castle
description: A rare fortified tower house perched on the Louth coastline, its unusual coastal position making it one of the most scenic ruins on the Irish Sea.
history: Dunmahon Castle is a medieval tower house on the coast of County Louth near Blackrock. The castle dates to the 15th century and served as a defensive position guarding the coastline of the Pale. Its coastal setting distinguishes it from the many inland tower houses of the region.

name: Carlingford Mint
description: A mysterious medieval building in Carlingford village, one of the best-preserved examples of a 15th-century townhouse in Ireland.
history: Known locally as 'The Mint,' this striking medieval building in Carlingford dates to the 15th century. Despite its name, there is no evidence it ever served as an actual mint. The building features fine carved limestone windows and is one of several remarkable medieval structures that make Carlingford one of Ireland's most complete medieval villages. It was likely a wealthy merchant's house.

name: Glin Castle
description: The ancestral seat of the Knights of Glin on the Shannon Estuary—a Georgian Gothic castle that was home to the FitzGerald dynasty for over 700 years.
history: Glin Castle has been the seat of the FitzGerald Knights of Glin for over 700 years. The current Gothic castle dates largely to the 1780s with later additions. The last Knight of Glin, Desmond FitzGerald, was a noted art historian and Christie's chairman who died in 2011. The castle and its beautiful gardens overlooking the Shannon Estuary have operated as a luxury accommodation venue.

name: Castle Oliver
description: A fantastical Victorian Gothic castle in the Limerick hills, reputedly the birthplace of the legendary Lola Montez and a masterpiece of theatrical architecture.
history: Built between 1845 and 1852 by the Gascoigne-Oliver family in an extravagant Gothic Revival style, Castle Oliver in County Limerick is a spectacular Victorian confection. The estate claims connection to Lola Montez (Eliza Gilbert), the dancer who became mistress of King Ludwig I of Bavaria. The castle was derelict for decades before being restored and is now available for exclusive hire.

name: Shanid Castle
description: The ancient cradle of the Desmond FitzGeralds, whose war cry 'Shanid abú!' echoed across Munster for three centuries of rebellion and conquest.
history: Shanid Castle near Shanagolden is the ancestral seat of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond, who took the war cry 'Shanid abú!' (Shanid forever!) from this fortress. The motte and castle date to the early 13th century and represent one of the earliest Norman strongholds in Munster. The Desmonds controlled much of south-west Ireland from here until the Desmond Rebellions of the 16th century.

name: Ballingarry Castle (Limerick)
description: A medieval tower in the Limerick countryside, linked to the turbulent Desmond dynasty and the wars that ravaged Munster.
history: Ballingarry Castle in County Limerick is a medieval tower house associated with the Desmond FitzGeralds and later families. The castle dates to the 15th century and stands in the agricultural heartland of west Limerick. It was part of the wider network of fortifications that controlled the Desmond lordship.

name: Castlerea Castle
description: Modest castle remains in the Roscommon town that shares its name, where the story of the Gaelic west meets the reality of English plantation.
history: Castlerea in County Roscommon takes its name from its medieval castle. The town later became associated with the Sandford family and their Clonalis House estate nearby, seat of the O'Conor Don, descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland. The castle remains are fragmentary but the town's heritage is rich with Gaelic and plantation history.

name: Donamon Castle
description: A fine medieval tower house beside the River Suck, now home to a Divine Word Missionaries religious community in the quiet Roscommon countryside.
history: Donamon Castle on the River Suck in County Roscommon dates to the 13th century and was held by the Kings of Connacht. The castle features a medieval tower house with later additions. In the 20th century, it was acquired by the Divine Word Missionaries who have used it as a retreat centre. The castle and grounds are set in a beautiful riverside location.

name: Rockfleet Castle
description: The iconic tower house of Grace O'Malley, the Pirate Queen of Connacht, standing defiant on the shores of Clew Bay where she once commanded her fleet.
history: Built in the mid-16th century, Rockfleet (Carrickahowley) Castle was the stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), Ireland's legendary pirate queen. She famously received the English governor Sir Richard Bingham here and later sailed to London to negotiate with Queen Elizabeth I. The four-storey tower house stands on the edge of Clew Bay with commanding views across the water.

name: Burrishoole Abbey
description: Hauntingly beautiful Dominican friary ruins on the shores of an inlet near Newport, where medieval arches frame views of Clew Bay and Croagh Patrick.
history: Founded in 1469 by Richard Burke, Burrishoole Abbey is a Dominican friary on the shores of an inlet of Clew Bay near Newport, County Mayo. The well-preserved ruins include a church with fine tracery windows, a cloister, and domestic buildings. Grace O'Malley is said to have worshipped here. The setting, with Croagh Patrick visible beyond, is sublimely beautiful.

name: Clare Island Castle
description: Grace O'Malley's island fortress commanding the entrance to Clew Bay, where Ireland's most famous female warrior held court over her maritime empire.
history: Clare Island Castle, also known as Granuaile's Castle, was a stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), the 16th-century pirate queen who controlled the seas off the west coast of Ireland. The tower house guards the harbour of Clare Island at the entrance to Clew Bay. O'Malley is traditionally buried in the nearby Cistercian abbey on the island.

name: Kilconly Tower
description: A modest medieval tower in the rural Mayo landscape, one of the scattered fortifications that once dotted the western seaboard.
history: Kilconly Tower is a medieval tower house in County Mayo, representative of the many small fortified residences built by local chieftains in the west of Ireland. These towers served both as residences and symbols of local authority in a landscape where power was fragmented among numerous clans.

name: Castlebar Castle
description: Scant remains of a Bingham stronghold in the county town of Mayo, where the 'Races of Castlebar' saw French and Irish rebels rout the British in 1798.
history: Castlebar Castle was built by the Bingham family, English administrators in Connacht, in the late 16th century. The town is most famous for the 1798 Battle of Castlebar, known as the 'Races of Castlebar,' when French forces under General Humbert and their Irish allies routed the British garrison in a humiliating defeat. Little of the castle survives today.

name: Kinlough Castle
description: Quiet ruins in the north Leitrim village of Kinlough, nestled between Lough Melvin and the Dartry Mountains on the Donegal border.
history: Kinlough Castle in County Leitrim stands in the village of Kinlough near the shores of Lough Melvin. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the O'Rourke clan, Lords of Breifne. The area is renowned for Lough Melvin's unique fish species and the dramatic Dartry Mountain backdrop.

name: Moyne Abbey
description: One of Ireland's most complete Franciscan friaries, its soaring six-storey tower and pristine cloisters standing timelessly on the banks of the River Moy.
history: Founded around 1460 by the Burke family, Moyne Abbey near Killala in County Mayo is one of the best-preserved Franciscan friaries in Ireland. The remains include a magnificent six-storey tower, beautiful cloister, refectory, kitchen, and dormitories. The abbey was burned by Sir Richard Bingham in 1590 but the friars continued to use it secretly until the 18th century.

name: Rosserk Friary
description: A remarkably intact Franciscan friary on the Moy estuary, featuring Ireland's only surviving medieval piscina decorated with a round tower carving.
history: Founded around 1460, Rosserk Friary on the River Moy near Killala is one of the best-preserved Franciscan friaries in Ireland. It features a unique double piscina carved with a round tower motif—the only such example known in Ireland. Burned by Sir Richard Bingham in 1590, the remarkably complete ruins include church, cloister, domestic buildings, and a beautiful riverside setting.

name: Manorhamilton Castle
description: The substantial ruins of a Scottish planter's castle in the Leitrim highlands, its massive walls a monument to the ambitions of the Ulster Plantation.
history: Built in 1634 by Sir Frederick Hamilton, a Scottish planter, Manorhamilton Castle gave its name to the town that grew around it. Hamilton was a notoriously ruthless figure who sacked Sligo Abbey in 1642. The castle was captured by Confederate forces during the 1641 Rebellion and eventually fell into ruin. Its substantial remains dominate the town square.

name: Castle Saunderson
description: A once-grand Big House on the Cavan-Fermanagh border, now the International Scout Centre of Ireland, where history meets outdoor adventure.
history: Castle Saunderson near Belturbet, County Cavan, was the seat of the Saunderson family from the 17th century. Colonel Edward Saunderson was a prominent Unionist politician. The castle was burned in 1922 and later partly rebuilt. In 2012, the estate was developed as Scouting Ireland's International Scout Centre, offering camping, kayaking, and outdoor activities on the shores of the River Erne.

name: Rathcline Castle
description: Remnants of an O'Farrell stronghold in the Longford lakelands, guarding the crossing point where ancient routes met the Shannon.
history: Rathcline Castle near Lanesborough, County Longford, was a stronghold of the O'Farrell clan on the shores of Lough Ree. The castle controlled a strategic crossing of the Shannon and was involved in various conflicts. The O'Farrells were the dominant Gaelic clan of Longford. The castle site overlooks the Shannon at one of its widest points.

name: Mannan Castle
description: A medieval motte and later tower near Carrickmacross, guarding the ancient route through the drumlin country of south Monaghan.
history: Mannan Castle near Carrickmacross, County Monaghan, features a Norman motte with later medieval additions. The site controlled a route through the drumlin landscape of south Ulster. The area was part of the McMahon territory. The castle site is now part of a golf course but retains historical interest as a marker of Norman expansion into Ulster.

name: Castle Balfour
description: A substantial Scottish planter's castle in the Fermanagh town of Lisnaskea, one of the best-preserved plantation castles in Ulster.
history: Built around 1618 by Captain James Balfour, a Scottish planter, Castle Balfour in Lisnaskea is one of the finest surviving examples of a plantation castle in Ulster. The T-shaped building features Scottish architectural details including corbelled turrets. It was damaged during the 1641 Rebellion and Williamite Wars but substantial ruins remain.

name: Castle Archdale
description: A plantation castle estate on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, famous as a World War II flying boat base and now a popular country park.
history: Castle Archdale on the shores of Lower Lough Erne was built by the Archdale family, English planters who arrived in the 17th century. During World War II, the estate served as a base for Catalina and Sunderland flying boats that patrolled the Atlantic for U-boats. The estate is now a popular country park with camping, cycling, and boat trips to White Island's mysterious carved figures.

name: Mountjoy Castle
description: A ruined Elizabethan fort on the shores of Lough Neagh, built during the Nine Years' War as a springboard for the conquest of Ulster.
history: Built in 1602 by Lord Mountjoy, the English Lord Deputy, as part of his campaign against Hugh O'Neill during the Nine Years' War. Mountjoy Castle near Coalisland, County Tyrone, stands on the shores of Lough Neagh. The fort was a strategic military installation that helped secure English control of Ulster. It was garrisoned until the mid-17th century and then fell into disuse.

name: Newtownstewart Castle
description: A massive plantation castle ruin dominating the Tyrone village of Newtownstewart, its shattered walls a monument to the turbulent Ulster Plantation.
history: Newtownstewart Castle in County Tyrone was built in the early 17th century as part of the Ulster Plantation. The substantial castle was destroyed by fire during the Williamite Wars in 1689 when the retreating Jacobite forces burned it. The impressive ruins, with their massive walls and window openings, stand prominently in the village centre.

name: Castlecaulfield
description: Elegant ruins of a Jacobean mansion in County Tyrone, where Renaissance refinement met the raw frontier of plantation Ulster.
history: Built around 1619 by Sir Toby Caulfield on the site of an O'Donnelly fort, Castlecaulfield is one of the finest examples of a Jacobean plantation house in Ireland. The mansion featured large mullioned windows, ornamental gatehouse, and elegant proportions. Burned by Patrick O'Donnelly during the 1641 Rebellion, the atmospheric ruins display the confident ambition of the planter class.

name: Brackfield Bawn
description: A rare surviving plantation bawn in County Derry, its defensive walls a tangible reminder of the insecure world of Ulster's settler community.
history: Brackfield Bawn near Claudy in County Derry is a rare surviving example of a plantation-era fortified enclosure (bawn). Built in the early 17th century as part of the Plantation of Ulster, the bawn provided a defensive compound for settler families. These enclosures were a distinctive feature of the plantation landscape, and Brackfield's survival makes it an important heritage site.

name: Strangford Castle
description: A compact tower house guarding the narrow entrance to Strangford Lough, one of Europe's great sea loughs and a wildlife wonderland.
history: Strangford Castle is a 16th-century tower house in the village of Strangford, County Down, overlooking the narrow strait that connects Strangford Lough to the Irish Sea. The name 'Strangford' comes from the Old Norse 'Strangr Fjorthr' meaning strong inlet, reflecting the powerful tidal currents. The castle, along with nearby Portaferry Castle, guarded this strategically vital waterway.

name: Ardglass Castles
description: A remarkable cluster of medieval tower houses and fortifications guarding an ancient Down harbour, the most castle-dense village in Ireland.
history: Ardglass in County Down boasts the greatest concentration of medieval castles in any village in Ireland. Jordan's Castle (15th century) is the best preserved and now houses a small museum. Other castles include King's Castle, Isabella's Tower, Cowd Castle, and Margaret's Castle. The town was one of the busiest ports in Ulster during the medieval period, hence its impressive fortifications.

name: Portaferry Castle
description: A sturdy 16th-century tower house guarding the eastern shore of the Strangford Lough narrows, facing its twin at Strangford across the rushing tide.
history: Portaferry Castle is a 16th-century tower house on the eastern shore of the Strangford Lough narrows in County Down. Built by the Savage family, it controlled the eastern approach to the lough alongside Strangford Castle on the opposite shore. The castle has been restored and houses a visitor information centre.

name: Mahee Island Nendrum
description: An early Christian monastery on a tidal island in Strangford Lough, where concentric stone walls enclose one of Ireland's most complete monastic sites.
history: Nendrum Monastery on Mahee Island in Strangford Lough was founded by St Mochaoi in the 5th century. The site features three concentric enclosures, a round tower stump, church ruins, and a remarkably well-preserved sundial—one of the oldest in Ireland. Excavations revealed a monastic tide mill dating to 619 AD, one of the earliest known in the world.

name: Buttevant Friary
description: Substantial Franciscan friary ruins in the north Cork town that gave the world the word 'steeplechase,' its medieval walls still standing amid the bustle of daily life.
history: Founded in 1251 by David Óg Barry, Buttevant Friary (Ballybeg Abbey) is a substantial Franciscan foundation in north Cork. The town of Buttevant is said to have given its name to the steeplechase—a race between the steeples of Buttevant and Doneraile churches in 1752. The friary features a fine church, cloister, and domestic buildings.

name: Duiske Abbey
description: A magnificently restored 13th-century Cistercian abbey in the charming Kilkenny town of Graiguenamanagh, still in active use as a parish church.
history: Founded in 1204 by William Marshal, 1st Earl of Pembroke, Duiske Abbey in Graiguenamanagh was one of the largest Cistercian abbeys in Ireland. After dissolution in the 16th century, the abbey fell into ruin but was gradually restored from the 1970s onward. It now serves as the parish church, with magnificent Romanesque and Gothic architectural details preserved.

name: Black Castle (Wicklow)
description: Dramatic Norman ruins perched on a rocky headland above Wicklow town harbour, battered by the Irish Sea for eight centuries.
history: Built by the FitzGerald family around 1176, Black Castle stands on a rocky promontory jutting into the sea at Wicklow town. The Norman fortress controlled the harbour and the surrounding coastline. The castle was attacked by Gaelic clans numerous times and was finally abandoned in the 17th century. The dramatic ruins, connected to the mainland by a narrow ridge, offer spectacular coastal views.

name: Coolbanagher Castle
description: A quiet tower house ruin in the Laois countryside, typical of the fortified residences that once lined the frontier between the English Pale and Gaelic Ireland.
history: Coolbanagher Castle in County Laois is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th or 16th century. The area was on the contested frontier between English-controlled territory and the Gaelic lands of the O'Mores. The nearby Church of Ireland church was designed by the famous architect James Gandon, best known for Dublin's Custom House.

name: Muckinish Castle
description: A small tower house ruin in the heart of the Burren, surrounded by the extraordinary limestone pavement and wildflowers of this unique landscape.
history: Muckinish Castle is a tower house in the Burren, County Clare, dating to the 15th or 16th century. Built by a local clan, it is one of many such towers that dot the Burren landscape. The castle's setting amid the karst limestone makes it a distinctive feature of this UNESCO-listed geopark region.

name: Finavarra Castle
description: A coastal tower house ruin near the Flaggy Shore in the Burren, where Seamus Heaney found poetic inspiration in the meeting of limestone and sea.
history: Finavarra Castle is a medieval tower house on the coast of the Burren in County Clare, near the Flaggy Shore made famous by Seamus Heaney's poem 'Postscript.' The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was likely built by the O'Briens or another local clan. Its coastal setting in the Burren makes it a unique heritage site.

name: Rossroe Castle
description: A small tower house on the northern edge of the Burren, overlooking the blue waters of Galway Bay and the distant Connemara mountains.
history: Rossroe Castle is a medieval tower house in the Burren region of County Clare, overlooking Galway Bay. The compact tower dates to the 15th or 16th century and was built by a local clan. Its position on the Burren coast provides fine views across to Connemara and the Aran Islands.

name: Ballymulcashel Castle
description: A ruined tower house in the Clare countryside near Lough Derg, standing quietly amid the rolling farmland of east Clare.
history: Ballymulcashel Castle is a medieval tower house in County Clare near the shores of Lough Derg. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of many such fortified residences in the area controlled by the O'Brien and McNamara clans. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land.

name: Caherkinmonwee Castle
description: A small fortification in the Burren limestone landscape, where medieval stonework merges with the ancient karst terrain in an almost seamless union.
history: Caherkinmonwee is a medieval fortified site in the Burren, County Clare. The structure blends into the limestone landscape characteristic of the Burren region. Like many fortifications in this area, it likely dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold for one of the Clare clans.

name: Mountcashel Castle
description: Ruins of a once-significant stronghold near Ennis, associated with the McNamara clan who controlled much of medieval Clare.
history: Mountcashel Castle in County Clare was associated with the McNamara clan, one of the principal families of the region. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local power centre. The McNamaras held extensive territories across Clare and built numerous tower houses and fortifications.

name: Kilkishen Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the east Clare countryside, one of the many small strongholds that once studded this landscape of clan rivalries.
history: Kilkishen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Clare. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was likely built by one of the local clans—the McNamaras or O'Briens—who controlled different parts of Clare. The tower stands in the rural landscape of east Clare.

name: Moghane Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Clare lowlands, standing near the ancient hillfort of Mooghaun where the greatest Bronze Age gold hoard in western Europe was discovered.
history: Moghane Castle is a medieval tower house near Newmarket-on-Fergus in County Clare. It stands close to the great prehistoric hillfort of Mooghaun, near where the famous Great Clare Gold Hoard was discovered in 1854. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortifications in the Thomond region.

name: Ballyallia Castle
description: A fine McNamara tower house overlooking Ballyallia Lake near Ennis, a well-preserved medieval landmark in the Clare capital's green hinterland.
history: Ballyallia Castle near Ennis, County Clare, is a well-preserved tower house dating to the 15th century. Built by the McNamara clan, it overlooks Ballyallia Lake, a popular walking and birdwatching destination. The castle retains much of its original structure and is a prominent landmark on the approach to Ennis from the north.

name: Clarecastle Tower
description: A medieval tower in the village of Clarecastle, once a Norman stronghold controlling the route southward from Ennis along the River Fergus.
history: Clarecastle was an important Norman settlement on the River Fergus south of Ennis. The tower dates to the medieval period and was part of the Norman presence in Thomond. The de Clare family, who gave the county its name, established their base here. Thomas de Clare was killed at the Battle of Dysert O'Dea in 1318, ending Norman power in Clare.

name: Rosslevan Castle
