description: A small medieval tower near Ennis, another thread in Clare's extraordinarily dense tapestry of tower houses and medieval fortifications.
history: Rosslevan Castle is a medieval tower house near Ennis, County Clare. Like many of the dozens of tower houses in the area, it was built by a local clan family in the 15th or 16th century. Clare has one of the highest concentrations of tower houses in Ireland, reflecting the wealth and competitiveness of the local clans.

name: Cahermurphy Castle
description: A tower house in the rolling west Clare landscape, one of the McNamara strongholds that once formed a chain of power across the county.
history: Cahermurphy Castle is a medieval tower house in west Clare. Built by the McNamara clan in the 15th or 16th century, it formed part of their extensive network of fortifications across the county. The tower stands in the rural landscape between Ennis and the Atlantic coast.

name: Clonderalaw Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the wild Shannon estuary landscape of west Clare, commanding views across the great river's wide mouth.
history: Clonderalaw Castle is a medieval tower house in west Clare overlooking the Shannon Estuary. Built in the 15th or 16th century, likely by a branch of the McNamara or O'Brien clans, it controlled territory along the estuary. The castle's setting in the estuary landscape provides wide views across the Shannon.

name: Carrigaholt Castle
description: A magnificent 15th-century tower house commanding the mouth of the Shannon, where dolphins now play beneath the walls that once watched for Spanish sails.
history: Built by the McMahon clan in the late 15th century, Carrigaholt Castle guards the entrance to the Shannon Estuary in west Clare. The impressive five-storey tower house was garrisoned by Spanish Armada survivors in 1588 before being captured by Cromwellian forces. Today the castle overlooks a harbour famous for dolphin-watching boat trips in the estuary.

name: Doonbeg Castle
description: Ruins of a tower house in the west Clare village of Doonbeg, guarding the mouth of the Doonbeg River where it meets the wild Atlantic.
history: Doonbeg Castle is a 16th-century tower house at the mouth of the Doonbeg River in west Clare. The castle was built by the Turlough clan and later held by various families. The village of Doonbeg sits between the Atlantic coast and the rolling Clare countryside. The nearby Trump International Golf Links has brought modern fame to the area.

name: Kilrush Tower House
description: A medieval tower in the heritage town of Kilrush, gateway to the Shannon Estuary and the mysterious monastic island of Scattery.
history: Kilrush Tower House stands in the west Clare town of Kilrush, which served as an important port on the Shannon Estuary. The tower dates to the medieval period and reflects the town's strategic significance. Kilrush is the departure point for boats to Scattery Island, with its remarkable cluster of monastic remains and round tower.

name: Knockfin Castle
description: A remote tower house ruin in the Galway countryside, standing watch over the vast boglands and stone-walled fields of east Connacht.
history: Knockfin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Like many such structures in the west, it was built by a local clan family as a fortified residence during the 15th or 16th century. The tower stands in the agricultural landscape of eastern Galway.

name: Derryhivenny Castle
description: A beautifully preserved tower house in east Galway, its imposing five-storey bulk and fine architectural details reflecting the wealth of the O'Madden clan.
history: Built in 1643 by Daniel O'Madden, Derryhivenny Castle near Portumna is one of the last tower houses built in Ireland. The fine five-storey tower features excellent stone carvings and a notable fortified gatehouse. It represents the final flowering of the Irish tower house tradition before the Cromwellian conquest transformed the country's architecture.

name: Kilcornan Castle
description: A quiet medieval tower in the Galway countryside, one of the chain of fortified residences that once controlled the fertile lowlands east of the city.
history: Kilcornan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Built in the 15th or 16th century by a local clan family, it was one of many such structures in the productive farmland east of Galway city. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land.

name: Caherlistrane Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Galway village of Caherlistrane, in the fertile plain between Lough Corrib and Tuam.
history: Caherlistrane Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. The village sits in the fertile agricultural plain between Lough Corrib and Tuam. The castle was built by a local clan family and served as a fortified residence during the turbulent medieval period.

name: Moylough Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the east Galway village of Moylough, where the famous Moylough Belt Shrine was discovered in nearby bogland.
history: Moylough Castle is a medieval fortification in County Galway. The village is famous for the discovery of the Moylough Belt Shrine, an 8th-century decorated bronze belt reliquary now in the National Museum of Ireland. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Monivea Castle
description: Ruins of a Ffrenche family castle in east Galway, surrounded by a beautiful demesne with an arboretum and nature trail.
history: Monivea Castle was the seat of the Ffrenche family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The castle evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial mansion that was burned in 1826 and rebuilt. The estate features a notable arboretum and woodland walks. The nearby village preserves the planned estate village character typical of the Big House era.

name: Ballinderry Castle
description: A tower house ruin in east Galway, standing quietly in the pastoral landscape that stretches between Loughrea and the Shannon plain.
history: Ballinderry Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of numerous tower houses in the productive farmland of east Galway. The castle served as a fortified residence for a local family during the medieval period.

name: Loughrea Castle
description: Fragmentary Norman remains in the heritage town of Loughrea, once the de Burgh stronghold that controlled the eastern approaches to Galway.
history: Loughrea Castle was built by Richard de Burgh in the 13th century as part of the Norman conquest of Connacht. The town grew around the castle and became an important Anglo-Norman settlement. The town walls, a Carmelite monastery, and the remarkable St Brendan's Cathedral (with its outstanding collection of Celtic Revival stained glass) all stem from this medieval foundation.

name: Garbally Castle
description: Ruins of a grand estate near Ballinasloe, once seat of the Clancarty family and now part of a school campus in east Galway.
history: Garbally Castle near Ballinasloe was the seat of the Le Poer Trench family, Earls of Clancarty. The original castle dates to the medieval period, with the main house rebuilt in the 18th and 19th centuries. The estate now houses Garbally College, a secondary school. The grounds retain parkland character and some historical features.

name: Clonbrock Castle
description: A melancholy Victorian ruin in east Galway, its crumbling towers rising from an overgrown demesne that was once a centre of Anglo-Irish society.
history: Clonbrock House near Ahascragh was the seat of the Dillon family, Lords Clonbrock. The house evolved from a medieval castle into an impressive Victorian mansion. The family's remarkable photographic archive, documenting Irish Big House life, is now in the National Library. The house fell into ruin after being sold in the 20th century.

name: Cloghanower Castle
description: A remote tower house in the wilds of Connemara, one of the few medieval fortifications in this rugged, stone-walled landscape.
history: Cloghanower Castle is a tower house in the Connemara region of County Galway. Medieval fortifications are rare in Connemara due to its harsh terrain and sparse population. The tower dates to the 15th or 16th century and likely served as a residence for a local O'Flaherty chieftain.

name: Renvyle Castle
description: Haunting remains of an O'Flaherty castle on the wild Renvyle Peninsula, later owned by Oliver St John Gogarty, the wit and model for Buck Mulligan.
history: Renvyle Castle on the tip of the Renvyle Peninsula in Connemara was an O'Flaherty stronghold. It later became the property of Oliver St John Gogarty, the surgeon, poet, and wit who was James Joyce's model for Buck Mulligan in Ulysses. Gogarty converted the castle into a hotel; it burned in the Civil War but was rebuilt. Renvyle House Hotel still operates today.

name: Hen's Castle
description: A mysterious island fortress on Lough Corrib, where Grace O'Malley allegedly defended the walls while her defenders wavered—hence 'the Hen's Castle.'
history: Hen's Castle (Caislean na Circe) sits on a small island in Lough Corrib. According to legend, Grace O'Malley defended the castle so fiercely when men refused to fight that it was renamed in her honour. The castle dates to the 13th century and was originally an O'Flaherty stronghold. Its island setting makes it difficult to access but atmospheric to view.

name: Carna Castle
description: A modest castle ruin in the Connemara Gaeltacht, where the Irish language and ancient traditions survive amid one of Europe's wildest coastal landscapes.
history: Carna Castle is in the heart of the Connemara Gaeltacht (Irish-speaking area) in County Galway. The castle dates to the medieval period and is associated with local clan families. The Carna area is renowned for its strong Irish language tradition, traditional music, and spectacular coastal scenery.

name: Ardamullivan Castle
description: A substantial and well-preserved tower house deep in the Burren, its pale limestone walls emerging from the moonscape of ancient rock like a natural formation.
history: Ardamullivan Castle is a well-preserved 16th-century tower house in the Burren, County Galway. Built by the O'Shaughnessy clan, the four-storey tower retains much of its original fabric and sits in a dramatic Burren landscape of limestone pavement and hazel scrub. It is one of the finest tower houses in the Burren region.

name: Pallas Castle
description: A striking tower house in the south Galway countryside, its walls rising sheer from farmland at the edge of the Burren.
history: Pallas Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway near the Burren border. Built by the Burke family in the 16th century, the tall tower retains good structural integrity. It was part of the network of fortified residences controlled by the various branches of the powerful de Burgh (Burke) family in Connacht.

name: Ballybrit Castle
description: A tower house near the famous Galway Racecourse, where medieval heritage meets the thunder of hooves during the legendary Galway Races.
history: Ballybrit Castle is a medieval tower house on the eastern outskirts of Galway city, near Ballybrit Racecourse, home of the famous Galway Races held each July/August. The castle dates to the medieval period and is one of numerous tower houses that once ringed the medieval city of Galway.

name: Rahasane Castle
description: A medieval tower ruin in the south Galway turlough country, surrounded by the seasonally flooding lakes that make this landscape unique in Europe.
history: Rahasane Castle is a medieval tower house near Rahasane in County Galway. The area is notable for its turloughs—seasonal lakes that fill in winter and drain in summer, a phenomenon unique to Ireland's limestone regions. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and served as a local stronghold.

name: Carrigadrohid Castle
description: A dramatic castle ruin on a rocky island in the River Lee, where a Catholic bishop was hanged from the walls during the Cromwellian wars.
history: Carrigadrohid Castle stands on a rock in the River Lee west of Cork city. Built by the McCarthy clan in the medieval period, it gained infamy during the Cromwellian wars when Bishop Boetius MacEgan was captured while trying to rally Confederate forces and hanged from the castle walls in 1650. The castle ruins are accessible via a bridge and create a dramatic riverside scene.

name: Castledonovan
description: A remote McCarthy tower house in the wild West Cork hills, its isolated setting among bog and mountain lending it an air of frontier defiance.
history: Castledonovan is a tower house built by the McCarthy clan in the 15th century in a remote location in West Cork. The castle was a stronghold of the McCarthy Reagh (southern McCarthy) branch. Its isolated hillside setting, surrounded by bog and rough grazing, reflects the harsh conditions of life in medieval West Cork.

name: Dunmanus Castle
description: A lonely O'Mahony tower on the wild shores of Dunmanus Bay, where the Mizen Peninsula's dramatic coastline meets the deep Atlantic.
history: Dunmanus Castle is an O'Mahony tower house on the shore of Dunmanus Bay on the Mizen Peninsula in West Cork. The castle dates to the 15th century and controlled access to the bay. The O'Mahonys held extensive territory along the West Cork coast. The castle's remote setting on one of Ireland's least-visited peninsulas adds to its atmosphere.

name: Rossbrin Castle
description: A small O'Mahony tower house overlooking Roaringwater Bay, where seals bask on rocks below walls that once watched for approaching Viking longships.
history: Rossbrin Castle near Schull in West Cork is a small tower house built by the O'Mahony clan. The castle overlooks Roaringwater Bay with views to the offshore islands. The O'Mahonys controlled much of the coastline between Baltimore and Bantry. The castle's compact scale and coastal setting make it characteristic of West Cork's maritime heritage.

name: Cloghane Castle
description: Ruins in the remote Dingle Peninsula village of Cloghane, nestled beneath the towering bulk of Mount Brandon and the wild Kerry mountains.
history: Cloghane Castle is a medieval fortification in the village of Cloghane on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry. The village sits beneath Mount Brandon, Ireland's second-highest mountain and an important pilgrimage site associated with St Brendan the Navigator. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Togher Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Cork countryside, one of the many medieval fortifications that dotted the fertile Blackwater and Lee valleys.
history: Togher Castle is a medieval tower house in County Cork. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of numerous tower houses in the productive agricultural lands of central Cork. The tower served as a fortified residence for a local family.

name: Castlemagner Castle
description: A medieval stronghold in the Blackwater Valley of north Cork, associated with the powerful Roche and Barry families of medieval Munster.
history: Castlemagner Castle in north Cork dates to the medieval period and was held by various Anglo-Norman families including the Roches and Barrys. The castle controlled territory in the fertile Blackwater Valley. The area was part of the complex patchwork of Norman and Gaelic lordships in medieval Munster.

name: Liscarroll Castle
description: One of the largest and most impressive Norman castles in Ireland, its massive curtain walls enclosing a vast area in the north Cork countryside.
history: Liscarroll Castle in north Cork is one of the largest Norman castles in Ireland, with massive curtain walls and corner towers enclosing a large rectangular area. Built in the 13th century, possibly by the Barry family, the castle was a major military stronghold. It was the site of a significant battle in 1642 during the Confederate Wars when Garret Barry defeated English forces.

name: Castlepook Castle
description: Ruins near the famous Castlepook Cave, one of Ireland's most important archaeological sites where Ice Age bear bones were discovered.
history: Castlepook Castle in north Cork stands near Castlepook Cave, where excavations revealed the bones of brown bears, arctic foxes, and other Ice Age fauna dating back 40,000 years. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortifications in the Blackwater Valley region.

name: Drishane Castle
description: A Victorian castellated mansion in Millstreet, north Cork, set in extensive grounds that now host the prestigious Millstreet International Horse Show.
history: Drishane Castle in Millstreet, County Cork, is a castellated Victorian mansion built on the site of an earlier McCarthy stronghold. The estate, now owned by Noel C. Duggan, hosts the Millstreet International Horse Show and has hosted the Eurovision Song Contest in 1993. The grounds cover over 400 acres of parkland.

name: Dromaneen Castle
description: A fine tower house in the rich farmland of north Cork, associated with the storied Nagle family who shaped Irish history.
history: Dromaneen Castle near Mallow in north Cork is a tower house associated with the Nagle family. Nano Nagle, founder of the Presentation Sisters and a pioneer of Catholic education in penal Ireland, was connected to this area. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the fertile Blackwater Valley farmland.

name: Castlelyons Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the pretty village of Castlelyons, where the powerful Barrymore Barrys once held sway over a wide swath of east Cork.
history: Castlelyons was the seat of the Barry family, Viscounts Barrymore, one of the most powerful Anglo-Norman families in Cork. The castle dates to the medieval period and the village retains a planned estate character. The nearby Barryscourt Castle in Carrigtwohill is a better-preserved Barry stronghold. A fine Carmelite friary also stands in the village.

name: Conna Castle
description: A tall, dramatic FitzGerald tower on the banks of the Bride River, its dark stone walls and strategic position reflecting centuries of Desmond power.
history: Conna Castle in east Cork is a tall tower house on the River Bride, built by the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond in the 15th century. The castle was a significant Desmond stronghold. Thomas FitzGerald, the last Earl of Desmond, was captured and killed near here in 1583, effectively ending the Desmond Rebellions. The imposing ruins overlook the river.

name: Ballymalis Castle
description: A tower house ruin near Killorglin in Kerry, standing in the shadow of the Reeks mountains in one of Ireland's most scenic landscapes.
history: Ballymalis Castle is a medieval tower house near Killorglin, County Kerry. Built in the 15th or 16th century, likely by the McCarthy or FitzGerald families, the castle stands in the scenic area between Killorglin and the MacGillycuddy's Reeks mountain range. The tower is in a ruinous state but retains some structural features.

name: Castleisland Castle
description: The historic heart of the Kerry town that bears its name, once a major FitzGerald stronghold commanding the route into the Dingle Peninsula.
history: Castleisland Castle was originally built by Geoffrey de Marisco in the 13th century and later became a stronghold of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond. The town grew around the castle and became an important market centre. The castle was destroyed during the Desmond Rebellions and again in the Cromwellian period. Fragments remain in the town centre.

name: Ballyheigue Castle
description: Romantic ruins of a Crosby family mansion above the long sandy beach of Ballyheigue on Kerry's north coast.
history: Ballyheigue Castle was the seat of the Crosby family in north Kerry. The castle estate overlooked one of Kerry's finest beaches. The original medieval castle was replaced by a substantial mansion that fell into ruin. The atmospheric remains stand above the sweeping bay with views to Kerry Head.

name: Fenit Castle
description: Coastal fortification remains at the harbour of Fenit, departure point for St Brendan's legendary voyage across the Atlantic.
history: Fenit in County Kerry is traditionally the birthplace and departure point of St Brendan the Navigator, who is said to have sailed to North America in the 6th century. The castle or fortification at Fenit dates to the medieval period and guarded the harbour. A modern statue of St Brendan commemorates the village's most famous son.

name: Parkavonear Castle
description: A romantically ruined tower house beside the lakes of Killarney, its ivy-clad walls reflecting in the still waters of the Aghadoe landscape.
history: Parkavonear Castle near Aghadoe outside Killarney is a medieval tower house in a picturesque lakeside setting. The castle was held by various families including the McCarthys and later the Herberts. Its position near the famous lakes of Killarney and the ancient church of Aghadoe makes it part of a rich heritage landscape.

name: Castlegregory Tower
description: A small tower in the Dingle Peninsula village of Castlegregory, gateway to the wild Maharees Peninsula and its spectacular beaches.
history: Castlegregory takes its name from Gregory Hoare's castle, built in the medieval period. The village sits at the base of the Maharees Peninsula, a sand spit with beautiful beaches and the historically significant Brandon Creek. The castle remains are modest but the village's heritage as a medieval settlement endures.

name: Castlemaine Castle
description: Scant but historically significant remains near the head of Castlemaine Harbour, where the River Maine meets the sea in the shadow of the Slieve Mish mountains.
history: Castlemaine Castle stood near the head of Castlemaine Harbour in Kerry, controlling the junction of several routes into the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas. The castle dates to the medieval period and was an important strategic position. The Castlemaine harbour area was traditionally a significant trading and fishing centre.

name: Duagh Castle
description: A medieval tower ruin in the quiet Kerry village of Duagh, birthplace of the great playwright and writer John B. Keane.
history: Duagh Castle is a medieval tower house in the north Kerry village of Duagh. The village is best known as the birthplace of John B. Keane, one of Ireland's most celebrated playwrights, author of 'The Field' and 'Sive.' The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Grantstown Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Tipperary countryside near the River Suir, part of the dense network of Butler strongholds in the Golden Vale.
history: Grantstown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. Built in the 15th century, it was part of the extensive Butler domain in the fertile Golden Vale. The tower stands in agricultural land near the River Suir and is representative of the many fortified residences built by Butler vassals and allies.

name: Knockgraffon Castle
description: A medieval motte and castle remains overlooking the Golden Vale, where the ancient coronation mound of the Kings of Munster still rises from the Tipperary fields.
history: Knockgraffon in County Tipperary features a Norman motte built on or near the ancient inauguration site of the Kings of Munster. The motte and castle date to the 12th-13th century period of Norman expansion. The site's dual heritage—pre-Christian royal inauguration mound and Norman military fortification—makes it historically significant.

name: Two-Mile-Borris Castle
description: A tower house near the Tipperary town of Two-Mile-Borris, another link in the chain of medieval fortifications across the Golden Vale.
history: Two-Mile-Borris Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle was part of the dense network of fortified residences in the rich agricultural lands of the Golden Vale. The area was contested between various Anglo-Norman and Gaelic families throughout the medieval period.

name: Moycarkey Castle
description: A substantial medieval castle ruin in the heart of Tipperary's Golden Vale, once a stronghold of the powerful Butler dynasty.
history: Moycarkey Castle in County Tipperary was a significant Butler stronghold in the Golden Vale. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled productive agricultural land. The Butlers of Moycarkey were a prominent branch of the wider Butler family that dominated Tipperary for centuries.

name: Dromineer Castle
description: A lakeside castle ruin on the shores of Lough Derg, now a picturesque harbour village popular with sailing enthusiasts.
history: Dromineer Castle stands on the shores of Lough Derg in north Tipperary. The medieval castle guarded the harbour on one of the Shannon's largest lakes. Dromineer is now a popular sailing centre and the castle ruins add character to the pretty waterfront. The village hosts regular sailing regattas.

name: Pottlerath Castle
description: A tower house in the Kilkenny countryside, its walls standing proud among the rolling fields of Ireland's medieval heartland.
history: Pottlerath Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny. Built in the 15th century, it was one of many such fortified residences in the Butler heartlands of Kilkenny. The tower retains structural integrity and stands in the pastoral landscape typical of the Nore valley region.

name: Kiltinan Castle
description: A massive and imposing Butler fortress near Fethard, one of the largest and most dramatic medieval castles in County Tipperary.
history: Kiltinan Castle near Fethard in County Tipperary is a large medieval castle associated with the Butler family. The impressive fortress features substantial walls and towers and controlled the rich farmland of south Tipperary. It has been partially restored and is in private ownership.

name: Ballycahill Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Tipperary midlands, overlooking the Devil's Bit mountain and the wild uplands of central Ireland.
history: Ballycahill Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary near the Devil's Bit mountain. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the area. The Devil's Bit mountain, with its distinctive gap in the ridge, is associated with numerous legends.

name: Sopwell Castle
description: A quiet ruin near Cloughjordan in north Tipperary, in the historically rich borderlands between Butler and O'Brien territories.
history: Sopwell Castle is a medieval fortification near Cloughjordan in north Tipperary. The area was on the contested border between the Butler lands to the south and the O'Brien territory of Thomond to the north. The castle reflects the military tensions of this frontier zone.

name: Castle Matrix
description: A beautifully restored FitzGerald tower house near Rathkeale, where Walter Raleigh and Edmund Spenser once dined and the potato was reputedly first planted in Ireland.
history: Castle Matrix near Rathkeale, County Limerick, dates to 1440 and was built by the 7th Earl of Desmond. The castle claims to be where Sir Walter Raleigh met Edmund Spenser and where the potato was first planted in Ireland. Beautifully restored in the 20th century, it housed an important collection of rare books and manuscripts.

name: Ballygrennan Castle
description: A tower house near Limerick city, its medieval walls now surrounded by the expanding suburbs of Ireland's third-largest city.
history: Ballygrennan Castle is a medieval tower house on the outskirts of Limerick city. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of several tower houses that guarded the approaches to the city. As Limerick has expanded, the castle has become an urban heritage site amid modern development.

name: Shanpallas Castle
description: A quiet tower house in the Limerick countryside, one of many medieval fortifications scattered across the rich lands of the old Desmond lordship.
history: Shanpallas Castle is a medieval tower house in County Limerick. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was part of the extensive network of fortified residences in the Desmond lordship. The tower stands in agricultural land in the fertile Limerick plain.

name: Fanningstown Castle
description: A restored medieval castle near Adare, offering atmospheric self-catering accommodation within authentic 15th-century walls.
history: Fanningstown Castle near Adare in County Limerick is a 15th-century tower house that has been restored and converted into luxury self-catering accommodation. The castle retains many original features and offers guests an authentic medieval castle experience in one of Ireland's prettiest villages. Nearby Adare is famous for its thatched cottages and medieval religious houses.

name: Newcastle West Castle
description: Impressive Desmond castle remains in the heart of Newcastle West, featuring two remarkable medieval halls—the finest secular medieval buildings in Limerick.
history: Newcastle West Castle was a principal seat of the FitzGerald Earls of Desmond. The castle complex includes Desmond Hall, a magnificent 15th-century banqueting hall, and the earlier Hall of the Asking. These are among the finest medieval secular buildings in Ireland. The castle was confiscated after the Desmond Rebellions and later partly demolished.

name: Carrickkildavnet Castle
description: Grace O'Malley's Achill Island tower house, standing on a rocky shore where the Pirate Queen commanded the seas between Clare Island and the mainland.
history: Carrickkildavnet Castle on Achill Island, County Mayo, was a stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), the 16th-century pirate queen. The tower house stands on the southern coast of Achill with commanding views across Achill Sound. It was a key position in O'Malley's control of the western seaways and remains an iconic Achill landmark.

name: Ballylahan Castle
description: A medieval bridge-castle spanning the River Moy in County Mayo, an unusual and picturesque combination of bridge and fortified tower.
history: Ballylahan Castle near Foxford in County Mayo is an unusual medieval structure combining a castle with a bridge over the River Moy. Built by the Jordan (Mac Siurtain) family in the 13th century, this castle-on-a-bridge is a rare type in Ireland. The remaining tower stands on one of the bridge piers over the river.

name: Turlough Round Tower
description: A fine early Christian round tower beside the historic Turlough village, now home to the National Museum of Country Life.
history: The Round Tower at Turlough, County Mayo, dates to the 12th century and stands beside the ruins of a medieval church. The nearby Turlough Park House, a Victorian Gothic mansion, now houses the National Museum of Ireland - Country Life, displaying rural Irish heritage. The round tower and church ruins add medieval atmosphere to the museum grounds.

name: Castlecarra
description: A ruined medieval tower on the shores of Lough Carra in County Mayo, overlooking one of Ireland's clearest and most beautiful limestone lakes.
history: Castlecarra is a medieval tower house on the shores of Lough Carra in County Mayo. Lough Carra is renowned as one of Ireland's most pristine and ecologically important lakes, with remarkably clear water and a rich archaeological landscape. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by local Mayo families.

name: Ballinrobe Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the south Mayo market town of Ballinrobe, where Captain Boycott's infamous treatment of tenants gave the English language a new word.
history: Ballinrobe Castle in County Mayo dates to the medieval period and is associated with the de Burgh (Burke) family's Norman expansion into Connacht. The town later gained fame as the home of Captain Charles Boycott, the land agent whose ostracism by local tenants in 1880 gave the word 'boycott' to the English language.

name: Shrule Castle
description: A Norman castle at a historic crossing of the River Black in south Mayo, where three counties meet and medieval frontier meets modern Ireland.
history: Shrule Castle in County Mayo was built at a strategic crossing of the River Black near the junction of counties Mayo, Galway, and Roscommon. The Norman castle dates to the 13th century and controlled this important route. In 1642, a tragic massacre of Protestant settlers occurred near Shrule during the Confederate Wars.

name: Castleaffy
description: A quiet tower house in the Mayo countryside, one of the scattered medieval fortifications built by the Burke clan across Connacht.
history: Castleaffy is a medieval tower house in County Mayo associated with the Burke (de Burgh) family. The Burkes were the dominant Anglo-Norman family in Connacht and built numerous castles across Mayo and Galway. This tower dates to the medieval period and served as a local Burke residence.

name: Belcarra Castle
description: A tower house ruin near the charming Mayo village of Belcarra, associated with the family of Margaret Burke Sheridan, Ireland's greatest opera diva.
history: Belcarra Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Belcarra in County Mayo. The village is associated with Margaret Burke Sheridan (1889-1958), Ireland's greatest opera singer, who was born nearby. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the Burke family's extensive holdings in Mayo.

name: Aille Castle
description: A small medieval tower on the wild Achill Island coast, surrounded by some of Ireland's most dramatic Atlantic scenery.
history: Aille Castle is a medieval tower on Achill Island, County Mayo. The castle stands in the wild coastal landscape of Ireland's largest island, surrounded by spectacular cliffs, beaches, and mountain scenery. Achill was home to a significant population throughout the medieval period despite its remote Atlantic location.

name: Owenduff Castle
description: Remote ruins near the Owenduff River in the wild boglands of northwest Mayo, one of the most isolated medieval sites in Ireland.
history: Owenduff Castle stands in the remote boglands of northwest Mayo near the Owenduff River. The castle dates to the medieval period and its extreme isolation reflects the scattered nature of settlement in this wild region. The Owenduff/Nephin Beg area is now one of Ireland's last remaining areas of intact Atlantic blanket bog.

name: Castlehill House
description: A Georgian mansion near Crossmolina in north Mayo, surrounded by parkland on the shores of Lough Conn.
history: Castlehill House near Crossmolina, County Mayo, is a Georgian country house on the shores of Lough Conn. The house was built by the Palmer family in the 18th century. It later served as an IDA training centre. The estate grounds overlook the scenic Lough Conn, one of Mayo's premier fishing lakes.

name: Errew Abbey
description: Evocative Augustinian ruins on a peninsula in Lough Conn, where medieval monks chose one of Mayo's most beautiful and secluded spots for prayer.
history: Errew Abbey on the shores of Lough Conn in north Mayo was an Augustinian foundation dating to the 13th century. The abbey occupies a beautiful lakeside peninsula. The ruins include a church with fine architectural details. Its remote setting on Lough Conn, surrounded by the mountains of north Mayo, makes it one of the most atmospherically sited monasteries in Ireland.

name: Castlestrange Stone
description: A remarkable La Tene-period decorated stone in the Roscommon countryside—one of only three such carved stones in Ireland, covered in mysterious Iron Age spirals.
history: The Castlestrange Stone near Athleague, County Roscommon, is a rounded granite boulder decorated with flowing La Tene (Celtic Iron Age) spiral carvings dating to around 500 BC-100 AD. It is one of only three such decorated stones known in Ireland (with Turoe Stone and Killycluggin). The purpose of these stones remains debated—possibly ceremonial or marking a sacred site.

name: Fuerty Castle
description: Medieval remains near the village of Fuerty in Roscommon, in an area rich with ecclesiastical heritage dating back to the early Christian period.
history: Fuerty Castle is a medieval fortification near the village of Fuerty, County Roscommon. The village has ancient ecclesiastical connections, with a monastery associated with St Justinian. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by local Roscommon families.

name: Tulsk Castle
description: Castle remains in the historically rich village of Tulsk, gateway to the legendary Rathcroghan complex—ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht.
history: Tulsk Castle in County Roscommon stands in a village of immense historical significance. Nearby Rathcroghan (Cruachan) was the ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht and the traditional entrance to the Celtic Otherworld. The castle dates to the medieval period and a Dominican priory was also founded here. An interpretive centre explains the area's rich archaeological heritage.

name: Dunamon Tower
description: A medieval tower on the River Suck in County Roscommon, now part of a Divine Word Missionaries community centre.
history: Dunamon Tower is a medieval fortification on the River Suck in County Roscommon. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by the Kings of Connacht. Later acquired by the Divine Word Missionaries, the tower and surrounding buildings serve as a religious community centre in a beautiful riverside setting.

name: Ballyfarnon Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Roscommon village of Ballyfarnon, near the shores of Lough Key in one of Ireland's most beautiful lake districts.
history: Ballyfarnon Castle is a medieval fortification near Ballyfarnon, County Roscommon. The village sits near Lough Key, one of Ireland's most scenic lakes, now the centrepiece of Lough Key Forest Park. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local Roscommon families.

name: Ballynakill Castle
description: A tower house in the Roscommon countryside, one of the chain of medieval fortifications that controlled the fertile midlands.
history: Ballynakill Castle is a medieval tower house in County Roscommon. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was part of the network of fortified residences in the Roscommon midlands. The tower served as a local stronghold for one of the region's landowning families.

name: Castleplunket Tower
description: A small medieval tower near the Roscommon village of Castleplunket, associated with the Plunkett family who held lands across the Irish midlands.
history: Castleplunket Tower in County Roscommon is a medieval fortification associated with the Plunkett family. The Plunketts were a significant Anglo-Norman family with extensive holdings across the Irish midlands. The tower dates to the medieval period and gave its name to the surrounding village.

name: Kilronan Castle
description: A magnificently restored 18th-century castle now operating as a luxury hotel, set in the rolling drumlin landscape of County Roscommon.
history: Kilronan Castle near Ballyfarnon was originally built in the 18th century by the Tenison family. After falling into disrepair, the castle was extensively restored and reopened as a luxury hotel in 2006. The estate sits in beautiful parkland near Lough Meelagh and offers a blend of period grandeur and modern comfort.

name: Glinsk Castle
description: A striking fortified house on the Galway-Roscommon border, its ornate facade an unusually refined piece of 17th-century architecture for the Irish west.
history: Glinsk Castle on the Galway-Roscommon border was built around 1630 by the Burke family. The castle is notable for its ornate facade with carved figures and decorative stonework—unusual sophistication for a building on the western frontier. The castle was confiscated during the Cromwellian settlement and fell into disrepair.

name: Templehouse Castle
description: A romantic lakeside estate in County Sligo, where a medieval Knights Templar castle stands beside a Georgian mansion on the shores of Templehouse Lake.
history: Templehouse near Ballymote, County Sligo, takes its name from a Knights Templar preceptory established here in the 12th century. The estate has been home to the Perceval family since the 17th century. The current Georgian house overlooks Templehouse Lake, with the ruins of the medieval castle nearby. The house operates as a guesthouse.

name: Markree Castle
description: Sligo's finest country house hotel, a magnificent Gothic Revival castle set in an ancient demesne along the banks of the River Unshin.
history: Markree Castle near Collooney, County Sligo, has been the seat of the Cooper family since 1663. The current Gothic Revival castle was substantially remodelled in the 1830s by architect Francis Johnston. The castle features a remarkable Great Hall and sits in 500 acres of parkland. It now operates as a luxury castle hotel.

name: Ardtermon Castle
description: A handsome fortified house on the wild Sligo coast near Mullaghmore, overlooking the Atlantic rollers of Donegal Bay.
history: Ardtermon Castle near Mullaghmore in County Sligo is a 17th-century fortified house in a dramatic coastal setting. The castle has been carefully maintained and restored. Its position on the Sligo coast, with views across Donegal Bay to the mountains of Donegal, makes it one of the most attractively sited historic houses in the northwest.

name: Lissadell House
description: The great Sligo house of the Gore-Booth sisters, immortalised by W.B. Yeats—a Greek Revival mansion where poetry, rebellion, and aristocratic idealism collided.
history: Lissadell House near Drumcliff, County Sligo, was home to the Gore-Booth family. Sisters Constance (later Countess Markievicz, the first woman elected to Westminster) and Eva (poet and suffragist) were immortalised by Yeats: 'The light of evening, Lissadell, Great windows open to the south.' The 1830s Greek Revival house has been restored and is open to visitors.

name: Kilglass Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Sligo countryside near Lough Gara, in a landscape rich with crannogs and ancient lake dwellings.
history: Kilglass Castle is a medieval tower house in County Sligo near Lough Gara. The lake area is renowned for its extraordinary concentration of crannogs (ancient lake dwellings), with over 350 identified. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Banada Abbey
description: Well-preserved Augustinian friary ruins in the south Sligo countryside, featuring fine medieval architecture and a peaceful rural setting.
history: Banada Abbey in County Sligo was an Augustinian priory founded in the 15th century. The well-preserved ruins include a church with fine window tracery and other medieval architectural details. The abbey sits in a quiet rural setting in south Sligo, near the Ox Mountains.

name: Castlebaldwin
description: A small fortification in the Sligo village of Castlebaldwin, perched above the scenic Curlew Pass between Sligo and Roscommon.
history: Castlebaldwin in County Sligo takes its name from a medieval castle associated with the Baldwin family. The village sits near the Curlew Pass, a historically strategic defile between Sligo and Roscommon that was the site of several battles. The ruins are modest but the location is historically significant.

name: Collooney Castle
description: A modest tower in the Sligo town of Collooney, site of a significant 1798 battle where Franco-Irish forces defeated the British.
history: Collooney Castle is a medieval tower in County Sligo. The town is famous for the 1798 Battle of Collooney, where Bartholomew Teeling's charge during General Humbert's French expedition secured a rebel victory. A monument to Teeling stands in the town. The castle predates these events by centuries.

name: Skreen Castle
description: Ruins near the ancient ecclesiastical site of Skreen in County Sligo, where an early Christian monastery and medieval church preserve layers of sacred history.
history: Skreen in County Sligo has deep ecclesiastical roots, with monastic origins possibly dating to the 6th century. The castle remains and medieval church stand near the site of this early Christian foundation. The area was important in the spread of Christianity in the northwest and retains its heritage character.

name: Enniscrone Castle
description: Coastal castle remains in the Sligo seaside town famous for its seaweed baths, where Atlantic breakers crash against a long golden strand.
history: Enniscrone (Inishcrone) Castle in County Sligo stands near the town's famous seaweed baths and long sandy beach. The castle dates to the medieval period and guarded the coastline. Enniscrone is renowned for its traditional seaweed bathing experience, where visitors soak in hot seawater baths filled with fresh seaweed.

name: Carrowmore Megalithic Cemetery
description: One of the largest and oldest collections of megalithic tombs in Europe, a haunting Stone Age landscape overlooked by the summit cairn of Knocknarea.
history: Carrowmore near Sligo town contains over 30 passage tombs dating to around 3500 BC, making it one of Europe's oldest and largest megalithic cemeteries. The tombs predate Newgrange by several centuries. The site is dominated by views of Knocknarea mountain, crowned by Queen Maeve's cairn. W.B. Yeats drew deep inspiration from this ancient landscape.

name: Drumcliff Tower
description: A round tower stump and high cross at the burial place of W.B. Yeats, beneath the dramatic limestone cliff of Benbulben.
history: Drumcliff in County Sligo was the site of a 6th-century monastery founded by St Columcille. The remains include a round tower stump and a fine 11th-century high cross with biblical scenes. W.B. Yeats is buried in the churchyard here, his epitaph reading 'Cast a cold eye on life, on death. Horseman, pass by!' The dramatic backdrop of Benbulben mountain completes the scene.

name: Aclare Castle
description: A medieval tower in the south Sligo village of Aclare, in the shadow of the Ox Mountains.
history: Aclare Castle is a medieval tower house in County Sligo near the Ox Mountains. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold. The village of Aclare sits in the foothills between the Ox Mountains and the Curlew Hills.

name: Dromahair Castle
description: Ruins of an O'Rourke stronghold in the picturesque Leitrim village of Dromahair, where the legend of Devorgilla and her elopement changed Irish history.
history: Dromahair Castle was the principal stronghold of the O'Rourke clan, Kings of Breifne. The village is associated with the legendary elopement of Devorgilla O'Rourke with Dermot MacMurrough in 1152, which ultimately led MacMurrough to invite the Normans to Ireland—changing the course of Irish history forever. Nearby Creevelea Abbey adds to the heritage.

name: Drumshanbo Castle
description: Castle remains in the Leitrim lakeland town of Drumshanbo, gateway to the Shannon-Erne Waterway and the wild Leitrim uplands.
history: Drumshanbo Castle in County Leitrim stands in the town at the southern end of Lough Allen. The castle dates to the medieval period. Drumshanbo is now known as the home of Drumshanbo Gunpowder Irish Gin and sits at the junction of the Shannon-Erne Waterway and Lough Allen.

name: Carrick-on-Shannon Castle
description: Scant medieval remains in the charming Leitrim county town, now better known as Ireland's boating capital on the Shannon.
history: Carrick-on-Shannon, the county town of Leitrim, had a medieval castle guarding the Shannon crossing. Little remains of the original fortification, but the town has developed into a major Shannon boating and tourism centre. The tiny Costello Chapel, one of the smallest churches in Europe, is a notable attraction.

name: Leitrim Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of the castle that gave County Leitrim its name, near the banks of the Shannon in Ireland's least populated county.
history: Leitrim Castle, from which the county takes its name, stood near the village of Leitrim on the Shannon. The castle was an O'Rourke stronghold. County Leitrim remains Ireland's least populated county, preserving a rural character that has largely disappeared elsewhere. The castle site is of primarily historical rather than architectural interest.

name: Fenagh Abbey
description: Early Christian and medieval monastic ruins in the quiet Leitrim village of Fenagh, where ancient heritage centres preserve local history.
history: Fenagh Abbey in County Leitrim has monastic origins dating to the 6th century, associated with St Caillin. The surviving ruins include medieval church buildings. A local heritage centre preserves the history of this quiet village, which was once an important ecclesiastical site in the northwest.

name: Ballinagleragh Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Leitrim lakelands, surrounded by the drumlins and small lakes that give this under-visited county its distinctive character.
history: Ballinagleragh Castle is a medieval fortification in County Leitrim. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the drumlin landscape typical of this part of Ireland. Leitrim's network of small lakes and hills creates a distinctive and peaceful rural landscape.

name: Jamestown Castle
description: Remains of a 17th-century Cromwellian fortification at a strategic Shannon crossing in County Leitrim.
history: Jamestown in County Leitrim was fortified in the 17th century as a strategic crossing point of the Shannon. The town was named after King James I and served as a garrison town. The fortifications were strengthened during the Cromwellian and Williamite periods. The town gate and some fortification elements survive.

name: Newtown Gore Castle
description: A tower in the Leitrim countryside near Newtowngore, in the quiet border country between Cavan and Leitrim.
history: Newtown Gore Castle is a medieval fortification near Newtowngore in County Leitrim. The castle stands in the border country between Cavan and Leitrim, an area of drumlins, small lakes, and quiet rural roads. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Mohill Castle
description: Castle remains in the Leitrim market town of Mohill, associated with the Leitrim family and the turbulent history of the northwest.
history: Mohill Castle in County Leitrim stands in the county's second-largest town. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with various local families. Mohill is notable as the home of Turlough O'Carolan (1670-1738), the legendary blind Irish harpist and composer, Ireland's national composer.

name: Drumlease Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the north Leitrim landscape, amid the lakes and mountains that inspired some of Ireland's greatest literary works.
history: Drumlease Castle is a medieval fortification in north County Leitrim. The area's lake-studded landscape inspired W.B. Yeats' famous poem 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree.' The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the O'Rourke territory of Breifne.

name: Kiltyclogher Castle
description: Ruins in the border village of Kiltyclogher in north Leitrim, birthplace of the folk hero and IRA leader Sean MacDiarmada.
history: Kiltyclogher in north Leitrim has castle remains dating to the medieval period. The village is best known as the birthplace of Sean MacDiarmada, one of the seven signatories of the 1916 Proclamation who was executed after the Easter Rising. A heritage centre commemorates his life.

name: Killeen Castle
description: A magnificent Anglo-Norman castle in the Meath countryside, seat of the Plunkett Earls of Fingall for over 800 years, now a prestigious golf resort.
history: Killeen Castle near Dunsany, County Meath, was the seat of the Plunkett family, Earls of Fingall, for over 800 years. The castle was remodelled in the Gothic Revival style by James Shiel and Francis Johnston in the early 19th century. The estate now operates as a luxury golf resort, having hosted the 2011 Solheim Cup.

name: Summerhill Castle
description: Scant remains of a grand Palladian mansion in the Meath countryside, once considered the finest house in Ireland before its destruction.
history: Summerhill House in County Meath was built in the 1730s for Hercules Langford Rowley and was considered the finest Palladian mansion in Ireland. The house was burned in 1921 during the War of Independence and demolished in 1965. Only fragments of this once-magnificent building survive, making it one of Ireland's greatest architectural losses.

name: Balrath Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Meath countryside, part of the dense network of fortified residences that protected the English Pale.
history: Balrath Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. Built in the 15th century, it was part of the network of tower houses that defended the English Pale around Dublin. The tower stands in the rich agricultural land of the Boyne Valley region.

name: Rathmore Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath hinterland, one of many medieval strongholds in the county that boasts Ireland's richest archaeological heritage.
history: Rathmore Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the defensive infrastructure of the Pale. Meath's extraordinary archaeological wealth, from Newgrange to medieval tower houses, makes it Ireland's premier heritage county.

name: Rataine Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Meath countryside near the Boyne Valley, Ireland's most archaeologically significant landscape.
history: Rataine Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near the Boyne Valley. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the extraordinarily rich heritage landscape of the Boyne Valley, which includes Newgrange, Knowth, and Dowth passage tombs.

name: Ardmulchan Castle
description: Medieval ruins on the banks of the Boyne, where the famous river winds through a landscape layered with five millennia of human history.
history: Ardmulchan Castle stands on the banks of the River Boyne in County Meath. The medieval castle and nearby church ruins occupy a picturesque riverside position. The Boyne Valley is one of the most historically significant landscapes in Europe, with heritage stretching from Neolithic passage tombs to the 1690 Battle of the Boyne.

name: Liscartan Castle
description: A tower house near the Meath village of Liscartan, in the fertile heartland of the Pale where Anglo-Norman culture took deepest root.
history: Liscartan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the dense network of fortified residences in the Pale. The area was firmly within the English sphere of influence and the tower houses here reflect the settled agricultural prosperity of the region.

name: Kilbeggan Castle
description: Medieval remains in the Westmeath town of Kilbeggan, home to the oldest licensed distillery in Ireland where whiskey has been made since 1757.
history: Kilbeggan Castle is a medieval fortification in County Westmeath. The town is most famous for Kilbeggan Distillery, established in 1757 and the oldest licensed distillery in Ireland. The castle dates to the medieval period and reflects the town's long history as a settlement on the route between Dublin and the west.

name: Castletown Geoghegan
description: Castle remains in a small Westmeath village bearing the name of the Gaelic MacGeoghegan clan who ruled this territory for centuries.
history: Castletown Geoghegan in County Westmeath takes its name from the MacGeoghegan clan who were lords of this territory in the Irish midlands. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a MacGeoghegan stronghold. The clan produced notable poets, historians, and soldiers over the centuries.

name: Clonyn Castle
description: A Victorian castellated mansion near Delvin, Westmeath, combining medieval tower house origins with 19th-century Gothic Revival grandeur.
history: Clonyn Castle near Delvin, County Westmeath, evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial Victorian castellated mansion. The estate was associated with the Greville-Nugent family. The castle combines medieval and Victorian elements in its architecture. The building has been used for various purposes over the years.

name: Multyfarnham Friary
description: A beautifully maintained Franciscan friary in the Westmeath countryside, remarkably still in active religious use after nearly 600 years.
history: Multyfarnham Friary in County Westmeath was founded in 1268 and has been in continuous Franciscan use for centuries, making it one of the longest continuously inhabited religious houses in Ireland. The friary features a fine medieval church and well-maintained grounds with Stations of the Cross set in a life-size Calvary scene.

name: Caulfield Castle
description: Modest castle remains in the Westmeath countryside, associated with the planter families who transformed the midlands in the 17th century.
history: Caulfield Castle in County Westmeath is associated with the Caulfield family, who were granted lands during the plantation period. The castle dates to the 17th century and reflects the transformation of landownership in the Irish midlands during this period.

name: Tyrellspass Castle
description: A picturesque tower house in one of Ireland's prettiest planned villages, where a crescent of Georgian houses curves around a village green.
history: Tyrellspass Castle in County Westmeath stands in one of Ireland's most attractive planned estate villages. The castle was built by the Tyrrell family in the 15th century and later passed to the Belvedere family. The village's crescent of Georgian houses around a green was planned by the Earls of Belvedere, creating an unusually elegant midlands townscape.

name: Rattin Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Westmeath countryside, part of the patchwork of Gaelic and Anglo-Norman fortifications across the midlands.
history: Rattin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortified residences in the midlands. The area was contested between Gaelic and Anglo-Norman families throughout the medieval period.

name: Moydrum Castle
description: A grand but roofless Victorian Gothic ruin near Athlone, its dramatic shell standing amid parkland in the Westmeath countryside.
history: Moydrum Castle near Athlone was the seat of the Handcock family, Lords Castlemaine. The Gothic Revival castle was burned during the Civil War in 1921 and stands as an impressive shell. The ruin's scale and decorative details, including fine window tracery, make it one of the more striking burned-out Big Houses of the midlands.

name: Castle Forbes
description: The ancestral seat of the Forbes family in County Longford, a grand Georgian house surrounded by extensive parkland.
history: Castle Forbes near Newtownforbes, County Longford, is the seat of the Forbes family, Earls of Granard. The Georgian mansion is set in extensive parkland. The Forbes family has been associated with this area since the plantation period. The estate remains in private hands and is not generally open to the public.

name: Abbeyshrule Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins beside the Royal Canal in the quiet Longford countryside, where medieval monks lived in this watery, pastoral landscape.
history: Abbeyshrule Abbey in County Longford was a Cistercian foundation established in 1200. The abbey ruins stand beside the Royal Canal. The site includes the remains of a church, cloister, and domestic buildings. Abbeyshrule is also home to a small airfield, one of Ireland's oldest, established in 1955.

name: Castlecor House
description: Ruins of a grand house in the Longford countryside, a melancholy monument to the vanished world of the Anglo-Irish gentry.
history: Castlecor House in County Longford was a substantial country house that fell into ruin during the 20th century. The house was associated with local landowning families and represents the decline of the Big House tradition in rural Ireland. The ruins stand in a pastoral landscape typical of the Longford midlands.

name: Ballymahon Castle
description: Castle remains in the Longford market town of Ballymahon, now better known as the nearest town to Longford's Center Parcs holiday resort.
history: Ballymahon Castle in County Longford stands in the town on the River Inny. The castle dates to the medieval period. The town gained new prominence with the opening of Center Parcs Longford Forest nearby, Ireland's first Center Parcs resort, in 2019.

name: Ardagh Castle
description: Heritage remains in the Longford village of Ardagh, where the famous Ardagh Chalice—Ireland's greatest metalwork treasure—was discovered.
history: Ardagh in County Longford is most famous as the find spot of the Ardagh Chalice, discovered in 1868 and now one of the finest examples of Celtic metalwork in the National Museum of Ireland. The village has medieval castle remains and retains a quiet, heritage-rich character.

name: Clondra Castle
description: A modest fortification at the junction of the Royal Canal and the Shannon, a strategic point in Ireland's great inland waterway system.
history: Clondra (also known as Cloondara) in County Longford sits at the junction of the Royal Canal and the River Shannon. The castle or fortification at this point reflects the strategic importance of this waterway junction. The village was a significant inland port during the canal era.

name: Edgeworthstown Castle
description: Heritage remains in the town associated with Maria Edgeworth, the pioneering novelist whose work anticipated Jane Austen and influenced Walter Scott.
history: Edgeworthstown (now officially Mostrim) in County Longford was the home of the Edgeworth family. Maria Edgeworth (1768-1849) was a pioneering novelist whose 'Castle Rackrent' (1800) was one of the first regional novels in English. Her father Richard Lovell Edgeworth was a noted inventor and educationalist. The family home has been restored as a heritage centre.

name: Shinrone Castle
description: Castle remains in the Offaly village of Shinrone, in the flat bogland of the Irish midlands where ancient trackways cross the peat.
history: Shinrone Castle in County Offaly dates to the medieval period. The village sits in the midland boglands of Offaly, an area rich in archaeological discoveries preserved in the peat. The castle was associated with local families in the border zone between Munster and Leinster.

name: Frankford Castle
description: A tower in the Offaly village of Frankford (Kilcormac area), in the heart of Ireland's bogland heritage landscape.
history: Frankford Castle in County Offaly dates to the medieval or early modern period. The area is surrounded by extensive raised bogs that have preserved remarkable archaeological finds. The castle reflects the history of settlement in this midland region.

name: Ballyboy Castle
description: A medieval tower house near the Offaly village of Ballyboy, in the rolling hills between the boglands and the Slieve Bloom Mountains.
history: Ballyboy Castle in County Offaly is a medieval tower house near the foothills of the Slieve Bloom Mountains. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was associated with local families in this border area between the Irish midlands and the uplands of the Slieve Blooms.

name: Seir Kieran Monastery
description: One of Ireland's oldest monastic sites, founded before St Patrick arrived, where ancient crosses and gravestones span 1,500 years of Christian worship.
history: Seir Kieran near Clareen in County Offaly is one of Ireland's oldest monastic sites, traditionally founded by St Ciaran of Saigir in the 5th century—possibly before St Patrick's mission. The site preserves early cross slabs, high cross fragments, and medieval church ruins. It remains an active graveyard, creating an unbroken link to the earliest days of Irish Christianity.

name: Durrow Abbey
description: The monastery where the magnificent Book of Durrow was created—one of Ireland's earliest illuminated manuscripts and a masterwork of Celtic art.
history: Durrow Abbey in County Offaly was founded by St Columcille in the 6th century. The monastery produced the Book of Durrow (c.650-700 AD), one of the earliest and most beautiful illuminated gospel books, now in Trinity College Dublin. The site preserves a fine 10th-century high cross and the remains of the medieval church. The surrounding parkland was later part of a country estate.

name: Kilcoursey Castle
description: A tower house in the Offaly midlands, one of the many fortified residences that dot this quietly beautiful landscape of bogs and farmland.
history: Kilcoursey Castle is a medieval tower house in County Offaly. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was associated with local landowning families. The midlands of Offaly, though often overlooked, contain numerous medieval fortifications and heritage sites.

name: Lemenaghan Castle
description: Medieval remains near an ancient monastic site in the Offaly boglands, where the Shannon Callows create one of Ireland's most important wetland habitats.
history: Lemenaghan Castle in County Offaly stands near the ancient monastery of Lemanaghan, associated with St Manchan. The area is on the edge of the Shannon Callows, extensive seasonal wetlands along the Shannon that support rare wildlife. The medieval castle and nearby monastic remains create a rich heritage landscape.

name: Emo Court
description: A magnificent neoclassical mansion designed by James Gandon, set in sweeping parkland and gardens—one of Ireland's finest architectural treasures.
history: Emo Court in County Laois was designed in 1790 by James Gandon (architect of Dublin's Custom House) for the 1st Earl of Portarlington. The neoclassical house features a magnificent rotunda and is surrounded by beautiful parkland with a lake and woodland walks. Restored by Jesuits and later the state, it is now managed by the OPW and open to visitors.

name: Timahoe Round Tower
description: One of Ireland's finest round towers, its remarkable Romanesque doorway featuring human faces and interlaced patterns of extraordinary beauty.
history: The Round Tower at Timahoe, County Laois, dates to the 12th century and stands 29 metres tall. Its most remarkable feature is the ornate Romanesque doorway at first-floor level, decorated with human faces, animal heads, and interlaced patterns. The tower is associated with a monastery founded by St Mochua in the 6th century. It is considered one of the finest round towers in Ireland.

name: Killeshin Church
description: A Romanesque church doorway of extraordinary richness in the Laois countryside—one of Ireland's finest examples of 12th-century carved stonework.
history: Killeshin Church in County Laois preserves a magnificent Romanesque doorway dating to the 12th century. The doorway features multiple orders of richly carved chevron, interlace, and figural decoration. The church was part of a monastery founded by St Comgan. The doorway is considered one of the finest examples of Irish Romanesque architecture.

name: Shaen Castle
description: Gothic Revival castle ruins in the Laois countryside, a dramatic Victorian shell surrounded by woodland and parkland.
history: Shaen Castle in County Laois was a Gothic Revival mansion built by the Fitzgerald family. The castle was destroyed by fire and stands as an impressive ruin in woodland parkland. The estate contains fine mature trees and is accessible via forest walks.

name: Heywood Gardens
description: A hidden gem of formal garden design by Sir Edwin Lutyens, featuring terraces, lakes, and plantings in the gentle Laois landscape.
history: Heywood Gardens near Ballinakill, County Laois, were designed by the great architect Sir Edwin Lutyens with planting by Gertrude Jekyll around 1909. The formal gardens feature terraces, a sunken garden, pergola, and ornamental lake. Though the house was demolished, the gardens have been restored by the OPW and are freely open to visitors.

name: Inch Castle
description: A dramatic O'Doherty tower house on a peninsula jutting into Lough Swilly, one of Donegal's most photogenic castle ruins.
history: Inch Castle on the shores of Lough Swilly, County Donegal, was an O'Doherty stronghold. The castle stands on Inch Island, connected to the mainland by a causeway. It was burned during the O'Doherty Rebellion of 1608 and never repaired. The atmospheric ruins overlooking Lough Swilly make it one of Donegal's most photographed heritage sites.

name: Buncrana Castle
description: O'Doherty castle ruins in the Inishowen resort town of Buncrana, where the shores of Lough Swilly meet the hills of Donegal's largest peninsula.
history: Buncrana Castle (also known as O'Doherty's Keep) stands in the town of Buncrana on the shores of Lough Swilly, County Donegal. Built by the O'Doherty clan, the castle dates to the medieval period. Nearby is the later Buncrana Castle built by John Vaughan in 1718, where Wolfe Tone was briefly held after his capture in 1798.

name: Moross Castle
description: A tower house ruin in County Donegal, standing in the wild landscape of the northwest where mountains meet the Atlantic.
history: Moross Castle is a medieval tower house in County Donegal. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local chieftain families. The Donegal landscape of mountains, lakes, and Atlantic coast provides a dramatic setting for its many historical sites.

name: Drumboe Castle
description: A castellated mansion near Stranorlar in Donegal, with a tragic Civil War history when four anti-Treaty prisoners were executed here in 1923.
history: Drumboe Castle near Stranorlar, County Donegal, is a 19th-century castellated mansion. During the Irish Civil War, four anti-Treaty IRA prisoners were executed by firing squad in the castle grounds in March 1923, a controversial act that shocked the local community. A memorial marks the site. The castle later served various purposes.

name: Na Dunaibh Castle
description: Coastal fortification remains in the Donegal Gaeltacht village of Downings, overlooking the spectacular beaches of Sheephaven Bay.
history: Na Dunaibh (Downings) in County Donegal has castle or fortification remains near the village on Sheephaven Bay. The area is part of the Donegal Gaeltacht and retains strong Irish language traditions. The spectacular beaches and coastal scenery of Sheephaven Bay make this one of Donegal's most beautiful areas.

name: Assaroe Abbey
description: Fragmentary Cistercian abbey ruins near Ballyshannon, beside the falls where the River Erne once tumbled into Donegal Bay.
history: Assaroe Abbey near Ballyshannon, County Donegal, was a Cistercian monastery founded in the 12th century. The abbey sits near where the River Erne's waterfalls (now harnessed for hydroelectric power) once provided one of Ireland's most spectacular natural scenes. The abbey ruins are fragmentary but the site retains historical significance in this ancient crossing point.

name: Knockmealdown Abbey
description: Monastic remains in the shadow of the Knockmealdown Mountains, on the Waterford-Tipperary border.
history: Knockmealdown Abbey is a monastic site in the foothills of the Knockmealdown Mountains on the Waterford-Tipperary border. The mountains themselves are named from the Irish 'Cnoc Maoldomhnaigh.' The abbey dates to the medieval period and sits in a scenic upland landscape.

name: Ballyquin Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the west Waterford countryside, near the Blackwater River and the scenic Dromana Gate.
history: Ballyquin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford near the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the Waterford countryside. The nearby Dromana Gate, a spectacular Hindu-Gothic folly, is one of Ireland's most unusual architectural features.

name: Knocktopher Abbey
description: Medieval monastic ruins in the pretty south Kilkenny village of Knocktopher, along the ancient pilgrim route through the Nore Valley.
history: Knocktopher Abbey in County Kilkenny was a Carmelite friary founded in the 13th century. The village sits on the ancient road through the Nore Valley. The abbey ruins include church remains with medieval architectural details. The village preserves its heritage character with an attractive streetscape.

name: Inistioge Castle
description: Castle remains in one of Ireland's most picturesque villages, where a graceful stone bridge spans the River Nore beneath wooded hillsides.
history: Inistioge in County Kilkenny is regularly voted one of Ireland's prettiest villages. The medieval castle and Augustinian priory ruins complement the village's beautiful setting on the River Nore. The village has been used as a filming location numerous times due to its photogenic character. A restored 18th-century bridge spans the river.

name: Grennan Castle
description: An imposing medieval fortress perched above the River Nore near Thomastown, commanding sweeping views across the Kilkenny countryside.
history: Grennan Castle near Thomastown in County Kilkenny is a substantial medieval castle overlooking the River Nore. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the Butler family. Its elevated position above the river provides commanding views of the surrounding countryside and the heritage town of Thomastown.

name: Powerscourt Castle
description: Not the famous Wicklow estate, but the less-known Kilkenny medieval castle near the town of Piltown—a quieter footnote in the Butler saga.
history: Powerscourt Castle near Piltown in County Kilkenny is a medieval fortification associated with the Butler and Power families. The castle dates to the medieval period and should not be confused with the famous Powerscourt Estate in County Wicklow. It was part of the network of strongholds in south Kilkenny.

name: Russborough House
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion stretching 210 metres across the Wicklow landscape—the longest house frontage in Ireland, housing a world-class art collection.
history: Russborough House near Blessington was built 1741-1755 for Joseph Leeson, 1st Earl of Milltown, by architect Richard Cassels. Its 210-metre frontage makes it the longest house in Ireland. The house contains the Beit Art Collection, which was famously robbed multiple times including by Martin Cahill ('The General') in 1986. Now managed by the Alfred Beit Foundation.

name: Cloyne Round Tower
description: A fine early Christian round tower in the east Cork cathedral town of Cloyne, once seat of the famous philosopher Bishop George Berkeley.
history: The Round Tower at Cloyne, County Cork, stands beside the medieval cathedral and dates to the 10th-11th century. Cloyne was an important early Christian monastic settlement. The town's most famous bishop was George Berkeley (1685-1753), the philosopher after whom Berkeley, California, is named. The tower is well-preserved and forms part of an attractive heritage streetscape.

name: Kilcolman Castle
description: The haunting ruin where Edmund Spenser wrote 'The Faerie Queene'—one of the greatest works in the English language, composed in the wilds of Cork.
history: Kilcolman Castle near Doneraile in north Cork was the home of the poet Edmund Spenser, who wrote much of 'The Faerie Queene' here in the 1580s-90s. Spenser received the castle as part of the Munster Plantation. The castle was burned in the 1598 Munster Rebellion and Spenser fled to London, where he died shortly after. The evocative ruins overlook a small lake.

name: Castletownroche Castle
description: Castle remains in the pretty Cork village of Castletownroche, strategically sited above the River Awbeg in the Blackwater Valley.
history: Castletownroche Castle in County Cork stands above the River Awbeg (a tributary of the Blackwater) in the village of Castletownroche. The castle was built by the Roche family, a powerful Anglo-Norman dynasty in Cork. The nearby Annes Grove Gardens are a celebrated woodland garden open to visitors.

name: Ightermurragh Castle
description: A fine tower house near the east Cork coast, standing amid the farmland between Castlemartyr and Ladysbridge.
history: Ightermurragh Castle in east Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and is one of several tower houses in the east Cork countryside. The area is rich in heritage, with the nearby medieval town of Cloyne and various Anglo-Norman castles and churches.

name: Kilbrittain Castle
description: A dramatic medieval tower house perched above the village of Kilbrittain in west Cork, with views stretching to the Celtic Sea.
history: Kilbrittain Castle in west Cork is a substantial tower house dating to the medieval period. The castle was associated with the de Courcey family and later the McCarthy clan. It commands an elevated position above the village with views toward the sea. The castle has been partially restored.

name: Rostellan Castle
description: Atmospheric ruins of an 18th-century mansion on the shores of Cork Harbour, its grounds once the most celebrated gardens in Munster.
history: Rostellan Castle near Whitegate in east Cork was an elaborate 18th-century mansion on the shores of Cork Harbour. The estate was famous for its extensive gardens and ornamental grounds. The house fell into ruin in the 20th century. The atmospheric shell stands near the shores of the harbour.

name: Castle Salem
description: A rare intact Elizabethan fortified house in West Cork, privately restored and offering an authentic glimpse into 16th-century Irish life.
history: Castle Salem near Rosscarbery in West Cork is an Elizabethan fortified house dating to the late 16th century. The building was constructed during the Munster Plantation and retains many original features. It has been carefully restored by its owners and occasionally opens to visitors, offering a rare experience of an authentic Elizabethan interior in Ireland.

name: Castlemartyr Castle
description: Medieval castle ruins in the east Cork village of Castlemartyr, where a Desmond stronghold now forms the centrepiece of a luxury resort.
history: Castlemartyr Castle in east Cork was a FitzGerald (Desmond) stronghold dating to the medieval period. The castle and estate were later developed into a grand country house. The entire estate was converted into the Castlemartyr Resort, a luxury hotel. The medieval ruins are preserved within the resort grounds.

name: Doon Castle (Clare)
description: A lakeside tower house on the shores of Doon Lough in Clare, its quiet waters reflecting medieval walls against a backdrop of the Burren hills.
history: Doon Castle in County Clare stands on or near the shores of Doon Lough. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local Clare families. The lakeside setting in the Clare countryside provides an atmospheric backdrop for the ruins.

name: Killone Abbey
description: Beautifully situated Augustinian nunnery ruins on the shores of Killone Lake near Ennis, one of the few medieval nunneries to survive in Ireland.
history: Killone Abbey near Ennis, County Clare, was an Augustinian nunnery founded in the 12th century by Donal Mor O'Brien, King of Thomond. It is one of the few surviving medieval nunneries in Ireland. The ruins are beautifully situated on the shores of Killone Lake and include a church with fine Romanesque details.

name: Termon Castle
