description: A tower house in the Clare countryside, named for the ancient Irish concept of church sanctuary lands that once surrounded ecclesiastical sites.
history: Termon Castle in County Clare takes its name from 'termon' (sanctuary), indicating its location on former ecclesiastical lands. The castle dates to the medieval period and is one of Clare's many tower houses. The concept of termon lands—sanctuary territories surrounding churches and monasteries—was an important feature of early Irish law.

name: Castle Taylor
description: Ruins of a grand estate near Ardrahan in south Galway, once home to a prominent landowning family connected to Lady Gregory and the Irish Literary Revival.
history: Castle Taylor near Ardrahan, County Galway, was the seat of the Taylor family. The estate was connected to the circle of Lady Augusta Gregory, whose nearby Coole Park was the epicentre of the Irish Literary Revival. W.B. Yeats, J.M. Synge, and other literary figures visited the area. The castle fell into ruin during the 20th century.

name: Kilcogan Castle
description: A tower house near the south Galway coast, in the flat limestone landscape between Galway Bay and the Burren.
history: Kilcogan Castle is a medieval tower house in south County Galway near the coast. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the region between Galway city and the Burren. The area's flat limestone terrain is characteristic of the south Galway coast.

name: Ardfry Castle
description: Ruined Georgian mansion on the shores of Galway Bay, once seat of the Blake family and later a college, now a haunting shell overlooking the Atlantic.
history: Ardfry Castle near Oranmore, County Galway, was the seat of a branch of the Blake family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The Georgian house overlooked Galway Bay. It later served as a university college before falling into ruin. The atmospheric shell stands in grounds with fine sea views.

name: Castle Hackett
description: A dramatic tower house crowning the summit of a rocky hill in south Galway, associated with the powerful Hackett family of medieval Connacht.
history: Castle Hackett in County Galway stands on a dramatic hilltop with commanding views. The castle was held by the Hackett family and later by the Kirwan family. The hilltop location on Castle Hackett Hill (Knockma) is associated in legend with the fairy king Finvarra. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Ballylee Castle
description: Thoor Ballylee—W.B. Yeats' beloved tower house, where Ireland's greatest poet wrote some of his finest work amid the wild beauty of south Galway.
history: Thoor Ballylee near Gort, County Galway, is a 16th-century Norman tower house purchased by W.B. Yeats in 1917 for £35. He restored it and lived here intermittently until 1929, writing masterworks including 'The Tower' and 'The Winding Stair.' The tower has been restored as a museum and literary shrine, though access has varied over the years.

name: Aghadoe Castle
description: Medieval ruins overlooking the glorious Lakes of Killarney, combining a cathedral, round tower, and castle in one of Kerry's most scenic viewpoints.
history: Aghadoe near Killarney, County Kerry, is a remarkable heritage site combining a 12th-century cathedral, round tower stump, and 13th-century castle ruin. The site commands one of the finest views of the Lakes of Killarney and the surrounding mountains. The cathedral was an important medieval see and the castle a Norman fortification.

name: Rattoo Round Tower
description: A fine round tower and medieval church ruin in north Kerry, one of the best-preserved early Christian monuments in Munster.
history: Rattoo Round Tower near Ballyduff in County Kerry stands 28 metres tall and dates to the 11th or 12th century. It is one of the best-preserved round towers in Munster. The adjacent church ruins and graveyard complete an atmospheric early Christian site. The tower retains its conical cap and the characteristic raised doorway.

name: Kilcash Castle
description: A magnificent Butler tower house at the foot of Slievenamon, immortalised in the haunting Irish lament 'Cill Chais'—one of the great poems in the Irish language.
history: Kilcash Castle in County Tipperary was a principal seat of the Butler family beneath Slievenamon mountain. The castle dates to the 15th century. It is the subject of the famous Irish poem 'Cill Chais' ('Kilcash'), lamenting the decay of the Gaelic world: 'What shall we do for timber? The last of the woods is down.' The evocative ruins stand in a sublime setting.

name: Farney Castle
description: A restored medieval tower house in County Tipperary, its round corners and fine stonework making it a distinctive landmark in the Golden Vale.
history: Farney Castle in County Tipperary is a medieval tower house with distinctive round corner towers. The castle has been restored and is in private ownership. Its unusual architectural features, including the round turrets, distinguish it from the many rectangular tower houses in the region.

name: Castletown Cox
description: An exquisite Palladian villa in the Kilkenny countryside, considered one of Ireland's most perfectly proportioned country houses.
history: Castletown Cox near Piltown, County Kilkenny, was designed by the great architect Davis Ducart for Michael Cox, Archbishop of Cashel, around 1767. The house is considered one of the finest Palladian villas in Ireland, with perfectly proportioned interiors. It remains in private hands and is not generally open to the public.

name: Ballysaggartmore Towers
description: Fantastical Gothic entrance lodges in the Waterford woodland, built to impress but bankrupting their owner before the mansion they heralded was ever built.
history: The Ballysaggartmore Towers near Lismore, County Waterford, are spectacular Gothic Revival gate lodges built around 1834 by Arthur Kiely-Usher. He spent so lavishly on these ornamental entrance lodges that he ran out of money before building the grand house they were meant to announce. The extravagant follies are now set in a public forest park.

name: Tacumshane Windmill
description: Ireland's only surviving complete thatched windmill, a charming 17th-century survivor standing in the flat Wexford farmland near the coast.
history: Tacumshane Windmill in County Wexford is Ireland's only complete surviving windmill, dating to 1846. The thatched windmill retains its sails and milling machinery. It stands in the flat agricultural landscape of south Wexford near Lady's Island, a traditional pilgrimage site.

name: Hook Lighthouse
description: The oldest operational lighthouse in the world, guiding mariners past the wild Wexford headland for over 800 years.
history: Hook Lighthouse on the Hook Peninsula, County Wexford, has been guiding ships for over 800 years, making it the oldest operational lighthouse in the world. The current tower was built by William Marshal around 1210 on a site where monks had tended a warning beacon since the 5th century. The phrase 'by hook or by crook' allegedly refers to the approaches to Waterford Harbour.

name: Loughcrew Cairns
description: A vast hilltop passage tomb cemetery in the Meath countryside, where Neolithic sun alignments illuminate Stone Age art each equinox.
history: The Loughcrew Cairns (Sliabh na Caillighe) in County Meath comprise over 30 passage tombs dating to around 3300 BC. The main cairn, Cairn T, contains remarkable megalithic art illuminated by the rising sun at the spring and autumn equinoxes. The complex rivals the more famous Boyne Valley sites and offers a more intimate, less crowded experience of Ireland's Neolithic heritage.

name: Hill of Slane
description: A sacred hilltop where St Patrick lit the Paschal Fire in 433 AD, challenging the High King of Tara and igniting the flame of Christianity across Ireland.
history: The Hill of Slane in County Meath is one of Ireland's most sacred sites. According to tradition, St Patrick lit the Paschal Fire here in 433 AD on Easter Eve, in direct defiance of the pagan festival at the Hill of Tara. This symbolic act is seen as the moment Christianity established itself in Ireland. The hill preserves a ruined friary, church, and college.

name: Kells Round Tower
description: A magnificent round tower and high crosses in the Meath town where the world-famous Book of Kells was kept for centuries.
history: The Round Tower at Kells, County Meath, is part of a monastic complex that housed the Book of Kells (now in Trinity College Dublin) for centuries after the monks fled Viking raids on Iona in the 9th century. The site also preserves several fine high crosses, including the remarkable South Cross with its biblical scenes. The tower and crosses together form one of Ireland's most important early Christian sites.

name: Clonard Castle
description: Remains near the ancient monastic site of Clonard in Meath, once one of Europe's greatest centres of learning in the Dark Ages.
history: Clonard in County Meath was one of the most important monastic sites in early Christian Ireland. Founded by St Finian in the 6th century, the monastery was reputedly attended by 3,000 students and produced the 'Twelve Apostles of Ireland.' The castle dates to the medieval period. Little remains of the original monastery but the site's significance in European intellectual history is immense.

name: Rathnageeragh Castle
description: A tower house in the Carlow countryside near the Barrow Valley, part of the medieval defence network of southeast Leinster.
history: Rathnageeragh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Carlow. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the defensive network in the Barrow Valley region of southeast Leinster. The area was contested between Anglo-Norman and Gaelic families throughout the medieval period.

name: Huntington Castle
description: A stunning 17th-century castle in County Carlow with medieval origins, famous for its Temple of Isis in the dungeon and magnificent gardens.
history: Huntington Castle near Clonegal, County Carlow, was built in 1625 by the Esmondes on the site of a Franciscan abbey. The castle has been continuously lived in by the same family for over 400 years. Its most unusual feature is a Temple of Isis established in the basement in 1976. The castle also features beautiful gardens and is said to be haunted.

name: Altamont Gardens
description: A hidden paradise of woodland gardens and formal plantings in the Carlow countryside, one of Ireland's most beautiful and romantic garden estates.
history: Altamont Gardens near Tullow, County Carlow, is one of Ireland's most beautiful gardens, covering 40 acres of formal gardens, woodland walks, and a lake. The gardens were largely created by Corona North, who tended them for decades. Now managed by the OPW, the gardens feature ancient oaks, rhododendrons, rare plants, and a atmospheric walk to the River Slaney.

name: Aughrim Castle
description: Ruins near the Galway village where the Battle of Aughrim in 1691—the bloodiest battle in Irish history—decided the fate of the Jacobite cause.
history: Aughrim in County Galway was the site of the Battle of Aughrim on 12 July 1691, the bloodiest battle ever fought on Irish soil, with an estimated 7,000 killed. The Williamite victory effectively ended the Jacobite War in Ireland. The castle ruins and an interpretive centre commemorate this pivotal event in Irish and European history.

name: Clonfert Cathedral
description: A tiny cathedral with the most ornate Romanesque doorway in Ireland—a riot of human heads, animal figures, and geometric patterns in ancient stone.
history: Clonfert Cathedral in County Galway preserves the finest Romanesque doorway in Ireland, dating to the 12th century. The extraordinary doorway features six orders of arches decorated with human heads, animal figures, chevrons, and foliage. The monastery was founded by St Brendan the Navigator in 563 AD. Though the cathedral is small, the doorway is an undisputed masterpiece of Irish medieval art.

name: Tuam Cathedral
description: A cathedral city in the heart of Galway, preserving a remarkable Romanesque chancel arch and high cross amid its medieval ecclesiastical heritage.
history: Tuam, County Galway, preserves the Cathedral of the Assumption with its magnificent 12th-century Romanesque chancel arch, one of the finest pieces of Romanesque architecture in Ireland. The town was the seat of the High Kings of Connacht and later an important archbishopric. A fine 12th-century high cross, the Cross of Tuam, also survives.

name: Corcomroe Abbey
description: A jewel of the Burren—a Cistercian abbey whose carved stonework blooms with wildflowers and human faces amid the grey limestone of County Clare.
history: Corcomroe Abbey in the Burren, County Clare, was founded around 1194 by Donal Mor O'Brien, King of Thomond. The Cistercian abbey is renowned for its exquisite stone carvings, which incorporate local flora—the carvings of flowers are so botanically accurate that the species can be identified. The abbey's setting in a Burren valley adds to its extraordinary atmosphere.

name: Kilfenora Cathedral
description: The medieval cathedral of the Burren, famous for its remarkable collection of high crosses and its unique status as a papal diocese.
history: Kilfenora Cathedral in County Clare dates to the 12th century and is famous for its collection of high crosses, including the remarkable Doorty Cross with its bishops' figures. Kilfenora is unique as a diocese technically still under the direct authority of the Pope—the only such parish in Ireland. The Burren Centre in the village explains the geology and heritage of the region.

name: Muckross House
description: A magnificent Victorian mansion at the heart of Killarney National Park, where Queen Victoria's visit in 1861 nearly bankrupted the Herbert family.
history: Muckross House, built 1839-1843 for Henry Arthur Herbert, is a Tudor Revival mansion set in the spectacular scenery of Killarney National Park. Queen Victoria visited in 1861, and the preparations for her visit were so lavish they contributed to the Herbert family's bankruptcy. The house and its traditional farms are now a major visitor attraction managed by the OPW.

name: Derrynane House
description: The ancestral home of Daniel O'Connell, 'The Liberator,' set in the stunning coastal scenery of the Iveragh Peninsula in County Kerry.
history: Derrynane House near Caherdaniel, County Kerry, was the home of Daniel O'Connell (1775-1847), who won Catholic Emancipation in 1829—one of the greatest political achievements of the 19th century. The house is now a museum dedicated to O'Connell's life and the struggle for Catholic rights. The grounds include beautiful gardens and access to Derrynane Beach.

name: Cahergall Stone Fort
description: A superbly preserved Iron Age stone fort on the Ring of Kerry, its massive drystone walls and beehive huts offering a vivid window into prehistoric Irish life.
history: Cahergall Stone Fort near Cahersiveen, County Kerry, is a spectacularly well-preserved cashel (stone ring fort) dating to around 600 AD. The massive drystone walls stand up to 5 metres high, and the fort contains the remains of a rectangular house and a clochán (beehive hut). Cahergall is one of the most impressive accessible stone forts in Ireland.

name: Leacanabuaile Fort
description: A beautifully restored stone ringfort near Cahersiveen, revealing the domestic arrangements of early medieval Irish farm life in remarkable detail.
history: Leacanabuaile Stone Fort near Cahersiveen, County Kerry, was excavated in 1939 and carefully restored. The cashel dates to the 9th-10th century and contained the foundations of houses and a souterrain. The excavation revealed artefacts including bronze pins, iron knives, and spinning whorls, illuminating daily life in early medieval Kerry.

name: Ballinvard Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Waterford countryside, standing amid the rolling farmland of the Suir Valley.
history: Ballinvard Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the fertile Suir Valley. It served as a local stronghold for one of the area's landowning families.

name: Mountlong Castle
description: A substantial fortified house in the Waterford countryside, an unusually well-preserved example of early 17th-century plantation architecture.
history: Mountlong Castle near Dungarvan, County Waterford, is a fortified house dating to the early 17th century plantation period. The substantial building features fine details and represents the transition from medieval castle to Renaissance residence. The castle was associated with the Long family.

name: Ballybeg Priory
description: Well-preserved Augustinian priory ruins near Buttevant in north Cork, with an imposing tower and extensive medieval domestic buildings.
history: Ballybeg Priory near Buttevant, County Cork, was an Augustinian foundation dating to the 13th century. The priory is remarkably well preserved, with substantial remains of the church, tower, domestic ranges, and enclosure walls. It is one of the best-preserved medieval monastic sites in north Cork.

name: Bridgetown Priory
description: Extensive Augustinian priory ruins beside the River Anner in south Tipperary, one of the largest and most atmospheric monastic complexes in the southeast.
history: Bridgetown Priory near Castlewarren in County Kilkenny (bordering Tipperary) was an Augustinian foundation established in the 13th century. The extensive ruins include a large church, cloister, tower, and domestic buildings spread across a riverside site. The priory is one of the largest medieval monastic complexes in the southeast.

name: Ardmore Cathedral
description: An extraordinary assemblage of early Christian treasures on a wild Waterford headland—round tower, cathedral, ogham stones, and cliff-edge holy well.
history: Ardmore, County Waterford, is one of Ireland's oldest Christian sites, associated with St Declan who may have predated St Patrick. The site includes a magnificent round tower, a 12th-century cathedral with remarkable Romanesque arcading depicting biblical scenes, ogham stones, and St Declan's holy well on the cliff edge. The round tower is one of the best preserved in Ireland.

name: Mahon Falls Tower
description: A signal tower near the dramatic Mahon Falls in the Comeragh Mountains, in one of Waterford's most spectacular mountain landscapes.
history: The tower near Mahon Falls in the Comeragh Mountains, County Waterford, is a Napoleonic-era signal tower, part of the coastal defence system built in the early 19th century. The nearby Mahon Falls cascade dramatically down a corrie headwall. The area is popular for hillwalking in the wild Comeragh landscape.

name: Strangely Castle
description: A tower house ruin near Dunmore East in County Waterford, overlooking the dramatic Waterford coastline.
history: Strangely Castle is a medieval tower house near Dunmore East, County Waterford. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the coastal landscape of south Waterford. The tower is in a ruinous state but retains some structural features.

name: Curraghmore House
description: The seat of the Marquis of Waterford—Ireland's largest private demesne, where a magnificent Georgian mansion sits amid 2,500 acres of parkland and ancient woodland.
history: Curraghmore House near Portlaw, County Waterford, is the ancestral seat of the de la Poer family (Marquesses of Waterford) and claims to be the oldest continuously inhabited house in Ireland. The estate covers 2,500 acres. The shell house in the grounds is one of Ireland's finest garden follies. The house opens to visitors on selected dates.

name: Cappoquin Castle
description: A medieval castle overlooking the River Blackwater in one of Waterford's prettiest towns, where the river makes a dramatic turn southward toward the sea.
history: Cappoquin Castle stands above the River Blackwater in the town of Cappoquin, County Waterford. The town sits at the point where the Blackwater makes its famous 90-degree turn southward. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the FitzGerald and Keating families.

name: Woodstown Castle
description: A tower house near the shores of Waterford Harbour, close to where a major Viking settlement was discovered in the 2000s.
history: Woodstown Castle is a medieval tower house near Waterford Harbour. Nearby, one of Ireland's most significant Viking archaeological discoveries was made in the 2000s when road construction revealed an extensive 9th-century Viking camp. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the historically rich landscape around Waterford Harbour.

name: Callan Friary
description: Augustinian friary ruins in the medieval Kilkenny town of Callan, birthplace of the great Irish rebel priest Edmund Ignatius Rice.
history: Callan Friary in County Kilkenny was an Augustinian foundation. The town of Callan is notable as the birthplace of Edmund Ignatius Rice (1762-1844), founder of the Christian Brothers and the Presentation Brothers. The friary ruins and medieval town heritage reflect Callan's importance in medieval Kilkenny.

name: Thomastown Castle
description: Castle remains in the charming Kilkenny town of Thomastown, a medieval riverside settlement that has become a centre for contemporary craft.
history: Thomastown, named after the Norman Thomas FitzAnthony, preserves medieval castle and town wall remains. The town sits on the River Nore and has developed a reputation as a centre for contemporary craft, particularly through the Grennan Mill Craft School and nearby Jerpoint Park. The medieval bridge and streetscape add to its heritage character.

name: Gowran Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the south Kilkenny town of Gowran, where a superb medieval church preserves remarkable carved stone effigies.
history: Gowran in County Kilkenny preserves medieval castle remains and an exceptional medieval church (Collegiate Church of St Mary) with outstanding carved stone tomb effigies of the 14th century. The church is considered one of the finest medieval parish churches in Ireland. Gowran is also known for its racecourse.

name: Tullaherin Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and medieval church ruin in the Kilkenny countryside, near the remarkable medieval village of Kells.
history: Tullaherin Round Tower in County Kilkenny is the stump of an early Christian round tower beside medieval church ruins. The site is near Kells Priory, one of Ireland's largest and most impressive medieval monastic enclosures. The round tower dates to the 9th-10th century and marks an early Christian monastic foundation.

name: Kilfane Church
description: A medieval church in the Kilkenny countryside containing one of Ireland's finest carved medieval effigies—a Norman knight in full armour known as 'The Long Cantwell.'
history: Kilfane Church in County Kilkenny preserves one of Ireland's most remarkable medieval stone effigies: the 'Long Cantwell,' a nearly 2.5-metre carved figure of a Norman knight in chain mail armour dating to the 13th century. The effigy is among the finest surviving examples of medieval funerary sculpture in Ireland.

name: Graiguenamanagh Abbey
description: See Duiske Abbey—this historic Kilkenny town on the River Barrow is one of Ireland's most attractive small towns.
history: Graiguenamanagh on the River Barrow is home to Duiske Abbey, founded in 1204. The town straddles the Kilkenny-Carlow border and is one of Ireland's most attractive riverside settlements. The restored abbey, medieval bridge, and mountain backdrop of Brandon Hill create an exceptionally scenic heritage town.

name: Turoe Stone
description: A superbly carved La Tene Celtic stone in County Galway—one of the finest examples of Iron Age art in Europe, covered in flowing abstract designs.
history: The Turoe Stone near Loughrea, County Galway, is a granite boulder decorated with flowing La Tene (Celtic Iron Age) designs dating to around 300-100 BC. The abstract curvilinear patterns are among the finest examples of Celtic art in Europe. The stone was probably a ceremonial object. It now stands in the grounds of Turoe Pet Farm.

name: Moore Hall
description: A haunting lakeside ruin on the shores of Lough Carra, once home to the Moore family whose most famous son, George Moore, became a pioneering modernist novelist.
history: Moore Hall on the shores of Lough Carra, County Mayo, was home to the Moore family. George Moore (1852-1933) was a pioneering novelist and art critic who influenced the Irish Literary Revival. The house was burned during the Civil War in 1923. Its roofless shell on the lake shore is one of the most evocative Big House ruins in the west.

name: Westport House
description: One of Ireland's great country houses, a Palladian mansion built on the foundations of a Grace O'Malley castle, set in glorious grounds above Clew Bay.
history: Westport House, County Mayo, was built in the 1730s by architect Richard Cassels on the foundations of a castle belonging to Grace O'Malley. The house has been home to the Browne family (Marquesses of Sligo) for over 300 years. The magnificent interiors include a stunning dining room. The grounds feature gardens, a pirate adventure park, and views of Clew Bay.

name: National Museum Collins Barracks
description: A vast 18th-century military barracks in Dublin, now housing the National Museum of Ireland's decorative arts, history, and military collections.
history: Collins Barracks (formerly the Royal Barracks) in Dublin was built in 1702 and is one of the oldest continuously occupied barracks in Europe. Renamed after Michael Collins in 1922, it was converted into a branch of the National Museum of Ireland in 1997. The museum houses superb collections of Irish decorative arts, military history, and cultural artefacts.

name: Rathfarnham Castle
description: A remarkable Elizabethan fortified house in south Dublin, whose austere exterior conceals exquisite 18th-century interiors of the highest quality.
history: Built in 1583 by Adam Loftus, Archbishop of Dublin, Rathfarnham Castle is an Elizabethan fortified house with four flanker towers. The austere exterior hides spectacular 18th-century interiors designed by Sir William Chambers and James 'Athenian' Stuart. The castle later served as a Jesuit college and was restored by the OPW. It offers guided tours of the remarkable interior.

name: St Audoen's Church
description: Dublin's oldest surviving medieval parish church, where a rare 12th-century font and lucky stone draw visitors to this atmospheric corner of the old city.
history: St Audoen's Church in Dublin's High Street is the city's only surviving medieval parish church still in use. The oldest parts date to the 12th century. The church preserves a 12th-century font, the 'Lucky Stone' (a 9th-century graveslab), and fine medieval tombs. The adjacent St Audoen's Arch is the only surviving gate of Dublin's medieval city walls.

name: St Mary's Abbey Dublin
description: The vaulted chapter house of Dublin's once-great Cistercian abbey, where Silken Thomas launched his doomed rebellion against Henry VIII in 1534.
history: St Mary's Abbey was Dublin's most important medieval monastery. In its chapter house in 1534, 'Silken Thomas' Fitzgerald dramatically threw down the Sword of State and renounced his allegiance to Henry VIII, launching a rebellion that led to his execution. The vaulted chapter house, rediscovered in the 19th century, is the only substantial surviving fragment.

name: Portlick Castle
description: A medieval tower house on the shores of Lough Ree in County Westmeath, overlooking one of the Shannon's largest and most beautiful lakes.
history: Portlick Castle on the shores of Lough Ree, County Westmeath, is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th century. The castle was held by the Dillon family and later by the Nugent family. Its lakeside position on Lough Ree provides fine views across the water. The castle has been restored in recent years.

name: Killua Castle
description: A magnificent Gothic Revival castle in the Westmeath countryside, recently restored after decades of dereliction into a stunning cultural venue.
history: Killua Castle near Clonmellon, County Westmeath, was the seat of the Chapman family (ancestors of T.E. Lawrence, 'Lawrence of Arabia'). The castle dates to the 15th century with substantial Gothic Revival additions. After decades of abandonment, it has been spectacularly restored as a luxury event venue.

name: Greville Arms Tower
description: A historic tower in the Westmeath town of Mullingar, associated with the Greville family and the hotel that has hosted Irish literary figures.
history: The Greville Arms in Mullingar is a historic hotel with medieval tower origins. The hotel has hosted notable literary figures including James Joyce, who spent time in the town. Mullingar is the county town of Westmeath and a major midlands centre.

name: Dun na Si Heritage Park
description: A heritage park in Moate, County Westmeath, recreating aspects of Irish rural life and history across the centuries.
history: Dun na Si Heritage Park in Moate, County Westmeath, is a heritage centre and park that explores Irish rural life, folklore, and history. The park features a reconstructed crannog, famine cottage, and other historical structures. It offers an accessible introduction to Irish heritage for families and visitors.

name: Ballymore Castle (Westmeath)
description: Substantial Norman castle remains in the Westmeath village of Ballymore, where massive earthworks testify to the scale of medieval fortification.
history: Ballymore Castle in County Westmeath is a substantial Norman castle with impressive earthwork defences. The castle controlled the route through the midlands and was an important Anglo-Norman stronghold. The large motte and extensive earthworks indicate the site's medieval military significance.

name: Corlea Trackway
description: A magnificently preserved Iron Age timber road across the Longford boglands, built in 148 BC and now displayed in a purpose-built visitor centre.
history: The Corlea Trackway near Kenagh, County Longford, is a large Iron Age road of oak planks built across a bog in 148 BC. Discovered in the 1980s, it is the largest prehistoric trackway found in Europe. The remarkable timbers are preserved in a purpose-built visitor centre that maintains the controlled atmosphere needed to prevent the ancient wood from deteriorating.

name: Dungar Castle
description: A tower house in the Waterford countryside near Roscrea, part of the medieval defensive landscape of the southeast.
history: Dungar Castle is a medieval tower house. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortified residences in the region. The tower stands in agricultural land.

name: Granagh Castle
description: A substantial medieval castle on the banks of the River Suir in County Waterford, commanding a strategic river crossing.
history: Granagh Castle on the River Suir in County Waterford is a substantial medieval castle that controlled a river crossing. The castle dates to the medieval period and features significant structural remains including walls and towers. Its riverside position made it strategically important.

name: Puck Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the Dublin suburb of Shankill, a surprising survivor of the old Pale's defensive network amid modern housing.
history: Puck Castle in Shankill, south Dublin, is a medieval tower house that once formed part of the defensive network of the Pale. The compact tower survives amid suburban development and is a reminder that Dublin's southern suburbs were once a contested medieval frontier.

name: Castle Kevin
description: A medieval tower in the Wicklow countryside, in the shadow of the mountains that sheltered early Christian monks at nearby Glendalough.
history: Castle Kevin is a medieval tower house in County Wicklow, in the foothills of the Wicklow Mountains. The area has deep historical connections to the great monastic site of Glendalough. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the defence of the Pale's southern frontier against the O'Toole and O'Byrne clans of Wicklow.

name: Castlemore
description: An Anglo-Norman motte and castle ruin in County Carlow, one of the early fortifications built during the Norman conquest of Leinster.
history: Castlemore in County Carlow features a Norman motte and the remains of a medieval castle. The motte was constructed during the early phase of Norman expansion into Leinster in the late 12th century. The site was strategically positioned to control routes through the Barrow Valley.

name: Cloondooan Castle
description: A well-preserved tower house perched dramatically above the Atlantic in County Clare, where the cliffs meet the sea.
history: Cloondooan Castle is a well-preserved tower house on the Clare coast near Spanish Point. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and stands in a dramatic cliff-edge position overlooking the Atlantic. Spanish Armada ships were wrecked nearby in 1588.

name: Ballyclogh Castle
description: A well-preserved tower house in the north Cork countryside, standing proudly amid the rich farmland of the Blackwater Valley.
history: Ballyclogh Castle in north Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house in the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century and retains good structural integrity. The Blackwater Valley is one of Ireland's richest agricultural areas and was densely settled during the medieval period.

name: Ardtully Castle
description: Ruins of a McCarthy stronghold near Kenmare in Kerry, in the wild landscape where the Roughty River flows toward Kenmare Bay.
history: Ardtully Castle near Kenmare, County Kerry, was a McCarthy Mor stronghold. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled territory along the Roughty River near Kenmare Bay. The McCarthys were one of the great Gaelic families of Munster.

name: Killagha Abbey
description: Augustinian abbey ruins near Milltown in Kerry, a tranquil monastic site in the gentle landscape between the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas.
history: Killagha Abbey near Milltown, County Kerry, was an Augustinian priory founded in the 13th century. The ruins include church remains with fine architectural details. The abbey sits in the quiet countryside between the Dingle and Iveragh peninsulas.

name: Lislaughtin Abbey
description: A Franciscan friary ruin near Ballylongford in north Kerry, featuring one of the finest surviving medieval processional crosses in Ireland.
history: Lislaughtin Abbey near Ballylongford, County Kerry, was a Franciscan friary founded in 1464. The friary is notable for having preserved a remarkable medieval processional cross, now in the National Museum. The atmospheric ruins stand in the quiet north Kerry countryside.

name: Adare Desmond Castle
description: A large medieval castle on the banks of the River Maigue in Ireland's prettiest village, overlooking the famous thatched cottages and medieval churches of Adare.
history: The Desmond Castle in Adare, County Limerick, was built by the Earls of Kildare (Fitzgerald family) in the 13th century on the banks of the River Maigue. The substantial ruins include a great hall, two towers, and extensive curtain walls. The castle is accessible via the Adare Heritage Centre. Adare itself is famous for its picturesque thatched cottages and three medieval religious houses.

name: Murrisk Abbey
description: Atmospheric Augustinian friary ruins at the foot of Croagh Patrick, where pilgrims have gathered for millennia to climb Ireland's holiest mountain.
history: Murrisk Abbey on the shores of Clew Bay was an Augustinian friary founded in 1457 by the O'Malley family. The ruins stand at the traditional starting point for the pilgrimage up Croagh Patrick, Ireland's holiest mountain where St Patrick supposedly fasted for 40 days. A nearby sculpture commemorates the Famine coffin ships.

name: Strade Abbey
description: A Dominican friary in north Mayo preserving a remarkable collection of medieval tomb sculpture, including an extraordinary double-effigy tomb.
history: Strade Abbey near Foxford, County Mayo, was a Dominican friary. The abbey preserves a remarkable collection of medieval tomb sculptures, including a unique double-effigy tomb showing both a living figure and a cadaver beneath—a rare memento mori. The sculptural details are among the finest surviving medieval funerary art in the west of Ireland.

name: Creevelea Abbey
description: The last Franciscan friary founded in Ireland before the suppression, beautifully preserved in the wooded Leitrim countryside beside the Bonet River.
history: Creevelea Abbey near Dromahair, County Leitrim, was founded in 1508 by Owen O'Rourke and Margaret O'Brien—the last Franciscan friary established in Ireland before the dissolution. The well-preserved ruins include a fine cloister with carvings of St Francis and his animal companions. The riverside setting amid woodland is exceptionally beautiful.

name: Roodstown Castle
description: A remarkably well-preserved tower house in County Louth, its intact roof and original features making it one of the finest examples of its type in Ireland.
history: Roodstown Castle near Ardee, County Louth, is one of the best-preserved tower houses in Ireland, retaining its original roof, battlements, and much of its interior fabric. The 15th-century castle was part of the defences of the Pale and offers a rare authentic glimpse into medieval domestic architecture.

name: Athlumney Castle
description: A dramatic castle ruin on the hill above the River Boyne near Navan, deliberately burned by its owner to prevent it falling to William of Orange.
history: Athlumney Castle near Navan, County Meath, was burned by its owner Sir Lancelot Dowdall after the Battle of the Boyne in 1690, to prevent it being used by the victorious Williamite forces. The dramatic act of defiance left a magnificent shell combining a 15th-century tower house with a later Elizabethan mansion. The ruins overlook the Boyne Valley.

name: Castle Bernard
description: The spectacular roofless shell of a grand Georgian mansion in Bandon, burned during the War of Independence in one of Cork's most dramatic Big House destructions.
history: Castle Bernard near Bandon, County Cork, was the seat of the Earls of Bandon. The grand Georgian mansion was burned by the IRA in June 1921 during the War of Independence, with the Earl's wife narrowly escaping. The impressive shell, with its fine stonework and soaring chimney stacks, is one of Cork's most dramatic Big House ruins.

name: Sherkin Island Abbey
description: Franciscan friary ruins on the charming island of Sherkin in Roaringwater Bay, where medieval monks chose one of West Cork's most idyllic island settings.
history: Sherkin Island Abbey was a Franciscan friary founded in 1460 by the O'Driscoll family on Sherkin Island in Roaringwater Bay, West Cork. The friary was sacked by Waterford forces in 1537. The atmospheric ruins stand near the island's ferry pier. Sherkin Island is a popular day trip from Baltimore, with beautiful beaches and a marine research station.

name: Movilla Abbey
description: One of Ireland's most important early Christian monastic sites, founded in the 6th century in the County Down town of Newtownards.
history: Movilla Abbey in Newtownards, County Down, was founded by St Finian in the 6th century. It became one of the most important monastic schools in Ireland and possibly produced the Movilla Psalter. The Augustinian priory ruins date to the medieval period. The site is within a graveyard and retains its ancient spiritual atmosphere.

name: Shane Castle
description: The O'Neill seat on the shores of Lough Neagh—a grand estate where Ireland's most powerful Gaelic dynasty held court.
history: Shane's Castle on the shores of Lough Neagh, County Antrim, has been the seat of the O'Neill family since the 16th century. The castle was badly damaged by fire in 1816 and never fully rebuilt. The estate includes extensive grounds with a narrow-gauge railway, nature reserve, and ruins. The O'Neills were the most powerful dynasty in Ulster for centuries.

name: Harry Avery Castle
description: An unusual hilltop castle in County Tyrone with twin D-shaped towers, named after a 14th-century O'Neill chieftain—a rare Gaelic stone castle.
history: Harry Avery's Castle near Newtownstewart, County Tyrone, is one of the few stone castles built by a Gaelic Irish chieftain. Named after Henry Aimhreidh O'Neill, a 14th-century lord, the castle features unusual twin D-shaped towers flanking the entrance. The hilltop position provides commanding views across the Tyrone countryside. It is a rare example of Gaelic lords adopting Norman-style stone fortification.

name: Castlegrace
description: A tower house in the south Tipperary countryside, one of many Butler strongholds in the productive agricultural heartland of the Golden Vale.
history: Castlegrace is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle was associated with the Grace family (from whom it takes its name) and the wider Butler lordship in the Golden Vale. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Portumna Priory
description: Dominican priory ruins near Portumna Castle, adding medieval monastic heritage to this important Shannon-side town.
history: Portumna Priory was a Dominican foundation near Portumna, County Galway, on the shores of Lough Derg. The priory complements the nearby Portumna Castle and adds to the town's rich heritage as a major Shannon crossing point and lakeside settlement.

name: Rathcroghan
description: The legendary capital of ancient Connacht and entrance to the Celtic Otherworld—one of the most important archaeological complexes in Europe.
history: Rathcroghan (Cruachan Ai) near Tulsk, County Roscommon, was the ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht and features in the great Irish epic Tain Bo Cuailnge. The complex includes over 60 monuments including a royal inauguration mound, ringforts, and Oweynagat ('Cave of the Cats')—traditionally the entrance to the Celtic Otherworld. A visitor centre at Tulsk interprets this extraordinary landscape.

name: Ballinskelligs Priory
description: Atmospheric Augustinian priory ruins on the wild Kerry coast, where medieval monks lived at the edge of the known world.
history: Ballinskelligs Priory on the coast of the Iveragh Peninsula, County Kerry, was an Augustinian foundation associated with the monks of Skellig Michael who moved ashore in the 12th century. The ruins stand on a beach exposed to the Atlantic. The monks likely moved from the remote Skellig rock seeking more sustainable living conditions while maintaining their spiritual life.

name: Staigue Fort
description: One of the finest and largest stone forts in Ireland, a massive drystone circular enclosure in the wild mountains of the Iveragh Peninsula.
history: Staigue Fort near Castlecove on the Iveragh Peninsula, County Kerry, is one of the finest stone ringforts in Ireland. The massive circular drystone wall stands up to 5.5 metres high and 4 metres thick, with an interior stairway system. Dating to the Iron Age or early Christian period, it was likely a royal or ceremonial site. The spectacular mountain setting adds to its impact.

name: Granuaile Tower
description: A tower associated with Grace O'Malley on the Clare Island or mainland coast, commemorating the legendary pirate queen of the western seas.
history: Granuaile (Grace O'Malley) towers and castles are found at several points along the Mayo coast, marking the territory of Ireland's most famous female warrior. The pirate queen controlled the seas off the west coast in the 16th century, commanding a fleet from her various strongholds and famously negotiating with Queen Elizabeth I.

name: Castle Hyde
description: A magnificent Georgian mansion on the banks of the River Blackwater, childhood home of Douglas Hyde, first President of Ireland.
history: Castle Hyde near Fermoy, County Cork, was the childhood home of Douglas Hyde (1860-1949), founder of the Gaelic League and first President of Ireland. The elegant Georgian mansion overlooks the River Blackwater, often called the 'Irish Rhine.' The estate was later purchased by Michael Flatley of Riverdance fame.

name: Glanworth Castle
description: A dramatic medieval fortress spanning the River Funshion in north Cork, where the oldest stone bridge in Ireland still carries traffic beneath castle walls.
history: Glanworth Castle in north Cork stands dramatically above the River Funshion. The castle was built by the Roche family (Lords of Fermoy) in the 13th century. Nearby is what is claimed to be the oldest bridge in Ireland, a 13th-century stone structure still in use. The castle ruins, bridge, and Dominican friary create a remarkable medieval ensemble.

name: Ballykinavarga Castle
description: A tower house in the heart of the Burren, surrounded by the extraordinary karst landscape of County Clare.
history: Ballykinavarga Castle is a medieval tower house in the Burren, County Clare. The castle sits amid the unique limestone landscape of the Burren, with its characteristic pavement, wildflowers, and archaeological richness. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Craggaunowen Castle
description: A restored tower house housing a Bronze Age living history centre, where a full-size replica of St Brendan's leather boat proves the Atlantic was crossable 1,500 years ago.
history: Craggaunowen near Quin, County Clare, is a reconstructed heritage site centred on a restored 16th-century tower house. The site includes recreated crannogs, ring forts, and the Brendan Boat—a leather-hulled currach in which Tim Severin sailed to North America in 1976, proving St Brendan's legendary 6th-century voyage was technically possible.

name: Killaloe Cathedral
description: A fine medieval cathedral in the charming lakeside town of Killaloe, where Brian Boru held court as High King of Ireland.
history: St Flannan's Cathedral in Killaloe, County Clare, dates to the 12th century and stands near the site where Brian Boru, High King of Ireland, had his palace of Kincora. The cathedral preserves a remarkable Romanesque doorway and an ogham stone with both ogham and runic inscriptions. Killaloe sits at the southern end of Lough Derg on the Shannon.

name: Kilmacduagh Round Tower
description: Ireland's most dramatically leaning round tower, standing over 34 metres tall beside a remarkable cluster of medieval churches in the Galway countryside.
history: Kilmacduagh Monastery near Gort, County Galway, features Ireland's tallest leaning round tower at 34 metres, with a striking tilt of about half a metre. The monastic complex, founded by St Colman MacDuagh in the 7th century, includes a cathedral, several churches, and the tower. The setting, with the Burren hills behind, is one of Ireland's most photographed heritage scenes.

name: Roscam Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and early Christian church ruin on the shores of Galway Bay, with views across the water to the Burren.
history: Roscam Round Tower and church ruins stand on the shore of Galway Bay east of Galway city. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period and preserves the stump of a round tower and medieval church ruins. The waterside setting with views across Galway Bay to the Burren hills is particularly scenic.

name: James Fort
description: A massive star-shaped artillery fort guarding Kinsale Harbour, built after the famous 1601 Battle of Kinsale that changed the course of Irish history.
history: James Fort on the eastern shore of Kinsale Harbour, County Cork, was built in 1602 following the Battle of Kinsale (1601)—the defeat that ended Gaelic Ireland's last major military resistance. The substantial star-shaped fortification was later superseded by Charles Fort across the harbour but remains an impressive military monument with fine harbour views.

name: Puxley Mansion
description: The dramatic ruined shell of a copper baron's mansion on the Beara Peninsula, inspiration for Daphne du Maurier's 'Hungry Hill.'
history: Puxley Mansion near Castletownbere on the Beara Peninsula, County Cork, was built by the Puxley family who made their fortune from copper mining in the Allihies mines. The grand Gothic Revival mansion inspired Daphne du Maurier's novel 'Hungry Hill.' Burned during the War of Independence in 1921, the spectacular ruin overlooks Dunboy Bay.

name: Kilcooly Abbey
description: A magnificently preserved Cistercian abbey in County Tipperary, featuring some of the finest medieval stone carvings in Ireland.
history: Kilcooly Abbey near Urlingford, County Tipperary, is a Cistercian monastery founded in 1184. After being damaged in the 15th century, it was rebuilt with extraordinary stone carvings including a remarkable sacristy doorway and a unique cloister arcade with figural sculptures. The quality of the stone carving rivals any medieval site in Ireland.

name: Croom Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the Limerick village of Croom, on the banks of the River Maigue where the famous Maigue Poets once held literary courts.
history: Croom Castle on the River Maigue in County Limerick dates to the medieval period. The village is famous as the home of the Maigue Poets, a circle of 18th-century Irish-language poets including Sean O Tuama and Aindrias Mac Craith who kept the bardic tradition alive during the penal era.

name: Lough Gur Visitor Centre
description: An interpretive centre for one of Ireland's most important archaeological landscapes, where Stone Age and Bronze Age monuments cluster around a magical lake.
history: Lough Gur in County Limerick is one of the most important archaeological sites in Ireland, with monuments spanning 6,000 years from the Neolithic to the medieval period. The Grange Stone Circle, Ireland's largest, stands nearby. The visitor centre interprets the extraordinary concentration of tombs, standing stones, crannogs, and medieval castles around this enchanted lake.

name: Ballina Friary
description: Augustinian friary remains in the Mayo town of Ballina, on the banks of the salmon-rich River Moy.
history: Ballina Friary in County Mayo was an Augustinian foundation on the banks of the River Moy. Ballina is renowned as one of Ireland's premier salmon fishing towns, with the Moy being one of the most productive salmon rivers in Europe. The friary ruins add medieval heritage to this lively market town.

name: Skryne Church
description: Medieval church ruins on the Hill of Skreen in County Meath, with legendary connections to the early spread of Christianity in Ireland.
history: The Hill of Skreen (Skryne) in County Meath was an important early Christian site. The medieval church ruins stand on a hill with views toward the Hill of Tara. According to tradition, St Patrick's disciple Colmcille founded a church here. The hill has deep archaeological significance in the Tara landscape.

name: Hill of Tara
description: The ancient seat of the High Kings of Ireland—a sacred hilltop where kingship, mythology, and archaeology converge in the most symbolically charged landscape in the country.
history: The Hill of Tara in County Meath was the ceremonial and political capital of ancient Ireland, seat of the High Kings for millennia. The site preserves the Lia Fail (Stone of Destiny), the Mound of the Hostages (a Neolithic passage tomb c.3000 BC), and extensive earthworks. Daniel O'Connell held a monster meeting here in 1843. Its symbolic significance in Irish identity is unmatched.

name: Black Abbey Kilkenny
description: A beautifully restored Dominican priory in the heart of medieval Kilkenny, still in active religious use with a stunning rosette window.
history: The Black Abbey in Kilkenny was founded in 1225 and is one of the oldest Dominican foundations in Ireland. The priory takes its name from the black cloaks of the Dominican friars. After dissolution and centuries of use as a courthouse, it was restored to religious use in 1866. The magnificent rosette window and medieval architecture make it one of Kilkenny's treasures.

name: St Canice's Cathedral
description: Kilkenny's magnificent medieval cathedral with Ireland's second-tallest round tower, offering panoramic views of the medieval city from its ancient summit.
history: St Canice's Cathedral in Kilkenny dates to the 13th century and is the second-longest medieval cathedral in Ireland. The adjacent round tower, dating to the 6th-9th century, is the second tallest in Ireland and can be climbed for spectacular views. The cathedral contains remarkable medieval tomb effigies and architectural details spanning 800 years of history.

name: Castle Ward
description: A unique National Trust estate with two architectural personalities—Classical on one side, Gothic on the other—reflecting a husband and wife who couldn't agree on style.
history: Castle Ward near Strangford, County Down, is a unique 18th-century mansion with its front in Classical Palladian style (Lord Bangor's choice) and its rear in Gothic (Lady Bangor's preference). The estate overlooks Strangford Lough and has been used as a filming location for Game of Thrones (as Winterfell). Now a National Trust property with extensive grounds and activities.

name: Donegal Abbey
description: Franciscan friary ruins on the banks of Donegal Bay, where the Annals of the Four Masters—Ireland's greatest historical chronicle—were compiled.
history: Donegal Abbey (Donegal Friary) was a Franciscan foundation established in 1474 by Red Hugh O'Donnell and his wife Nuala O'Brien. It is most famous as the place where the Annals of the Four Masters were compiled (1632-36), recording Irish history from the Deluge to 1616. The atmospheric ruins stand on the shore of Donegal Bay in the town centre.

name: Classiebawn Castle
description: A dramatic Victorian castle on the Mullaghmore Peninsula in Sligo, tragically associated with the murder of Lord Mountbatten in 1979.
history: Classiebawn Castle on the Mullaghmore Peninsula, County Sligo, was built in the 1870s for Lord Palmerston. It later passed to the Mountbatten family. On 27 August 1979, Lord Louis Mountbatten was killed by an IRA bomb while fishing from his boat at Mullaghmore harbour. The castle remains in private hands and is visible from the coast road.

name: Strokestown House
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion housing the National Famine Museum, where harrowing landlord correspondence reveals the human tragedy of Ireland's darkest chapter.
history: Strokestown Park House in County Roscommon is a Palladian mansion built in the 1730s. The house is most significant for its National Famine Museum, which uses the estate's archive of landlord-tenant correspondence to tell the devastating story of the Great Famine (1845-1852). Major Denis Mahon, the landlord, was assassinated in 1847. The house, museum, and restored gardens form one of Ireland's most important heritage sites.

name: Dungarvan Castle
description: A compact but impressive Norman castle guarding the harbour of Dungarvan, one of Waterford's most vibrant and foodie-friendly towns.
history: Dungarvan Castle was built by King John around 1185 and stands guard over the harbour of this attractive Waterford town. The castle features a large circular keep within a polygonal curtain wall. Dungarvan has become one of Ireland's premier food destinations, and the castle adds medieval character to the bustling harbour town. Managed by the OPW.

name: Tybroughney Castle
description: A riverside tower house on the Tipperary-Kilkenny border, overlooking the confluence of the Suir and its tributaries.
history: Tybroughney Castle is a medieval tower house on the banks of the River Suir near Piltown, on the Tipperary-Kilkenny border. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled a section of the Suir. Its riverside setting reflects the strategic importance of waterway control in medieval Ireland.

name: Duncannon Fort
description: A massive star-shaped fortress commanding the approaches to Waterford Harbour, where four centuries of military engineering have created an extraordinary coastal stronghold.
history: Duncannon Fort on the Hook Peninsula, County Wexford, is a large star-shaped fort built in 1588 to counter the Spanish Armada threat. It was expanded over the following centuries and saw action in the Confederate Wars and the 1798 Rebellion. The fort remained in military use until 1922. Its commanding position at the mouth of Waterford Harbour and massive stone defences make it one of Ireland's most impressive military heritage sites.

name: Leighlinbridge Castle
description: A Norman castle on the banks of the River Barrow, where one of Ireland's oldest bridges crosses a river that has been a highway since prehistoric times.
history: Leighlinbridge Castle in County Carlow stands beside one of Ireland's oldest bridges over the River Barrow. Built around 1180 by the Normans, the castle controlled this important river crossing. The attractive village preserves its medieval character with the castle, bridge, and riverside setting creating a harmonious heritage scene.

name: Clonegal Castle
description: A castle in the Carlow-Wexford border village of Clonegal, close to the ancient Wicklow Gap pass into the mountains.
history: Clonegal Castle in County Carlow stands in a picturesque village on the River Derry at the border of Carlow and Wexford. The castle dates to the medieval period and was near the route through the Wicklow Mountains. The village retains a quiet charm with its riverside setting.

name: Wicklow Gaol
description: A chilling 18th-century prison now a museum, where the horrors of transportation, the 1798 Rebellion, and the Great Famine come vividly to life.
history: Wicklow Gaol, built in 1702, now operates as an award-winning heritage museum. The gaol held prisoners including 1798 rebels and transported convicts. The museum uses actors, sound effects, and multimedia to recreate the harrowing conditions. The Great Famine section is particularly moving. It is one of Ireland's most popular heritage attractions.

name: Baltinglass Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins in the Wicklow town of Baltinglass, where medieval monks built on a hilltop already sacred for thousands of years.
history: Baltinglass Abbey in County Wicklow was a Cistercian foundation established in 1148 by Dermot MacMurrough, King of Leinster. The abbey ruins include fine Romanesque architectural details. The nearby hilltop preserves a Neolithic passage tomb, creating a landscape where Christian and pre-Christian sacred sites coexist.

name: Oughterard Round Tower
description: The stump of an early Christian round tower in the Kildare village of Oughterard, near an ancient cemetery with a remarkable medieval church ruin.
history: Oughterard Round Tower in County Kildare is the stump of an early Christian round tower beside a medieval church ruin and ancient graveyard. The site is near Straffan and preserves early Christian heritage in the heart of the Kildare countryside. The round tower marks the site of an early monastery.

name: Athy Castle
description: A substantial medieval tower in the Kildare town of Athy, commanding the crossing of the River Barrow since the 15th century.
history: Athy Castle (also known as White's Castle) in County Kildare stands beside the River Barrow. Built in the 15th century by the Earl of Kildare, it guarded the river crossing. Athy was an important military position on the southern frontier of the Pale. The castle now houses a local heritage centre.

name: Clane Friary
description: Franciscan friary remains in the Kildare town of Clane, founded by a FitzGerald lord in the medieval heartland of the Pale.
history: Clane Friary in County Kildare was a Franciscan foundation. The town of Clane sits on the River Liffey in the heart of County Kildare, historically within the English Pale. The friary dates to the medieval period and was one of several religious houses in this area of strong Anglo-Norman influence.

name: Abbeyleix Heritage House
description: A heritage centre in one of Ireland's most attractive planned towns, where the de Vesci family created an elegant estate village.
history: Abbeyleix Heritage House in County Laois tells the story of this beautiful planned estate town created by the de Vesci family. The town is considered one of Ireland's most attractive, with its wide main street and fine buildings. The heritage centre, in the former North Boys School, also covers the Cistercian abbey founded here in 1183.

name: Kilbeggan Abbey
description: Medieval monastic remains near the distillery town of Kilbeggan in Westmeath, adding ecclesiastical heritage to the town's whiskey story.
history: Kilbeggan Abbey is a medieval monastic site near Kilbeggan, County Westmeath. The town is primarily known for its distillery (established 1757), but the abbey remains add a medieval dimension to the local heritage. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period.

name: Longford Castle
description: Castle remains in the county town of Longford, where the O'Farrells once held sway over the Irish midlands.
history: Longford Castle stood in the county town of Longford and was associated with the O'Farrell clan and later English planter families. The town developed as a market centre and the castle served various administrative functions. Little of the original medieval castle survives in the modern town.

name: Drumlane Abbey
description: Well-preserved medieval monastic ruins near Milltown in County Cavan, featuring a fine round tower stump and Augustinian church.
history: Drumlane Abbey near Milltown, County Cavan, combines a round tower stump with substantial Augustinian church ruins. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period, with the surviving buildings primarily medieval. The round tower and church are set in a quiet, atmospheric graveyard in the Cavan drumlin country.

name: Kilmore Cathedral
description: The cathedral of the Diocese of Kilmore in County Cavan, incorporating a remarkable Romanesque doorway rescued from a medieval monastery on a nearby island.
history: Kilmore Cathedral in County Cavan incorporates a magnificent 12th-century Romanesque doorway that was originally part of a monastery on Trinity Island in Lough Oughter. The richly carved doorway features interlaced patterns and human heads. The current cathedral dates to 1860 but the Romanesque doorway makes it a significant medieval heritage site.

name: Monaghan Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of a plantation-era castle in the county town of Monaghan, where the complexities of Ulster's colonial history are writ in stone.
history: Monaghan Castle was built in the early 17th century as part of the plantation of Ulster. The castle served as an English military and administrative centre. Little survives above ground, but the site is historically significant as a marker of the plantation's impact on this part of Ulster. The town of Monaghan developed around the castle.

name: Clones Round Tower
description: A fine round tower in the Monaghan border town of Clones, where early Christian heritage meets the complex history of the Ulster borderlands.
history: The Round Tower at Clones, County Monaghan, dates to the 10th-12th century and is part of an early Christian monastic complex that also includes a high cross and a sarcophagus in the Diamond (town square). Clones was an important monastic centre founded by St Tiernach in the 6th century. The town later became famous as a GAA stronghold and lace-making centre.

name: Dunsoghly Castle
description: One of the best-preserved tower houses in Ireland, uniquely retaining its original oak roof timbers from the 15th century.
history: Dunsoghly Castle near Dublin Airport is one of the most important medieval buildings in Ireland—one of only a handful of tower houses to retain its original medieval timber roof. Built by the Plunkett family around 1450, the four-storey castle is remarkably well preserved. The roof timbers have been tree-ring dated to confirm their medieval origin.

name: Clontarf Castle
description: A grand castellated hotel on the site where Brian Boru won the Battle of Clontarf in 1014, breaking Viking power in Ireland forever.
history: Clontarf Castle Hotel stands on land associated with the famous Battle of Clontarf (1014), where High King Brian Boru defeated a Viking coalition but was killed in his tent after the victory. The current castellated building dates mainly to the 19th century but incorporates earlier elements. It now operates as a luxury hotel.

name: Downhill House
description: The spectacular roofless shell of an 18th-century bishop's palace perched on the wild Derry clifftops, with the iconic Mussenden Temple clinging to the cliff edge.
history: Downhill House was built in the 1770s for Frederick Hervey, the eccentric Earl Bishop of Derry. The grand house was damaged by fire twice and finally abandoned. Its roofless shell stands dramatically on the Antrim coast cliffs. The nearby Mussenden Temple, inspired by the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli, perches precariously on the cliff edge overlooking the Atlantic.

name: Sampson's Tower Derry
description: A historic tower within the walls of Derry, part of the finest example of a walled city in Ireland and Britain.
history: Sampson's Tower is one of the features of Derry's city walls, the most complete and finest example of a walled city in Ireland and Britain. The walls were built between 1613 and 1619 during the Plantation of Ulster and have never been breached. They played a crucial role during the famous Siege of Derry in 1689.

name: Armagh Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins in the ancient ecclesiastical capital of Ireland, where St Patrick established his church in the 5th century.
history: Armagh Friary is part of the rich heritage of Armagh, Ireland's ecclesiastical capital since St Patrick established his church here in 445 AD. The city is the seat of both the Catholic and Church of Ireland Archbishops of Armagh. The friary ruins add to the medieval religious heritage of this historically significant city.

name: Kilnasaggart Pillar Stone
description: One of the oldest dated Christian monuments in Ireland, an 8th-century inscribed pillar stone in the Armagh countryside.
history: The Kilnasaggart Pillar Stone near Jonesborough, County Armagh, dates to around 700 AD and is one of the earliest inscribed Christian monuments in Ireland. The inscription records a grant of the site to a person named Ternoc. The stone is decorated with crosses and the inscription is in Irish. It stands at an ancient crossroads in a simple enclosure.

name: Rathdrum Castle
description: Castle remains in the Wicklow village of Rathdrum, boyhood home of Charles Stewart Parnell, the 'uncrowned king of Ireland.'
history: Rathdrum in County Wicklow has castle remains and is best known as the location of Avondale House, childhood home of Charles Stewart Parnell (1846-1891), the great nationalist leader known as the 'uncrowned king of Ireland.' Avondale House and Forest Park are now a major visitor attraction.

name: Taghmon Castle
description: A medieval tower and church ruin in the Westmeath village of Taghmon, preserving early Christian heritage in the Irish midlands.
history: Taghmon in County Westmeath preserves medieval castle and church remains. The name derives from 'Teach Munna' (House of St Munna), indicating early Christian monastic origins. The village retains its heritage character in the quiet Westmeath midlands.

name: Fethard Castle
description: Medieval town walls and castle in one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval towns, where an almost complete circuit of walls still stands in County Tipperary.
history: Fethard in County Tipperary is one of the best-preserved medieval walled towns in Ireland. The town retains an almost complete circuit of medieval walls, several tower houses, a medieval main street, and a fine Augustinian priory. The castle and town walls together create one of Ireland's most intact medieval urban landscapes.

name: Oola Castle
description: A tower house in the Limerick-Tipperary borderlands, near the historic pass where the medieval dioceses of Cashel and Emly met.
history: Oola Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Oola on the Limerick-Tipperary border. The area was on the boundary between the medieval dioceses of Cashel and Emly. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the fertile agricultural lands of the Golden Vale.

name: Hospital Church
description: Medieval church and Knights Hospitaller preceptory remains in the Limerick village of Hospital, named for the medieval military order.
history: The village of Hospital in County Limerick takes its name from the Knights Hospitaller who established a preceptory here in the medieval period. The church ruins preserve fine medieval architectural details. The Knights Hospitaller (Order of St John of Jerusalem) held extensive properties across Ireland.

name: Ballyhannon Castle Clare
description: A tower house in the Clare countryside, one of the many castles built by the O'Brien and McNamara families across Thomond.
history: Ballyhannon Castle in County Clare is a medieval tower house. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was part of the dense network of tower houses in County Clare, which has one of the highest concentrations of such structures in Ireland.

name: Kilnaboy Church
description: A medieval church in the Burren with a remarkable sheela-na-gig carving and a fine Romanesque doorway, deep in Clare's limestone landscape.
history: Kilnaboy Church in the Burren, County Clare, preserves a fine Romanesque doorway and one of Ireland's best-known sheela-na-gig carvings above the south door. The church dates to the 11th-12th century and was associated with St Inghean Baoith. The Burren setting adds to the atmospheric quality of this remarkable medieval site.

name: Clare Island Abbey
description: A medieval Cistercian abbey on Clare Island featuring rare and extraordinary 13th-century wall paintings—unique survivors in western Ireland.
history: Clare Island Abbey on Clare Island, County Mayo, is a medieval Cistercian (or Carmelite) foundation notable for its extraordinary 13th-century ceiling paintings—the only surviving medieval wall paintings in the west of Ireland. The paintings depict mythological and religious scenes. Grace O'Malley is traditionally buried in the abbey.

name: Meelick Friary
description: A medieval Franciscan friary on the Shannon near Eyrecourt, one of the older religious foundations along Ireland's great river.
history: Meelick Friary on the River Shannon in County Galway is a Franciscan foundation dating to the medieval period. The friary stands near the Shannon, Ireland's longest river, and was an important medieval religious house. The atmospheric ruins include church and domestic building remains.

name: Abbey Knockmoy
description: A Cistercian abbey in County Galway preserving rare medieval wall paintings depicting the legend of the Three Living and Three Dead.
history: Knockmoy Abbey near Abbeyknockmoy, County Galway, was a Cistercian monastery founded in 1190 by Cathal Crobdearg O'Connor, King of Connacht. The abbey preserves rare medieval wall paintings including the 'Three Living and Three Dead'—a memento mori showing three kings encountering three skeletal figures. These are among the finest surviving medieval wall paintings in Ireland.

name: Annaghkeen Castle
description: A lakeside tower house on the shores of Lough Corrib, accessible by boat or a scenic walk through the Galway countryside.
history: Annaghkeen Castle on the shores of Lough Corrib, County Galway, is a medieval tower house in a picturesque lakeside setting. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local Galway families. Its lakeside position on Lough Corrib, Ireland's largest lake, makes it a scenic heritage site.

name: Ballycurrin Castle
description: A dramatic tower house on a headland jutting into Lough Corrib, with commanding views across Ireland's largest lake.
history: Ballycurrin Castle on Lough Corrib, County Galway, occupies a dramatic headland position on Ireland's largest lake. The castle dates to the medieval period. The lighthouse beside it was built in the 19th century—one of the few inland lighthouses in Ireland, guiding boats through Lough Corrib's sometimes treacherous waters.

name: Loughrea Priory
description: Carmelite priory remains in the heritage town of Loughrea, complementing the town's remarkable collection of Celtic Revival stained glass.
history: Loughrea Priory was a Carmelite foundation in the medieval town of Loughrea, County Galway. The town's St Brendan's Cathedral houses one of the finest collections of Celtic Revival stained glass in Ireland, by artists including Harry Clarke and Sarah Purser. The priory ruins add to the town's rich medieval and artistic heritage.

name: Scattery Island Monastic Site
description: A magnificent cluster of early Christian remains on an island in the Shannon Estuary, including Ireland's tallest round tower at 36 metres.
history: Scattery Island (Inis Cathaigh) in the Shannon Estuary, County Clare, preserves one of Ireland's most remarkable monastic complexes. Founded by St Senan in the 6th century, the island features Ireland's tallest round tower (36 metres), five churches, and a holy well. The round tower is unusual in having its doorway at ground level. Boat trips run from Kilrush in summer.

name: Cahermore Stone Fort
description: A large stone ringfort in the Burren near Fanore, its massive walls offering a dramatic example of early medieval defensive architecture.
history: Cahermore (Caher Mor) is a large stone ringfort in the Burren, County Clare, near the coast at Fanore. The substantial drystone walls enclose a large area and the fort dates to the early medieval period. Its Burren setting amid the limestone pavement makes it atmospheric to visit, and it is less crowded than the nearby Caherconnell fort.

name: Lemaneagh Castle
description: A dramatic composite castle on the edge of the Burren—a medieval tower married to a grand 17th-century fortified house by the fierce Maire Rua.
history: Lemaneagh Castle on the edge of the Burren, County Clare, combines a 15th-century tower house with a 17th-century four-storey fortified house built by Conor O'Brien and his formidable wife Maire Rua (Red Mary). Maire Rua is the subject of numerous legends involving murdered husbands and a defiant spirit. The dramatic ruin stands at a crossroads on the Burren's eastern edge.

name: Clonmel West Gate
description: The only surviving gate of Clonmel's medieval town walls, in the largest town in County Tipperary and a centre of commerce since Norman times.
history: The West Gate is the only surviving gate of Clonmel's medieval town walls. Clonmel is the largest town in County Tipperary and has a rich medieval heritage. The town walls were substantial enough for the garrison to resist Oliver Cromwell's forces for several weeks in 1650—one of the few towns to offer serious resistance.

name: Monaincha Abbey
description: A remarkable island monastery in a drained bog near Roscrea, where a Romanesque church and high cross stand in extraordinary isolation.
history: Monaincha Abbey near Roscrea, County Tipperary, was originally on an island in a bog lake (now drained). The site preserves a fine 12th-century Romanesque church and a high cross. The extraordinary setting—a stone church apparently standing in the middle of flat bogland—creates one of Ireland's most unusual heritage scenes.

name: Kilrossanty Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Waterford countryside, in the scenic foothills of the Comeragh Mountains.
history: Kilrossanty Church is a medieval ruin in County Waterford near the Comeragh Mountains. The church dates to the medieval period and served the local parish. The Comeragh Mountains, with their dramatic corrie lakes and mountain trails, provide a scenic backdrop.

name: Annaduff Church
description: An early Christian church site near Drumsna in County Leitrim, associated with the monastic heritage of the Shannon region.
history: Annaduff Church near Drumsna, County Leitrim, is an early Christian and medieval ecclesiastical site. The area has deep religious heritage, and Drumsna is notable as the place where Anthony Trollope began writing his first novel. The church ruins preserve early and medieval Christian heritage.

name: Clonalis House
description: The ancestral home of the O'Conor Don—descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland—housing a treasure trove of manuscripts and the inaugural harp of the kings.
history: Clonalis House near Castlerea, County Roscommon, is the seat of the O'Conor Don, direct descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland. The Victorian Italianate mansion houses a remarkable collection including the coronation stone of the Kings of Connacht, the harp of Turlough O'Carolan (Ireland's national composer), and manuscripts dating back 1,500 years.

name: Fuerty Church
description: A medieval church ruin near Roscommon town, in an area of rich ecclesiastical heritage stretching back to early Christianity.
history: Fuerty Church near Roscommon is a medieval church ruin associated with the ancient monastic foundation of St Justinian. The site preserves medieval church architecture in the Roscommon countryside.

name: Aghalurcher Church
description: A medieval church ruin in County Fermanagh, in the quiet lake country of the upper Erne basin.
history: Aghalurcher Church is a medieval church ruin in County Fermanagh. The site has early Christian origins and the medieval church remains stand in a traditional graveyard. The area is part of the lake-studded Erne basin landscape.

name: Lough Navar Castle
description: A viewpoint and fortification site in the Fermanagh lakelands, offering one of Ireland's finest panoramic views across Lower Lough Erne.
history: Lough Navar Forest in County Fermanagh includes castle ruins and offers the famous Cliffs of Magho viewpoint—one of the finest panoramic views in Ireland, stretching across Lower Lough Erne to the Donegal mountains. The forest park contains trails, wildlife habitats, and historical features.

name: Lisnaskea Castle
description: Castle remains in the Fermanagh market town of Lisnaskea, near the shores of Upper Lough Erne in the heart of the Lakeland district.
history: Lisnaskea in County Fermanagh has castle remains near the town centre. The town sits near the shores of Upper Lough Erne and has a long history as a market town. Nearby Castle Balfour is the more substantial castle in the town.

name: Killala Round Tower
description: A fine round tower in the Mayo coastal town of Killala, where the French invasion of 1798 began with General Humbert's dramatic landing.
history: The Round Tower at Killala, County Mayo, dates to the 10th-12th century and stands beside the Protestant cathedral. Killala is most famous as the landing place of General Humbert's French expeditionary force in August 1798, supporting the Irish rebellion. The tower is well preserved and the town retains its quiet seaside character.

name: Gortmore Castle
description: A tower house in the Mayo countryside, one of many scattered medieval fortifications in this wild western landscape.
