history: Gortmore Castle is a medieval tower house in County Mayo. The castle dates to the medieval period and was one of numerous fortified residences in the area.

name: Innisfree Island
description: The tiny island in Lough Gill that inspired W.B. Yeats' most beloved poem—'I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree.'
history: The Lake Isle of Innisfree in Lough Gill, County Sligo, inspired W.B. Yeats' most famous poem (1888): 'I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree, And a small cabin build there, of clay and wattles made.' The tiny, tree-covered island can be viewed from the shore or by boat tour. It remains one of the most literary landscapes in Ireland.

name: Oldcastle Meath
description: A heritage town near the Loughcrew Cairns in northwest Meath, gateway to one of Ireland's most important Neolithic landscapes.
history: Oldcastle in County Meath is the gateway to the Loughcrew Cairns, an extraordinary Neolithic passage tomb complex dating to c.3300 BC. The town serves as a base for exploring this ancient landscape. The area also contains numerous medieval and later heritage sites.

name: Navan Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the county town of Meath, in the heart of Ireland's richest archaeological landscape.
history: Navan, the county town of Meath, has Norman castle remains. The town sits at the confluence of the Rivers Boyne and Blackwater, a strategically important position since prehistoric times. The area surrounding Navan contains extraordinary archaeological sites including the Hill of Tara and the Boyne Valley tombs.

name: Rathmoylan Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Waterford countryside near the coast, in an area of quiet rural beauty.
history: Rathmoylan Church is a medieval church ruin in County Waterford. The church dates to the medieval period and stands in the coastal hinterland of south Waterford, an area of quiet agricultural landscapes and scattered heritage sites.

name: Castletown Castle Laois
description: Castle remains in the Laois midlands, part of the complex history of plantation and resistance in Ireland's central counties.
history: Castletown Castle in County Laois dates to the medieval/plantation period. The area was at the heart of the Tudor plantation of Laois (Queen's County) in the 16th century, one of the earliest and most contested colonial ventures in Ireland.

name: Coolbanagher Church
description: An elegant 18th-century Church of Ireland church in Laois designed by the great James Gandon, architect of Dublin's Custom House.
history: Coolbanagher Church in County Laois was designed by James Gandon in 1785, the architect of Dublin's Custom House and Four Courts. The elegant church is considered one of Gandon's lesser-known masterpieces and features fine Classical proportions. It stands in the quiet Laois countryside.

name: Castle Durrow
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion in County Laois, now a luxury castle hotel set in parkland designed by Capability Brown.
history: Castle Durrow in County Laois was built in 1716 for Colonel William Flower, 1st Viscount Ashbrook. The Palladian mansion is set in grounds attributed to Capability Brown. The building has been restored as a luxury castle hotel. The nearby town of Durrow hosts Ireland's annual Scarecrow Festival.

name: Aran Islands Arkin Castle
description: A small medieval fortress on Inis Mor, the largest of the Aran Islands, guarding the harbour where currach boats have landed for centuries.
history: Arkin Castle (Arkyn's Castle) on Inis Mor, the largest of the Aran Islands, County Galway, is a small medieval castle near Kilronan harbour. The Aran Islands are famous for their extraordinary concentration of ancient monuments including Dun Aonghasa, the spectacular clifftop fort. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Inishmaan Castle
description: A ruined tower house on Inis Meain, the quietest Aran Island, where J.M. Synge found inspiration for his masterpiece 'The Playboy of the Western World.'
history: Inishmaan Castle on Inis Meain (the middle Aran Island) is a medieval tower house. Inis Meain was the island where playwright J.M. Synge spent summers absorbing the Irish language and island culture that inspired his great plays. The island remains the quietest and most traditional of the three Aran Islands.

name: Dun Eochla
description: A dramatically sited prehistoric stone fort on the highest point of Inis Mor, with spectacular views across the Atlantic and the Aran Islands.
history: Dun Eochla is an oval stone fort on the highest point of Inis Mor, the largest of the Aran Islands, County Galway. The fort dates to the early medieval or earlier period. Its elevated position provides dramatic 360-degree views across the island, the Atlantic Ocean, and the Connemara and Burren coastlines.

name: Derrymacloughna Castle
description: A tower house on the shores of Lough Derg in County Clare, overlooking one of the Shannon's largest and most beautiful lakes.
history: Derrymacloughna Castle is a medieval tower house on the shores of Lough Derg, County Clare. The castle dates to the medieval period and commands views over the lake. Lough Derg, the third-largest lake in Ireland, is a popular sailing and boating destination.

name: Mountshannon Castle
description: Castle remains in the pretty Clare lakeside village of Mountshannon, gateway to the monastic island of Inis Cealtra on Lough Derg.
history: Mountshannon in County Clare is a pretty planned village on the shores of Lough Derg. The village has castle remains and serves as the main departure point for boat trips to Inis Cealtra (Holy Island), one of Ireland's most important early Christian monastic sites.

name: Holy Island Inis Cealtra
description: A remarkable monastic island in Lough Derg, preserving round towers, churches, high crosses, and Viking inscriptions in one of Ireland's most sacred and atmospheric settings.
history: Inis Cealtra (Holy Island) in Lough Derg, County Clare, is one of Ireland's most important early Christian monastic sites. Founded by St Caimin in the 7th century, the island preserves a round tower, multiple churches, high crosses, and unique inscribed stones including Viking graffiti. Brian Boru's brother Marcan is buried here. Boat trips from Mountshannon provide access.

name: Ballycullen Castle
description: A tower house in the Tipperary countryside, part of the extensive network of medieval fortifications across the Golden Vale.
history: Ballycullen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of the many tower houses in the productive agricultural lands of the Golden Vale.

name: Killaleigh Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Offaly countryside, preserving architectural heritage amid the quiet midland landscape.
history: Killaleigh Church is a medieval church ruin in County Offaly. The church dates to the medieval period and stands in the quiet Offaly countryside.

name: Dunlicky Castle
description: A dramatic cliff-edge castle ruin on the Loop Head Peninsula, overlooking the wild Atlantic at the very tip of County Clare.
history: Dunlicky Castle on the Loop Head Peninsula, County Clare, stands dramatically on sea cliffs overlooking the Atlantic. The castle dates to the medieval period and commands a wild and remote coastal position at the southwestern extremity of Clare. The Loop Head Peninsula is one of Ireland's finest coastal driving routes.

name: Ballinlough Castle Roscommon
description: A fine castle and estate near Clonmellon on the Roscommon-Westmeath border, hosting music festivals in its beautiful parkland.
history: Ballinlough Castle on the Roscommon-Westmeath border is a castle estate that dates to the medieval period. The castle has been home to the same family for centuries and the estate grounds now host festivals and events including the Body & Soul music festival. The parkland and lake setting creates an atmospheric venue.

name: Shannonbridge Fortification
description: A massive Napoleonic-era artillery fortification guarding a strategic Shannon crossing, one of the most impressive military heritage sites in the midlands.
history: The Shannonbridge Fortification in County Offaly is a substantial early 19th-century military installation built to defend a strategic crossing of the River Shannon during the Napoleonic Wars. The massive fort features thick walls, gun emplacements, and defensive positions. It is one of the largest Napoleonic-era fortifications in Ireland.

name: Nendrum Monastic Site
description: See Mahee Island Nendrum—one of the most complete early Christian monastic enclosures in Ireland, on a tidal island in Strangford Lough.
history: Nendrum on Mahee Island in Strangford Lough, County Down, is one of Ireland's best-excavated early monastic sites with three concentric enclosures, church, round tower stump, and a remarkably early tidal mill (dated 619 AD).

name: Tynagh Castle
description: A tower house in east Galway's mining country, where medieval heritage meets the remains of Ireland's once-thriving mineral extraction industry.
history: Tynagh Castle is a medieval tower house in east County Galway. The area is notable for the Tynagh Mine, which was a significant lead-zinc mine in the 20th century. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the agricultural landscape of east Galway.

name: Ardrahan Castle
description: Castle remains in the south Galway village of Ardrahan, near the celebrated literary landscape of Coole Park and Thoor Ballylee.
history: Ardrahan Castle in County Galway stands near the village of Ardrahan in south Galway. The area is close to Coole Park (Lady Gregory's home) and Thoor Ballylee (Yeats' tower), making it part of the literary landscape of the Irish Revival. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Lawrencetown Castle
description: A tower house near the Galway village of Lawrencetown, in the productive farmland of east Galway.
history: Lawrencetown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the agricultural landscape of east Galway.

name: Islandmore Castle Killarney
description: A castle ruin on one of the islands in the Lakes of Killarney, accessible only by boat across the legendary waters.
history: Islandmore Castle on an island in the Lakes of Killarney, County Kerry, dates to the medieval period. The Killarney lakes and their islands have been inhabited since prehistoric times. The castle adds medieval heritage to the lakes' already extraordinary natural beauty.

name: Kilmalkedar Church
description: A remarkable Romanesque church on the Dingle Peninsula, where an alphabet stone, sundial, and ogham stones chart the evolution of writing in Ireland.
history: Kilmalkedar Church on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry, is a fine 12th-century Romanesque church. The site preserves an alphabet stone, a medieval sundial, ogham stones, and a cross-inscribed pillar, together charting the history of writing in Ireland from ogham to Latin script. The church was part of the medieval pilgrim route to Mount Brandon.

name: Reask Monastic Site
description: A tiny early Christian settlement on the Dingle Peninsula, where carved pillar stones bear some of the earliest Christian symbols in Ireland.
history: Reask Monastic Site on the Dingle Peninsula, County Kerry, is an early Christian settlement preserving decorated pillar stones with some of the earliest Christian carvings in Ireland. The small enclosure contains the remains of beehive huts, cross-slabs, and a beautifully carved pillar stone. The site exemplifies the hermitic tradition of early Irish Christianity.

name: Banna Strand Tower
description: A signal tower above the Kerry beach where Roger Casement landed from a German submarine in 1916, three days before the Easter Rising.
history: Banna Strand in County Kerry is famous as the landing place of Sir Roger Casement from a German U-boat on Good Friday 1916, three days before the Easter Rising. He was arrested nearby and later executed for treason. A memorial and signal tower mark this historic site on the long sandy beach.

name: Ballyteague Castle
description: A tower house near the banks of the River Barrow in County Kildare, overlooking the wetlands and canal heritage of the Barrow Valley.
history: Ballyteague Castle in County Kildare is a medieval tower house near the River Barrow. The castle dates to the medieval period and overlooks the low-lying Barrow Valley with its rich ecological and canal heritage.

name: Morristown Latin Castle
description: A medieval castle in the Kildare countryside, its unusual name a mystery that adds to its charm among the rolling horse-country fields.
history: Morristown Latin Castle in County Kildare is a medieval tower house. The curious 'Latin' in the name may refer to a medieval school or religious association. The castle stands in the heart of Kildare's famous horse-breeding country.

name: Jigginstown Castle
description: The vast roofless shell of an unfinished 17th-century mansion near Naas—one of the largest brick buildings attempted in Ireland, abandoned before completion.
history: Jigginstown Castle near Naas, County Kildare, was begun around 1636 by Thomas Wentworth, Earl of Strafford, Lord Deputy of Ireland. The enormous brick building was intended to host King Charles I but was never completed after Strafford's execution in 1641. The massive shell—one of the earliest large-scale brick buildings in Ireland—stands as a monument to thwarted ambition.

name: Woodstock Castle Kilkenny
description: The ruins of a Victorian mansion set in fine gardens and woodland parkland near Inistioge, with panoramic views across the Nore Valley.
history: Woodstock House near Inistioge, County Kilkenny, was a grand Victorian mansion set in 50 acres of gardens and woodland. The house was burned in 1922 during the Civil War. The gardens and arboretum have been restored and offer stunning views across the Nore Valley. The shell of the house and the restored gardens form an atmospheric heritage park.

name: Shee Alms House
description: A rare surviving medieval almshouse in the heart of Kilkenny city, one of the finest examples of medieval charitable architecture in Ireland.
history: The Shee Alms House on Rose Inn Street, Kilkenny, was founded in 1582 by Sir Richard Shee to provide accommodation for the poor. It is one of very few surviving Tudor almshouses in Ireland. The building has been beautifully restored and now serves as a tourist information office while preserving its remarkable medieval architecture.

name: Rothe House
description: A magnificent Tudor merchant's house in the heart of medieval Kilkenny—the finest surviving example of its kind in Ireland, now a museum.
history: Rothe House on Parliament Street, Kilkenny, was built between 1594 and 1610 by the Rothe family, wealthy merchants of Kilkenny. The complex of three houses connected by courtyards is the only surviving example of a Tudor merchant's townhouse in Ireland. Now a museum and genealogical centre, it preserves Kilkenny's rich medieval and Tudor heritage.

name: Coolhill Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Kilkenny countryside near Inistioge, in one of Ireland's most scenic riverside landscapes.
history: Coolhill Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny near Inistioge. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the beautiful Nore Valley landscape, one of the most scenic areas of southeast Ireland.

name: Inistioge Church
description: Medieval church remains in one of Ireland's prettiest riverside villages, complementing the heritage streetscape of this charming Kilkenny settlement.
history: Inistioge Church is part of the medieval heritage of Inistioge village, County Kilkenny. The village's medieval churches, castle, and priory, combined with its beautiful bridge and riverside setting on the Nore, make it one of Ireland's most photogenic and frequently filmed villages.

name: Castlemorris House
description: A Georgian house in the Kilkenny countryside, reflecting the elegance of 18th-century landed gentry life in southeast Ireland.
history: Castlemorris House in County Kilkenny is a Georgian country house reflecting the refined lifestyle of the 18th-century Irish landed gentry. The house stands in the productive agricultural landscape of Kilkenny.

name: Ballybur Castle
description: A substantial medieval tower house near Kilkenny city, one of the many fortified residences that ring the medieval city of the Marble City.
history: Ballybur Castle near Kilkenny city is a substantial medieval tower house. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of numerous tower houses surrounding Kilkenny, the medieval capital of the Butler lordship.

name: Ballyclogh Castle Cork
description: A well-preserved tower house near Mallow in north Cork, standing in the rich agricultural land of the Blackwater Valley.
history: Ballyclogh Castle near Mallow in County Cork is a well-preserved medieval tower house in the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century.

name: Blackwater Castle
description: A restored castle on the banks of the River Blackwater near Castletownroche, now a luxury venue for weddings and events.
history: Blackwater Castle near Castletownroche, County Cork, stands on the banks of the River Blackwater. The castle has medieval origins and has been restored as a luxury event and wedding venue. The Blackwater, often called the Irish Rhine, is one of Ireland's most scenic rivers.

name: Castlepook Cave Castle
description: Castle ruins near the significant Castlepook Cave in north Cork, where Ice Age animal remains were discovered.
history: Castlepook Castle stands near Castlepook Cave in north Cork, an important archaeological site where bones of brown bears, mammoths, and other Pleistocene fauna were discovered, some dating back 40,000 years.

name: Doneraile Court
description: A grand Georgian mansion set in the only surviving deer park in Ireland, where Edmund Spenser once walked and Canon Sheehan wrote.
history: Doneraile Court near Mallow, County Cork, is a Georgian mansion set in Doneraile Park, the only surviving deer park in Ireland. The estate has connections to Edmund Spenser (nearby Kilcolman Castle), Canon Sheehan (the novelist), and Elizabeth Aldworth (the first woman Freemason). The park features 166 hectares of parkland with herds of deer.

name: Kilmeaden Castle
description: A tower house near the Waterford city suburbs, in the valley where the famous Kilmeaden cheese was once produced.
history: Kilmeaden Castle is a medieval tower house near Kilmeaden village, County Waterford. The village lends its name to the popular Kilmeaden cheese brand. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Annestown Castle
description: A tower house near the scenic Waterford coastline, overlooking one of the Copper Coast's beautiful cove beaches.
history: Annestown Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Annestown on the Waterford coast. The area is part of the Copper Coast UNESCO Global Geopark, named for 19th-century copper mining. The dramatic coastline features sea stacks, coves, and geological formations.

name: Bourchier Castle Lough Gur
description: A tower house on the shores of Lough Gur in Limerick, within one of Ireland's most archaeologically rich landscapes.
history: Bourchier Castle on the shores of Lough Gur, County Limerick, dates to the 15th century. Lough Gur is surrounded by one of the densest concentrations of archaeological monuments in Ireland, spanning from the Neolithic to the medieval period.

name: Cappagh Castle Limerick
description: A tower house in the Limerick countryside, part of the network of medieval fortifications in the former Desmond lordship.
history: Cappagh Castle in County Limerick is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th or 16th century.

name: Kilmallock Friary
description: Extensive Dominican friary ruins in one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval walled towns, the former capital of the Earls of Desmond.
history: Kilmallock Dominican Friary in County Limerick was founded in 1291 and features extensive well-preserved ruins. Kilmallock was the medieval capital of the Earls of Desmond and retains remarkable medieval heritage including town walls, tower houses, and religious buildings. It is often called 'the Balbec of Ireland' for its rich ruins.

name: Kilmallock Castle
description: Medieval castle and town wall remains in the historic Limerick town that was the Desmond capital—Ireland's finest walled medieval town outside Dublin.
history: Kilmallock's castle and town walls are part of one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval urban landscapes. The town was the principal seat of the Earls of Desmond and retains an extraordinary collection of medieval structures.

name: Collegiate Church Kilmallock
description: An impressive medieval collegiate church in Kilmallock, one of the finest ecclesiastical buildings in Munster.
history: The Collegiate Church of SS Peter and Paul in Kilmallock, County Limerick, dates to the 13th century and is one of the finest medieval church buildings in Munster. The substantial ruins include a tower, nave, and transepts with fine architectural details.

name: Bruree Castle
description: Castle remains near the Limerick village of Bruree, boyhood home of Eamon de Valera, the dominant figure in 20th-century Irish politics.
history: Bruree in County Limerick has castle remains and is best known as the boyhood home of Eamon de Valera (1882-1975), who dominated Irish politics for over 50 years as leader of Fianna Fail, Taoiseach, and President of Ireland. A heritage centre in the old school tells his story.

name: Mungret Abbey
description: An early Christian monastic site on the outskirts of Limerick city, where two medieval churches survive from what was once a major seat of learning.
history: Mungret Abbey on the outskirts of Limerick city was an important early Christian monastery, reputedly housing 1,500 monks at its peak. Two medieval churches survive on the site. The monastery was founded in the 6th century and became one of the great seats of learning in Munster.

name: Castleconnell Castle
description: Castle remains in the pretty Limerick village of Castleconnell on the River Shannon, famous for its salmon fishing.
history: Castleconnell Castle in County Limerick stands in a scenic village on the Shannon. The village is renowned for its salmon fishing on the Shannon and its attractive riverside setting. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Thurles Castle
description: A medieval tower in the heart of Tipperary's cathedral town, where the GAA was founded in 1884, transforming Irish sport and culture.
history: Thurles Castle (also known as Bridge Castle) stands in the town where the Gaelic Athletic Association (GAA) was founded at Hayes's Hotel on 1 November 1884—one of the most significant cultural events in modern Irish history. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Burncourt Castle
description: The spectacular roofless shell of an enormous fortified house in County Tipperary, deliberately burned by its owner to deny it to Cromwell's forces.
history: Burncourt Castle (Clogheen) in County Tipperary was built by Sir Richard Everard around 1641. The massive mansion—with 26 gables and seven chimneys—was burned by Lady Everard to prevent it falling to Cromwellian forces. The impressive shell, one of the largest roofless ruins in Ireland, stands in farmland in the Galtee foothills.

name: Moorstown Castle
description: A well-preserved fortified house in the Tipperary countryside, displaying the transition from medieval castle to Tudor mansion.
history: Moorstown Castle in County Tipperary is a well-preserved fortified house dating to the early 17th century. The building displays the transition from medieval tower house to more comfortable domestic architecture. It features fine window details and retains good structural integrity.

name: Carrick-on-Suir Castle
description: See Ormond Castle—the finest Elizabethan manor house in Ireland, built by Black Tom Butler to welcome Queen Elizabeth I.
history: Carrick-on-Suir's Ormond Castle is the finest surviving Elizabethan manor in Ireland, built by the 10th Earl of Ormond. See Ormond Castle entry for full details.

name: Creagh Castle Skibbereen
description: A tower house near Skibbereen in West Cork, in the heart of the region most devastated by the Great Famine.
history: Creagh Castle near Skibbereen, County Cork, is a medieval tower house. Skibbereen was one of the areas worst affected by the Great Famine (1845-1852) and the town's heritage centre tells this devastating story. The castle predates the Famine by centuries.

name: Lough Hyne Tower
description: A signal tower above Ireland's only saltwater lake, Lough Hyne (Lough Ine), a unique marine nature reserve in West Cork.
history: The tower above Lough Hyne (Lough Ine) near Skibbereen, County Cork, is a Napoleonic-era signal tower. Lough Hyne is Ireland's first marine nature reserve and the only saltwater lake in the country, connected to the sea by a narrow rapid. The extraordinary biodiversity of the lake has attracted scientific study since the 19th century.

name: O'Brien's Castle Inis Oirr
description: A small castle on the highest point of Inis Oirr, the smallest Aran Island, with views across the Atlantic to the Cliffs of Moher.
history: O'Brien's Castle on Inis Oirr (Inisheer), the smallest of the Aran Islands, County Galway, dates to the 14th century and was built by the O'Brien clan within an older ringfort. The castle occupies the highest point on the island with spectacular views to the Cliffs of Moher and the Burren.

name: Magheraghanrush Court Tomb
description: A Neolithic court tomb in County Sligo, part of one of Europe's densest concentrations of megalithic monuments.
history: Magheraghanrush Court Tomb in County Sligo is a Neolithic court tomb dating to around 3500 BC. County Sligo has one of the highest concentrations of megalithic monuments in Europe, reflecting its importance in Neolithic times.

name: Aghmore Castle Leitrim
description: A small castle ruin in the quiet Leitrim countryside, in Ireland's most sparsely populated county.
history: Aghmore Castle in County Leitrim is a medieval fortification in the quiet rural landscape of Ireland's least populated county.

name: Castlecaldwell Castle
description: A ruined castle on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, now a forest park famous for its fiddler's stone and birdwatching opportunities.
history: Castlecaldwell on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, County Fermanagh, is a ruined castle now within a forest park. The estate is famous for the Fiddler's Stone, commemorating a musician who drowned after falling off a boat while playing. The area is excellent for birdwatching with common scoter ducks nesting on the lake islands.

name: Errigal Keerogue Cross
description: A fine early Christian carved cross in County Tyrone, preserving intricate patterns from the golden age of Irish art.
history: Errigal Keerogue in County Tyrone preserves a fine early Christian cross and church remains. The cross features intricate carvings characteristic of the golden age of Irish ecclesiastical art (8th-10th centuries). The rural setting in mid-Tyrone adds to the atmospheric quality.

name: Tullaghoge Fort
description: The ancient inauguration site of the O'Neill kings of Tyrone, destroyed by Mountjoy in 1602 to symbolically end Gaelic sovereignty.
history: Tullaghoge Fort near Cookstown, County Tyrone, was the inauguration site of the O'Neill dynasty. Here new chieftains were crowned on a sacred stone chair. In 1602, Lord Mountjoy deliberately destroyed the stone chair to symbolically end O'Neill authority. The hilltop fort remains an evocative site of enormous cultural significance.

name: Caledon Castle
description: A grand estate in County Tyrone, home to the Earl of Caledon, surrounded by parkland and the picturesque village of Caledon.
history: Caledon Castle in County Tyrone is the seat of the Earl of Caledon. The grand Georgian house is set in extensive parkland. The nearby village of Caledon is one of Northern Ireland's prettiest planned estate villages.

name: Donaghmore High Cross
description: A magnificent reconstructed high cross in the Tyrone village of Donaghmore, featuring intricate biblical scenes carved in stone.
history: The High Cross at Donaghmore, County Tyrone, is a fine early Christian cross reconstructed from two fragments. The cross features detailed biblical scenes including the Fall of Adam and Eve. Donaghmore also preserves the remains of a monastery founded by St Patrick.

name: Ardboe High Cross
description: One of the finest high crosses in Ulster, standing 5.5 metres tall on the western shore of Lough Neagh with 22 carved biblical panels.
history: The Ardboe High Cross on the shores of Lough Neagh, County Tyrone, is one of the best-preserved high crosses in Northern Ireland. Standing 5.5 metres tall, it features 22 carved panels depicting Old and New Testament scenes. The cross dates to the 10th century and stands beside an early Christian church site.

name: Gosford Castle
description: A spectacular Norman Revival castle in County Armagh, designed by Thomas Hopper—one of the largest and most dramatic 19th-century castles in Ireland.
history: Gosford Castle near Markethill, County Armagh, was built between 1819 and 1850 by Thomas Hopper for the Acheson family (Earls of Gosford) in a dramatic Norman Revival style. The massive castle, one of the largest 19th-century castles in Ireland, was used as a military base during WWII. It has been converted into apartments in recent years.

name: Ballymoyer Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Armagh countryside, in the rolling drumlin landscape of south Armagh.
history: Ballymoyer Church is a medieval church ruin in County Armagh, in the quiet rural landscape of south Armagh.

name: Duleek Priory
description: Medieval priory ruins in one of Ireland's oldest Christian settlements, where St Patrick is said to have built the first stone church in Ireland.
history: Duleek in County Meath is one of Ireland's earliest Christian settlements. St Patrick reportedly established the first stone church in Ireland here. The priory ruins date to the 12th century and include fine architectural details. Duleek is also notable for its medieval wayside cross.

name: Dowth Hall
description: A Georgian mansion near the famous Dowth passage tomb in the Boyne Valley, in the heart of one of Europe's most important archaeological landscapes.
history: Dowth Hall in County Meath stands near Dowth passage tomb, one of the three great Neolithic tombs of the Boyne Valley (alongside Newgrange and Knowth). The Georgian hall is part of a later landscape layer in this extraordinarily multi-period heritage zone.

name: Moone High Cross
description: One of the tallest and most remarkable high crosses in Ireland, its slender profile decorated with delightfully naive biblical scenes.
history: The Moone High Cross in County Kildare is one of the tallest high crosses in Ireland at over 5 metres. Dating to the 8th-9th century, the cross features charming, somewhat naive carved panels depicting scenes including Daniel in the Lions' Den and the Flight into Egypt. The distinctive granite cross is housed in the ruins of an early Christian monastery.

name: Old Kilcullen Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and high cross fragments on a hilltop in Kildare, site of an important early Christian monastery.
history: Old Kilcullen in County Kildare preserves a round tower stump and high cross fragments on a hilltop marking the site of an important early Christian monastery. The site offers wide views across the Kildare plains.

name: Seaforde Butterfly House
description: A tropical butterfly house and maze in the grounds of a historic County Down estate, combining natural history with heritage gardens.
history: Seaforde in County Down is home to the Seaforde Butterfly House and Tropical Gardens, set in the grounds of the historic Seaforde estate. The gardens feature a hornbeam maze and tropical greenhouse with free-flying butterflies.

name: Ballycopeland Windmill
description: The only surviving complete working windmill in Ireland, beautifully preserved near the County Down coast.
history: Ballycopeland Windmill near Millisle, County Down, is the only surviving complete working windmill in Ireland. Built around 1790, the windmill operated until 1915. It has been restored to working order and is open to visitors.

name: Tory Island Round Tower
description: A round tower on Ireland's most remote inhabited island, where an ancient Celtic-speaking community maintains traditions stretching back millennia.
history: Tory Island off the Donegal coast preserves a round tower and early Christian monastic remains. The island has been continuously inhabited for millennia and maintains a unique culture including its own 'king.' The island is also famous for its school of naive painters, founded in the 1950s.

name: Carrigart Castle
description: Castle remains in the Donegal coastal village of Carrigart, gateway to the wild Rosguill Peninsula and the Atlantic Drive.
history: Carrigart Castle in County Donegal stands in the village that serves as gateway to the Rosguill Peninsula and its famous Atlantic Drive, one of Ireland's most spectacular coastal roads.

name: Tullydonnell Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Inishowen Peninsula, County Donegal, in Ireland's most northerly landscape.
history: Tullydonnell Castle on the Inishowen Peninsula, County Donegal, is a medieval tower house in Ireland's most northerly region.

name: Clonca Church
description: A medieval church in Inishowen, Donegal, featuring a remarkable carved tombstone with a sword, hurley, and ball—the earliest known depiction of hurling.
history: Clonca Church in Inishowen, County Donegal, preserves a remarkable medieval tombstone featuring a carved sword, hurley stick, and ball—believed to be the earliest known depiction of the Irish sport of hurling. The church also features a fine carved lintel and high cross.

name: Dunmoran Castle
description: A coastal castle ruin near Mullaghmore in County Sligo, in the dramatic landscape of the north Sligo coast.
history: Dunmoran Castle near Mullaghmore, County Sligo, is a medieval fortification on the dramatic north Sligo coast.

name: Mullaghmore Castle
description: A coastal castle in the Sligo resort village of Mullaghmore, beneath the dramatic mountain of Benbulben.
history: Mullaghmore in County Sligo has castle remains near the harbour. The village is known for its connection to Lord Mountbatten, who was killed by an IRA bomb in 1979. The dramatic coastline beneath Benbulben mountain provides a spectacular setting.

name: Cruachan Ai Visitor Centre
description: An interpretive centre at Tulsk explaining the extraordinary Rathcroghan archaeological complex—ancient capital of Connacht's kings.
history: The Cruachan Ai Visitor Centre at Tulsk, County Roscommon, interprets the remarkable Rathcroghan archaeological complex nearby, including the royal mound, ringforts, and Oweynagat cave—the legendary entrance to the Celtic Otherworld.

name: Cloonfree Castle
description: A tower house in the Roscommon countryside, one of the scattered medieval fortifications in the Irish midlands.
history: Cloonfree Castle is a medieval tower house in County Roscommon.

name: Shanagolden Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins in the west Limerick village of Shanagolden, near the Shannon Estuary.
history: Shanagolden Friary in County Limerick was a Franciscan foundation near the Shannon Estuary. The atmospheric ruins include church and domestic building remains.

name: Lough Rynn Castle
description: A beautifully restored Victorian castle on the shores of Lough Rynn in Leitrim, now a luxury hotel set in extensive parkland.
history: Lough Rynn Castle near Mohill, County Leitrim, was the seat of the Clements family (Earls of Leitrim). The Victorian castle is set on the shores of Lough Rynn in beautiful parkland. It has been restored as a luxury hotel and wedding venue.

name: Cloone Round Tower
description: A round tower stump in the quiet Leitrim village of Cloone, marking an early Christian monastic foundation.
history: The Round Tower at Cloone, County Leitrim, is the stump of an early Christian round tower marking a monastic site. The quiet village preserves this heritage in the gentle Leitrim landscape.

name: Cavan Burren Park
description: A remarkable megalithic landscape in County Cavan, featuring wedge tombs, standing stones, and geological formations in a scenic forest park.
history: Cavan Burren Park near Blacklion, County Cavan, is a remarkable landscape of megalithic tombs, standing stones, and natural geological features in a forest setting. The park features a network of trails leading to wedge tombs dating to the Bronze Age and other archaeological monuments.

name: Ballyjamesduff Castle
description: Castle remains in the Cavan market town of Ballyjamesduff, named after James Duff who received the lands in the 17th century.
history: Ballyjamesduff in County Cavan has castle remains. The town is named after Captain James Duff, who received lands here during the Cromwellian settlement. The Cavan County Museum in the town holds significant archaeological collections.

name: Ballyhaise House
description: A fine Palladian house in County Cavan, designed by Richard Cassels, now serving as a campus for Dundalk Institute of Technology.
history: Ballyhaise House in County Cavan was designed by the architect Richard Cassels (Castle) around 1733. The fine Palladian house now serves as a campus for DKIT (Dundalk Institute of Technology) agricultural college.

name: Hope Castle Castleblayney
description: A castellated mansion in the Monaghan town of Castleblayney, set in the beautiful Lough Muckno parkland.
history: Hope Castle in Castleblayney, County Monaghan, was the seat of the Blayney and Hope families. The castellated mansion overlooks Lough Muckno and its parkland is now a popular recreational area with walking trails and fishing.

name: Dartrey Estate
description: A ruined mansion in the Monaghan countryside, set in extensive demesne parkland beside the shores of Dartrey Lake.
history: Dartrey Estate near Rockcorry, County Monaghan, was the seat of the Dawson family (Earls of Dartrey). The mansion has fallen into ruin but the extensive parkland and estate landscape remain. The grounds include fine mature woodland and a lake.

name: Inniskeen Round Tower
description: A round tower stump in the Monaghan village of Inniskeen, birthplace of Patrick Kavanagh, one of Ireland's greatest poets.
history: The Round Tower at Inniskeen, County Monaghan, marks an early Christian monastic site. The village is most famous as the birthplace of Patrick Kavanagh (1904-1967), whose poetry celebrating the ordinary beauty of rural Monaghan ('On Raglan Road', 'The Great Hunger') made him one of Ireland's greatest poets. A museum tells his story.

name: Lough Oughter Castle Island
description: See Clogh Oughter Castle—a hauntingly beautiful circular tower on an island in Lough Oughter, the last castle to fall to Cromwell.
history: See Clogh Oughter Castle entry. The circular castle rising from the island in Lough Oughter is one of Ireland's most atmospheric and photogenic ruins.

name: Banagher Old Church Derry
description: A remarkable early Christian church ruin in County Derry with fine stone carvings and a rare medieval tomb shrine.
history: Banagher Old Church near Dungiven, County Derry, is an early Christian church site with fine stone carvings. The nearby Dungiven Priory contains the tomb of Cooey na Gall O'Cahan, one of the finest medieval tomb shrines in Ireland, featuring carved gallowglass warriors.

name: Bovevagh Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Roe Valley of County Derry, in a beautiful area of woodland, rivers, and historical heritage.
history: Bovevagh Church is a medieval church ruin in the Roe Valley, County Derry. The Roe Valley is a scenic area of woodland and rivers, now largely a country park. The church dates to the medieval period.

name: Springhill House
description: A charming 17th-century plantation house in County Derry, one of the National Trust's most atmospheric properties in Northern Ireland.
history: Springhill House near Moneymore, County Derry, is a 17th-century plantation house built by the Conyngham family. The charming whitewashed house features original furniture, a costume collection, and a reputed ghost. It has been a National Trust property since 1957 and is one of the most atmospheric historic houses in Northern Ireland.

name: Killough Towers
description: Tower remains in the County Down coastal village of Killough, a planned estate town on the shores of the Irish Sea.
history: Killough in County Down is a charming planned estate village with tower remains and a small harbour. The village was developed as a port by the Ward family of Castle Ward in the 18th century.

name: Struell Wells
description: A remarkable group of medieval bath houses fed by holy wells near Downpatrick, associated with St Patrick and ancient healing traditions.
history: Struell Wells near Downpatrick, County Down, is a group of medieval bath houses fed by holy wells. Associated with St Patrick, the wells were famous for their healing properties and attracted pilgrims for centuries. The site includes drinking well, eye well, and separate bath houses for men and women.

name: Saul Church
description: The site where St Patrick established his first church in Ireland after landing at nearby Strangford Lough in 432 AD.
history: Saul, County Down, is where St Patrick established his first church in Ireland around 432 AD. A modern church and round tower were built on the site in 1932 to mark the 1,500th anniversary. The original church was a barn ('sabal') given to Patrick by Dichu, a local chieftain whom he converted.

name: Downpatrick Cathedral
description: The burial place of St Patrick, Ireland's patron saint, where a granite slab marks the traditional grave of the man who brought Christianity to Ireland.
history: Down Cathedral in Downpatrick, County Down, is the traditional burial place of St Patrick. A granite slab placed in 1900 marks where Ireland's patron saint is believed to be buried. The cathedral has medieval origins though substantially rebuilt. SS Brigid and Columcille are also traditionally said to be buried here.

name: Dromore Mound
description: A large Norman motte in the Down town of Dromore, one of the finest examples of a Norman earthwork in Ulster.
history: Dromore Mound in County Down is one of the largest and best-preserved Norman mottes in Ulster. The impressive earthwork was constructed during the Norman advance into Down. The town of Dromore also has a medieval high cross and cathedral.

name: Rathlin Island Ruins
description: Heritage ruins on Ireland's most northerly inhabited island, where Robert the Bruce famously watched a spider and resolved to fight on.
history: Rathlin Island off the Antrim coast is Ireland's most northerly inhabited island. It features various ruins and is famous as the place where Robert the Bruce, hiding in a cave in 1306, watched a spider repeatedly try to spin its web—inspiring him to continue his struggle for Scottish independence. The island is also an important seabird sanctuary.

name: Cushendun Caves Castle
description: Heritage features near the remarkable sea caves of Cushendun, a charming Antrim coastal village designed by Clough Williams-Ellis.
history: Cushendun in the Glens of Antrim features sea caves (used as a Game of Thrones filming location) and heritage buildings. The village was designed by architect Clough Williams-Ellis (creator of Portmeirion in Wales) in a distinctive Cornish style for the Cushendun estate.

name: Dunserverick Castle
description: Dramatic cliff-edge castle ruins on the Antrim coast near the Giant's Causeway, where the road from Tara to the sea once ended.
history: Dunserverick Castle on the Antrim coast near the Giant's Causeway stands on a dramatic cliff-top promontory. The site was occupied from the 5th century and the ruined castle dates mainly to the medieval period. It stood on the ancient route from Tara to the sea. Only fragments remain but the coastal setting is spectacular.

name: Randalstown Castle
description: Castle remains in the Antrim town of Randalstown, in the lush valley of the River Maine near Lough Neagh.
history: Randalstown Castle in County Antrim was associated with the O'Neill family. The town sits on the River Maine near Lough Neagh. The surrounding Randalstown Forest provides walking trails and wildlife habitat.

name: Massereene Castle
description: Castle remains in the Antrim town of Antrim, near the shores of Lough Neagh, Ireland's largest lake.
history: Massereene Castle in the town of Antrim was the seat of the Viscount Massereene. The castle grounds, now part of Antrim Castle Gardens, have been restored as fine heritage gardens open to the public. Antrim sits on the shores of Lough Neagh, the largest lake in Britain and Ireland.

name: Ballintoy Church
description: A charming church near the Antrim coast at Ballintoy Harbour, a Game of Thrones filming location with spectacular sea views.
history: Ballintoy on the North Antrim coast features a charming church near the famous harbour used as a filming location for Game of Thrones (the Iron Islands). The dramatic coastal scenery, including Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge nearby, makes this one of the most spectacular stretches of Irish coastline.

name: Layde Church
description: Medieval church ruins in a scenic clifftop graveyard near Cushendall in the Glens of Antrim, with views across the sea to Scotland.
history: Layde Church near Cushendall in the Glens of Antrim is a medieval church ruin in a beautiful clifftop graveyard overlooking the sea. The church dates to the 13th century and contains MacDonnell family tombs. On clear days, Scotland is visible across the narrow strait.

name: Greyabbey House
description: A Cistercian abbey ruin and associated house in the County Down village of Greyabbey, one of the finest medieval monastic remains in Ulster.
history: Greyabbey in County Down preserves the ruins of a Cistercian abbey founded in 1193 by Affreca, wife of John de Courcy, in thanks for a safe sea crossing. The abbey features one of the first examples of Gothic architecture in Ireland. The atmospheric ruins sit in landscaped grounds.

name: Drogheda Magdalene Tower
description: The dramatic bell tower of a medieval Dominican friary in the heart of Drogheda, one of the most striking medieval monuments in the Boyne Valley.
history: The Magdalene Tower in Drogheda, County Louth, is the 14th-century bell tower of a Dominican friary. It is one of the most prominent medieval monuments in Drogheda, a town with rich medieval heritage including St Laurence's Gate and several medieval churches.

name: Millmount Fort Drogheda
description: A historic motte-and-bailey fortification overlooking Drogheda, now a museum complex telling the story of this ancient Boyne Valley town.
history: Millmount Fort in Drogheda, County Louth, is a prominent motte on the south bank of the Boyne. The Martello tower on top was built during the Napoleonic era on the site of a much older fortification. The complex now houses the Millmount Museum, telling the rich history of Drogheda from prehistoric to modern times.

name: Ballymacpeake Castle
description: A tower house in the Derry countryside, one of the medieval fortifications in the Sperrin Mountains region.
history: Ballymacpeake Castle is a medieval tower house in County Derry near the Sperrin Mountains.

name: Inishowen Head Tower
description: A signal tower at the very tip of the Inishowen Peninsula, Ireland's most northerly point, overlooking the wild Atlantic.
history: Inishowen Head Tower is a Napoleonic-era signal tower near Malin Head on the Inishowen Peninsula, Ireland's most northerly mainland point. The tower was part of the coastal defence system. The dramatic coastal scenery and the sense of being at the edge of the world make this a compelling destination.

name: Ballintotis Castle
description: A tower house in east Cork, in the fertile agricultural countryside between Castlemartyr and Midleton.
history: Ballintotis Castle is a medieval tower house in east County Cork. The castle dates to the 15th century and stands in productive farmland.

name: Kilgobbin Castle Cork
description: A tower house in the Cork countryside, one of the many medieval fortifications scattered across the county.
history: Kilgobbin Castle in County Cork is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th century.

name: Ballynacorra Castle
description: A tower house near Midleton in east Cork, in the area famous for its whiskey distilling heritage.
history: Ballynacorra Castle is a medieval tower house near Midleton, County Cork, famous for Jameson Irish Whiskey. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Dromana Gate
description: One of the most extraordinary follies in Ireland—a spectacular Hindu-Gothic gate lodge spanning the entrance to the Dromana estate above the Blackwater.
history: The Dromana Gate near Cappoquin, County Waterford, is one of Ireland's most remarkable architectural follies—a stunning Hindu-Gothic gateway built in 1849, inspired by the Brighton Pavilion. The exotic gate leads to the Dromana estate overlooking the River Blackwater. It was built as a temporary decoration for the return of the estate owners but proved so popular it was made permanent.

name: Kilbarry Castle Waterford
description: A tower house near Waterford city, now surrounded by the expanding suburbs of Ireland's oldest city.
history: Kilbarry Castle is a medieval tower house near Waterford city. The castle dates to the medieval period and once stood in open countryside that has been absorbed by Waterford's suburban expansion.

name: Coolhull Castle Wexford
description: A well-preserved tower house in the Wexford countryside, retaining its original features and defensive character.
history: Coolhull Castle in County Wexford is a well-preserved medieval tower house retaining many original features.

name: Mayglass Tower
description: A tower house in the south Wexford countryside, in the Barony of Forth where the ancient Yola language survived until the 19th century.
history: Mayglass Tower is a medieval tower house in south County Wexford, in the historic Barony of Forth where the ancient Yola language (a form of Norman French-English) survived into the 19th century.

name: Kilcloggan Castle
description: A tower house in the Wexford countryside near the coast, part of the dense Norman settlement of southeast Ireland.
history: Kilcloggan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Wexford.

name: Lady's Island Castle
description: Castle ruins on a tidal island in County Wexford, a major medieval pilgrimage site still visited by devotees each August.
history: Lady's Island in County Wexford has castle and church ruins on a tidal island in a lake. The island has been a major Marian pilgrimage site since the medieval period, with annual pilgrimages still taking place each August. The castle was an Anglo-Norman fortification.

name: Selskar Abbey
description: A substantial Augustinian abbey in the heart of Wexford town, where Henry II did penance for the murder of Thomas Becket.
history: Selskar Abbey in Wexford town was an Augustinian foundation where Henry II of England spent Lent 1172 doing penance for the murder of Thomas Becket. The substantial ruins stand in the town centre. The abbey was an important religious house throughout the medieval period.

name: Ferrycarrig Castle
description: A Norman tower on a rocky promontory at the mouth of the River Slaney, near the Irish National Heritage Park.
history: Ferrycarrig Castle near Wexford town stands on a rocky promontory at the mouth of the River Slaney. The Norman tower dates to the 12th century. Nearby is the Irish National Heritage Park, which recreates 9,000 years of Irish settlement from the Mesolithic to the Norman period.

name: Moyglare Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath-Kildare borderlands, in the heart of Ireland's finest horse-breeding country.
history: Moyglare Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near Maynooth. The area is famous for thoroughbred horse breeding, with the renowned Moyglare Stud nearby.

name: Lackan Castle
description: A tower house in the Westmeath midlands, part of the medieval fortified landscape of the Irish central plain.
history: Lackan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath.

name: Tristernagh Abbey
description: Augustinian priory ruins near the shores of Lough Iron in Westmeath, in the quiet lake country of the Irish midlands.
history: Tristernagh Abbey near Lough Iron, County Westmeath, was an Augustinian priory. The ruins stand near the lake in the quiet Westmeath midlands.

name: Dowdstown Castle Meath
description: A tower house in the rich Meath countryside, part of the medieval infrastructure of the English Pale.
history: Dowdstown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, part of the Pale's defensive network.

name: Rathmullan Castle Meath
description: A tower house in County Meath, in the historically rich landscape of the Boyne Valley.
history: Rathmullan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near the Boyne Valley.

name: Drumconrath Castle
description: Castle remains in the north Meath village of Drumconrath, near the border with County Louth.
history: Drumconrath Castle is a medieval fortification in County Meath.

name: Proleek Dolmen
description: A remarkable portal tomb near Dundalk with an enormous capstone balanced impossibly on tiny supports—legend says throwing a stone that stays on top grants a wish.
history: The Proleek Dolmen near Dundalk, County Louth, is a spectacular portal tomb dating to around 3000 BC. The enormous capstone (estimated at 40 tonnes) appears to balance impossibly on tiny point supports. Legend says if you can throw a pebble that stays on the capstone, your wish will come true.

name: Annagassan Viking Fort
description: The site of the first recorded Viking longphort in Ireland, where Norse raiders established a permanent base on the Louth coast in 841 AD.
history: Annagassan in County Louth is the site of the longphort of Linn Duachaill, one of the first permanent Viking settlements in Ireland, established in 841 AD. Archaeological excavations have revealed the extent of this important Norse base. The site is on the coast between Drogheda and Dundalk.

name: Kildemock Church Jumping
description: The 'Jumping Church' of Kildemock near Ardee—a medieval church ruin where one wall has inexplicably moved from its foundation, seemingly jumping inward.
history: The 'Jumping Church' of Kildemock near Ardee, County Louth, is a medieval church ruin famous for a wall that has mysteriously shifted from its foundation, leaning inward at an angle. Legend says the wall 'jumped' to exclude the grave of an excommunicate buried within the church. The phenomenon is likely due to subsidence but the mystery endures.

name: Ballyfin Demesne
description: Ireland's most luxurious country house hotel, a magnificent Regency mansion set in 614 acres of parkland, lakes, and formal gardens.
history: Ballyfin Demesne in County Laois is a grand Regency mansion built in the 1820s for Sir Charles Coote. The house was designed by Dominick Madden and Richard Morrison and features extraordinary interiors. After years as a school, it was restored and reopened in 2011 as Ireland's most exclusive country house hotel, set in 614 acres of parkland.

name: Sharavogue Castle
description: A tower house in the Offaly countryside, one of the medieval fortifications in the midland bogland landscape.
history: Sharavogue Castle is a medieval tower house in County Offaly.

name: Fancroft Mill Castle
description: A tower house near Roscrea in County Tipperary, with an associated mill that reflects the agricultural heritage of the area.
history: Fancroft Mill Castle near Roscrea, County Tipperary, combines a medieval tower house with a mill, reflecting the agricultural and commercial heritage of the area.

name: Gormanston Castle
description: A grand castle and estate on the Meath coast, now a Franciscan college, seat of the Viscounts Gormanston who hold Ireland's oldest surviving peerage.
history: Gormanston Castle in County Meath is the seat of the Viscounts Gormanston, holders of Ireland's oldest surviving peerage (created 1478). Legend says foxes gather and bark at the castle when a Viscount dies. The estate now houses a Franciscan school. The castle combines medieval and later elements.

name: Painestown Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Meath countryside, in the rich archaeological hinterland of the Boyne Valley.
history: Painestown Church is a medieval church ruin in County Meath. The church stands in the archaeologically rich landscape of the Boyne Valley.

name: Piercetown Castle
description: A tower house in the Wexford countryside, part of the dense Norman settlement pattern of southeast Leinster.
history: Piercetown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Wexford.

name: Ballylaneen Castle
description: A tower house in the Waterford countryside between Dungarvan and Tramore.
history: Ballylaneen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford.

name: Clonea Castle Waterford
description: A tower house near the popular Clonea Strand beach in County Waterford.
history: Clonea Castle is a medieval tower house near Clonea Strand, one of Waterford's finest beaches.

name: Knockmealdown Castle
description: A fortification in the shadow of the Knockmealdown Mountains on the Waterford-Tipperary border.
history: Knockmealdown Castle is near the Knockmealdown Mountains, a scenic range on the Waterford-Tipperary border.

name: Freshford Church
description: A medieval church in the Kilkenny village of Freshford, with a fine Romanesque doorway.
history: Freshford Church in County Kilkenny features a notable Romanesque doorway dating to the 12th century.

name: Clogh Castle Kilkenny
description: A tower house in north Kilkenny, near the border with County Laois.
history: Clogh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny.

name: Ballyshannon Castle
description: Castle remains in the historic Donegal town of Ballyshannon, Ireland's oldest town and birthplace of Rory Gallagher.
history: Ballyshannon in County Donegal claims to be the oldest town in Ireland, with settlement dating back to the Neolithic. The town has castle remains and is famous as the birthplace of blues-rock legend Rory Gallagher (1948-1995). An annual music festival celebrates his legacy.

name: Horn Head Tower
description: A signal tower on the spectacular sea cliffs of Horn Head, one of Donegal's most dramatic headlands.
history: Horn Head signal tower stands on one of Donegal's most dramatic headlands near Dunfanaghy. The Napoleonic-era tower perches on cliffs over 200 metres high with views across the Atlantic. The headland is one of the finest coastal walks in Ireland.

name: Killydonnell Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins near Rathmullan on the shores of Lough Swilly, in the historically rich Donegal landscape.
history: Killydonnell Friary near Rathmullan, County Donegal, was a Franciscan foundation on the shores of Lough Swilly. The atmospheric ruins stand in a quiet coastal setting.

name: Magh Adair
description: The ancient inauguration mound of the Dalcassian kings near Tulla in County Clare, where Brian Boru was crowned King of Thomond.
history: Magh Adair near Tulla, County Clare, is the ancient inauguration mound of the Dalcassian kings of Thomond. Brian Boru, who became High King of Ireland, was crowned here in the 10th century. The mound and surrounding earthworks mark one of the most historically significant royal sites in Munster.

name: Inchicronan Church
description: A medieval church on an island in a lake near Crusheen, County Clare, in an atmospheric lakeland setting.
history: Inchicronan Church stands on or near a lake island near Crusheen, County Clare. The ecclesiastical site dates to the early Christian period.

name: Aillwee Cave Tower
description: Heritage features near the famous Aillwee Cave in the Burren, where a show cave reveals the limestone underworld of this unique landscape.
history: Aillwee Cave in the Burren, County Clare, is a show cave featuring stalactites, underground rivers, and the remains of brown bear hibernation nests. The visitor centre includes a birds of prey centre. The cave was discovered by a farmer in 1944.

name: Cahercommaun Fort
description: A spectacular triple-walled stone fort perched on a cliff edge in the Burren, where ancient builders chose one of Ireland's most dramatic natural positions.
history: Cahercommaun Fort in the Burren, County Clare, is a dramatic triple-walled stone fort perched on the edge of an inland cliff. Excavated in the 1930s by Harvard archaeologists, the fort yielded Viking silver and evidence of early medieval habitation. The cliff-edge setting is one of the most spectacular in the Burren.

name: Ballycorick Castle
description: A tower house in the Clare countryside, one of the many medieval fortifications in this county of extraordinary castle density.
history: Ballycorick Castle is a medieval tower house in County Clare.

name: O'Dea Castle Museum
description: A restored tower house near Corofin housing the Clare Heritage Centre, where the pivotal 1318 Battle of Dysert O'Dea ended Norman power in Clare.
history: O'Dea Castle (Dysert O'Dea) near Corofin, County Clare, is a restored tower house housing the Clare Heritage Centre. The nearby battlefield of Dysert O'Dea (1318), where Conor O'Dea defeated Richard de Clare, ended Norman expansion into Thomond and preserved Clare's Gaelic character for centuries.

name: Spanish Point Tower
description: A tower near Spanish Point on the Clare coast, where Spanish Armada sailors were shipwrecked and buried in 1588.
history: Spanish Point on the Clare coast is named for the Spanish Armada sailors who were shipwrecked here in September 1588. Many survivors were executed by the English sheriff. A tower and memorial mark this tragic chapter in the Armada story.

name: Abbeydorney Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins in the north Kerry village of Abbeydorney, one of the most substantial medieval monastic remains in the county.
history: Abbeydorney Abbey in County Kerry was a Cistercian foundation dating to 1154. The substantial ruins include church and domestic building remains. The abbey is one of the most significant medieval monastic sites in north Kerry.

name: Castlecove Castle
description: A tower house near the village of Castlecove on the Ring of Kerry, in one of Ireland's most spectacular coastal landscapes.
history: Castlecove Castle is a medieval tower house near Castlecove on the Iveragh Peninsula (Ring of Kerry), County Kerry. The castle stands in one of Ireland's most scenic coastal landscapes, near the famous Staigue Fort.

name: Ballybunnion Castle
description: Dramatic castle ruins perched on the cliffs above Ballybunnion's famous beach and world-renowned golf links on the Kerry coast.
history: Ballybunnion Castle stands on the cliffs above Ballybunnion, County Kerry, famous for its beach and world-class golf links (Bill Clinton is a regular visitor). The castle dates to the medieval period and its cliff-edge position creates a dramatic landmark visible from the beach.

name: Killenaule Castle
description: A tower in the south Tipperary town of Killenaule, in the productive agricultural heartland of the Golden Vale.
history: Killenaule Castle is a medieval tower in County Tipperary.

name: Castle Otway
description: A castellated mansion in the Tipperary countryside near Templemore, surrounded by parkland in the shadow of the Devil's Bit mountain.
history: Castle Otway in County Tipperary is a castellated mansion near Templemore. The estate is set in parkland near the Devil's Bit mountain.

name: Newcastle Tipperary Castle
description: Castle remains in the Tipperary village of Newcastle, in the foothills of the Galtee Mountains.
history: Newcastle Castle in County Tipperary stands in a village in the foothills of the Galtee Mountains, Ireland's highest inland range.

name: Ballynaclogh Castle
description: A tower house in north Tipperary, in the area between the Devil's Bit and Silvermines mountains.
history: Ballynaclogh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary.

name: Ballylooby Castle
description: A tower house in the south Tipperary countryside near the Knockmealdown Mountains.
history: Ballylooby Castle is a medieval tower house near the Knockmealdown Mountains in County Tipperary.

name: Carriggundel Castle
description: A medieval castle near the Shannon in County Limerick, guarding a crossing of Ireland's longest river.
history: Carriggundel Castle in County Limerick is a medieval fortification near the River Shannon.

name: Kildare Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the county town of Kildare, famous for its cathedral, round tower, and ancient connection to St Brigid.
history: Kildare town preserves castle remains, a fine cathedral with a round tower that can be climbed, and the heritage of St Brigid, one of Ireland's patron saints. The town is also the heart of Ireland's thoroughbred horse-breeding industry.

name: Ballymore Eustace Castle
description: A tower house in the picturesque Kildare village of Ballymore Eustace on the River Liffey.
history: Ballymore Eustace Castle in County Kildare stands in a charming village on the River Liffey. The castle dates to the medieval period. The village's riverside setting and proximity to the Poulaphouca Reservoir make it a scenic spot.

name: Leixlip Castle
description: A fine Norman castle in the Kildare town of Leixlip, at the confluence of the Liffey and Rye rivers—one of the oldest inhabited castles in Ireland.
history: Leixlip Castle in County Kildare was built around 1172 by Adam de Hereford and is one of the oldest inhabited castles in Ireland. The town name comes from the Norse 'Lax hlaup' (salmon leap). The castle is in private ownership and was famously home to Desmond Guinness, founder of the Irish Georgian Society.

name: Tullaroan Castle
description: A tower house near the Kilkenny village of Tullaroan, famous as the heartland of hurling in Ireland.
history: Tullaroan Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Tullaroan, County Kilkenny. Tullaroan is famous in the GAA world as one of the great heartlands of hurling, having produced numerous All-Ireland champions.

name: Castle Gore Mayo
description: A ruined Big House in County Mayo, one of the many grand mansions that fell into decay during the 20th century.
history: Castle Gore in County Mayo was a substantial country house that fell into ruin during the 20th century.

name: Ballinafad Castle
description: A substantial plantation castle in County Sligo, built to control the strategic route between Sligo and Roscommon.
history: Ballinafad Castle near Boyle, County Sligo, was built around 1619 by Captain St Barbe as a plantation castle to control the strategic Curlew Pass between Sligo and Roscommon. The substantial rectangular castle with corner towers is well preserved.

name: Clonroad Castle
description: Castle remains near Ennis, the county town of Clare, in one of Ireland's most vibrant traditional music centres.
history: Clonroad Castle near Ennis, County Clare, dates to the medieval period. Ennis is famous as one of Ireland's premier traditional music towns.

name: Lorrha Priory
description: Augustinian priory and Dominican friary ruins in the heritage village of Lorrha, north Tipperary, one of Ireland's most important medieval ecclesiastical sites.
history: Lorrha in north Tipperary preserves the ruins of both an Augustinian priory and a Dominican friary, making it one of the most significant medieval ecclesiastical sites in Munster. The monastery has origins in the 6th century and produced the Stowe Missal, an important early Irish manuscript.

name: Fennor Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath countryside.
history: Fennor Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath.

name: Abington Castle
description: A tower house in the Limerick countryside near Murroe.
history: Abington Castle is a medieval tower house in County Limerick near Murroe.

name: Any Castle
description: A small tower in the Limerick countryside.
history: Any Castle is a medieval fortification in County Limerick.

name: McKenna's Castle
description: A fortification in the Ulster borderlands associated with the McKenna clan.
history: McKenna's Castle is a fortification associated with the McKenna clan in the Ulster border region.

name: Mothel Abbey
description: Medieval abbey ruins in the Waterford countryside.
history: Mothel Abbey is a medieval monastic site in County Waterford.

name: Kilkieran High Crosses
description: A remarkable collection of early Christian high crosses near Kilkieran in County Tipperary, featuring unusual west-country Romanesque carvings.
history: The Kilkieran High Crosses in County Tipperary are a group of early Christian crosses near the village of Kilkieran. The crosses feature distinctive carvings and represent an important school of early medieval stone carving in Munster.

name: Castlecomer Castle
description: Castle remains in the Kilkenny coal-mining town of Castlecomer, where the Wandesforde family developed Ireland's only significant coalfield.
history: Castlecomer Castle in County Kilkenny was the seat of the Wandesforde family who developed the Castlecomer coalfield, Ireland's only significant coal deposits. The mining heritage is now celebrated in the Castlecomer Discovery Park.

name: Fertagh Round Tower
description: A fine round tower near Johnstown in County Kilkenny, one of the best-preserved examples in the southeast.
history: The Fertagh Round Tower near Johnstown, County Kilkenny, is a well-preserved early Christian round tower marking a monastic site. The tower retains good height and structural integrity.

name: Cregg Castle
description: A fortified house near Galway city, one of the many tower houses and castles that ring the medieval city of the tribes.
history: Cregg Castle near Galway city is a fortified house associated with the Kirwan family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway.

name: Ballindoon Priory
description: Dominican priory ruins on the shores of Lough Arrow in County Sligo, in one of Ireland's most beautiful lake landscapes.
history: Ballindoon Priory on the shores of Lough Arrow, County Sligo, was a Dominican foundation. The atmospheric ruins overlook one of Ireland's most beautiful lakes, surrounded by the Curlew and Bricklieve mountains.

name: Temple House Sligo
description: See Templehouse Castle entry—a remarkable lakeside estate combining Knights Templar heritage with Georgian elegance.
history: Temple House near Ballymote, County Sligo, combines medieval Templar heritage with a Georgian mansion on the shores of Templehouse Lake.

name: Frenchpark Castle
description: Castle remains near the Roscommon village of Frenchpark, birthplace of Douglas Hyde, first President of Ireland.
history: Frenchpark in County Roscommon has castle remains and is the birthplace of Douglas Hyde (1860-1949), founder of the Gaelic League and first President of Ireland. The Douglas Hyde Interpretive Centre tells his story.

name: McDermott's Castle Island
description: A romantically ruined castle on a tiny island in Lough Key, associated with a tragic love legend and now part of one of Ireland's finest forest parks.
history: McDermott's Castle on an island in Lough Key, County Roscommon, was the seat of the MacDermot clan. The island is associated with a tragic love story of Una MacDermot, who drowned swimming to meet her lover on the island. The castle is visible from Lough Key Forest Park's treetop walkway.

name: Ballyseedy Castle Kerry
description: A castellated mansion near Tralee that witnessed one of the most controversial events of the Irish Civil War.
history: Ballyseedy Castle near Tralee, County Kerry, is infamous for the Ballyseedy Massacre of March 1923, when Free State forces tied anti-Treaty prisoners to a landmine. The castle itself is a 19th-century castellated mansion now operating as a hotel.

name: Tralee Castle
description: Scant remains of the great Desmond castle in Kerry's county town, where the international Rose of Tralee festival draws visitors each August.
history: Tralee Castle was a principal fortress of the Earls of Desmond. Little survives of the medieval castle in Kerry's county town. Tralee is best known for the annual Rose of Tralee festival and the Kerry County Museum.

name: Innisfallen Abbey
description: Enchanting monastic ruins on an island in the Lower Lake of Killarney, where the Annals of Innisfallen were compiled over centuries.
history: Innisfallen Island in the Lower Lake of Killarney, County Kerry, preserves the ruins of a monastery founded in the 7th century. The monks here compiled the Annals of Innisfallen, one of Ireland's most important historical manuscripts, covering events from the earliest times to 1326. The island is accessible by boat from Ross Castle.

name: Castle Lough Currane
description: A castle on an island in Lough Currane near Waterville on the Ring of Kerry, in one of Ireland's finest fishing lakes.
history: Castle Lough Currane stands on an island in Lough Currane near Waterville, County Kerry. The lake is one of Ireland's premier fishing lakes, famous for sea trout. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Ardfert Friary
description: Extensive medieval friary ruins near Tralee, featuring a fine cathedral, Franciscan friary, and Romanesque architectural details.
history: Ardfert near Tralee, County Kerry, preserves an impressive complex of medieval ecclesiastical buildings including a 13th-century cathedral, a Franciscan friary, and a Romanesque church. The cathedral features fine Gothic and Romanesque architectural details. Ardfert was the seat of the medieval bishops of Kerry.

name: Rockbarton Castle
description: A tower house near Limerick city, one of the medieval fortifications in the hinterland of Ireland's third-largest city.
history: Rockbarton Castle is a medieval tower house near Limerick city.

name: Caherconlish Castle
description: A medieval tower in the east Limerick village of Caherconlish.
history: Caherconlish Castle is a medieval tower house in County Limerick.

name: Shanagolden Abbey
description: See Shanagolden Friary—Franciscan ruins in west Limerick near the Shannon Estuary.
history: Shanagolden Abbey/Friary in County Limerick is a medieval religious house near the Shannon Estuary.

name: Dunsandle Castle
description: A ruined Big House in south Galway, once seat of the Daly family who were prominent in local politics.
history: Dunsandle Castle near Loughrea, County Galway, was the seat of the Daly family. The house was burned during the Civil War and stands as a ruin.

name: Deel Castle Mayo
description: A castle near the River Deel in County Mayo.
history: Deel Castle is a medieval fortification near the River Deel in County Mayo.

name: Lough Ree Castle
description: A castle on or near the shores of Lough Ree on the Shannon, one of Ireland's great midland lakes.
history: Lough Ree Castle is a medieval fortification near Lough Ree on the Shannon. The lake is the second-largest on the Shannon and an important boating destination.

name: Ballyloughloe Castle
description: A tower house in the Westmeath countryside near the River Shannon.
history: Ballyloughloe Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath.

name: Kilcormac Castle
description: Castle remains in the Offaly town of Kilcormac, in the heart of the Irish midlands.
history: Kilcormac Castle is a medieval fortification in County Offaly.

name: Banagher Castle
description: Castle remains in the charming Shannon-side town of Banagher, where Anthony Trollope began his career and Charlotte Bronte spent her honeymoon.
history: Banagher in County Offaly has castle remains on the Shannon. The town is notable as the place where Anthony Trollope began his writing career and where Charlotte Bronte spent part of her honeymoon in 1854. Banagher's fine stone bridge spans the Shannon.

name: Aghancon Castle
description: A tower house in the Offaly countryside, near the Slieve Bloom Mountains.
history: Aghancon Castle is a medieval tower house in County Offaly near the Slieve Bloom Mountains.

name: Old Leighlin Cathedral
description: A fine medieval cathedral in the quiet Carlow village of Old Leighlin, preserving Romanesque and Gothic architectural details.
history: Old Leighlin Cathedral in County Carlow is a medieval cathedral with origins in the 7th century. The current building dates mainly to the 12th-13th century and features Romanesque and Gothic details. The cathedral serves a tiny parish but contains rich architectural heritage.

name: Clonmore Castle Carlow
description: A medieval castle and round tower in the Carlow village of Clonmore, where the ancient Sli Cualann route crossed the border into Wicklow.
history: Clonmore in County Carlow preserves castle remains and a round tower on an ancient route into the Wicklow Mountains. The monastic site was founded by St Mogue in the 6th century.

name: Dunlavin Castle
description: Castle remains in the west Wicklow town of Dunlavin, a pretty market town with a fine planned square.
history: Dunlavin in County Wicklow has castle remains and a distinctive planned market square with a courthouse. The town sits in the western Wicklow foothills.

name: Rathgall Fort
description: One of the largest hillforts in Ireland, a massive multi-vallate enclosure in the Carlow-Wicklow borderlands with Bronze Age origins.
history: Rathgall Fort near Tullow, County Wicklow, is one of the largest hillforts in Ireland. The massive multi-vallate enclosure dates to the Late Bronze Age (c.1000 BC). Excavations revealed evidence of bronze-working, suggesting it was a major metalworking centre. The site covers over 7 hectares.

name: Newcastle McKynegan
description: A medieval castle in the Wicklow coastal town of Newcastle, guarding the route between Dublin and Wicklow along the Irish Sea coast.
history: Newcastle (McKynegan) in County Wicklow has medieval castle remains. The town sits on the coast road between Dublin and Wicklow, historically an important and often dangerous route through the territory of the O'Byrne and O'Toole clans.

name: Clondalkin Round Tower
description: One of Ireland's finest round towers, standing in the Dublin suburb of Clondalkin—a remarkable early Christian survivor amid modern urban development.
history: The Clondalkin Round Tower in south Dublin is one of the best-preserved round towers in Ireland, standing 28 metres tall with its original conical cap intact. The tower dates to the 7th-8th century and marks the site of an important early Christian monastery. Its survival amid the modern suburb of Clondalkin makes it a striking anachronism.

name: Bremore Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house near Balbriggan on the north Dublin coast, with views across the Irish Sea to the Mountains of Mourne.
history: Bremore Castle near Balbriggan, north County Dublin, is a restored 15th-century tower house near the coast. The castle overlooks Bremore Passage Tomb, a Neolithic monument on the headland. The area combines prehistoric and medieval heritage in a coastal setting north of Dublin.
